quotes.quote: We present the first results from very-high-energy observations of the dwarf spheroidal satellite candidate Triangulum II with the MAGIC telescopes from 62.4 hours of good-quality data taken between August 2016 and August 2017. We find no gamma-ray excess in the direction of Triangulum II, and upper limits on both the differential and integral gamma-ray flux are presented. Currently, the kinematics of Triangulum II are affected by large uncertainties leading to a bias in the determination of the properties of its dark matter halo. Using a scaling relation between the annihilation J factor and heliocentric distance of well-known dwarf spheroidal galaxies, we estimate an annihilation J factor for Triangulum II for WIMP dark matter of ( 0.5^ )/ GeV ^ 2 cm ^ 5 = 19.35 0.37 . We also derive a dark matter density profile for the object relying on results from resolved simulations of Milky Way sized dark matter halos.

Triangulum II is an ultra-faint MW satellite discovered in 2015 as part of the Pan-STARRS survey 2010SPIE.7733E..0EK . It is located at RA(J2000) 02h13m17.4s, Dec(J2000) +36 ^ 10'42.4'', with an absolute magnitude of M_ v = -1.8 0.5 at a heliocentric distance of d = 30 2 kpc 2015ApJ...802L..18L . A first spectroscopic study, based on six member stars, predicted Tri II to have a M/L of 3600^ +3500 _ 2100 \ M_ /L_ and one of the highest DM concentrations of any galaxy ever found 2015ApJ...814L...7K .

sources.summary: The Gaia study uses two Triangulum II member stars, reviews its spectroscopy and chemical-abundance follow-up, and places it on a close-pericenter, early-infall orbit.

quotes.quote: I compile the spectroscopically-confirmed member stars in each dwarf bright enough for Gaia astrometry from the literature, producing member samples ranging from 2 stars in Triangulum II to 68 stars in Bo o tes I.

After Tuc III, the next closest orbital pericenters are those of Segue 1 and Tri II, each at 20 kpc. Among the galaxies for which the binding energy is well-behaved, the rocha12 relation suggests infall times of 7  Gyr for Tri II, Segue 2, and Willman 1, 8  Gyr for Draco II and Tuc III, and >12  Gyr for Ret II.

2000, , 120, 1579 The first spectroscopy of Triangulum II (Tri II) was obtained by kirby15b with Keck/DEIMOS, which was quickly followed up with additional DEIMOS spectroscopy by martin16a . Given the discrepant results between the two studies, kirby17 reobserved the entire sample of member stars identified by kirby15b and martin16a with DEIMOS. The chemical abundances of the brightest two stars on the red giant branch were studied at high spectral resolution with Gemini-N/GRACES by venn17 , and a similar analysis of the brighter star was carried out by kirby17 with Keck/HIRES. Because of its very low luminosity, Tri II contains just two spectroscopically confirmed member stars brighter than g=20 , but the three DEIMOS studies produced a total of 32 confirmed non-members brighter than this limit. belokurov09 presented MMT/Hectochelle spectroscopy of Segue 2 in the discovery paper for the system, which kirby13 supplemented with a large Keck/DEIMOS data set.

gmw_00011641.name: SDSS J024121.80+000329.2
gmw_00011641.overview: SDSS J024121.80+000329.2 is an isolated, low-redshift dwarf galaxy with no obvious companion galaxies [arXiv:1105.4556]. It exhibits a low-excitation spectrum with a relatively high nitrogen-to-oxygen ratio and a somewhat depressed star formation rate [arXiv:1105.4556].

sources.summary: The paper treats SDSS J024121.80+000329.2 as an isolated low-redshift dwarf selected for an apparent 0.40 dex metallicity excess, then argues the new spectra favor ordinary oxygen abundance with high N/O and low excitation.

quotes.quote: For SDSS J022628.28+010937.7, SDSS J024121.80+000329.2, SDSS J082639.19+253553.5, and SDSS J082633.77+252959.2 respectively (hereafter abbreviated as SDSS- plus the first six digits of the Right Ascension), these oxygen abundances lie 0.37, 0.40, 0.55, and 0.55 dex above (and well outside the 0.1 dex scatter of) the luminosity-metallicity relationship histogram medians presented by P08. Like all of the objects in the P08 parent sample, these four galaxies are fairly isolated Note that J082639.19+253553.5 and J082633.77+252959.2 are members of the same group (Peeples, private communication, 2010). , low-redshift ( 0.0051<z<0.0227 ) dwarfs ( 17< M _ B <-15 ), with no obvious companions and somewhat depressed star formation rates (P08).

Our new observations extend the wavelength coverage to include the [ O 2 ] 3726,3729 doublet, which adds leverage in oxygen abundance estimates and allows measurements of N/O ratios. All four spectra are low excitation, with relatively high N/O ratios ( N/O 0.10 ), each of which tend to bias estimates based on strong emission lines toward high oxygen abundances. These spectra all fall in a regime where the ``standard'' strong line methods for metallicity determinations are not well calibrated either empirically or by photoionization modeling. By comparing our spectra directly to photoionization models, we estimate oxygen abundances in the range of 7.9 12 + (O/H) 8.4 , consistent with the scatter of the mass-metallicity relation.

gmw_00011642.name: SDSS J082639.19+253553.5
gmw_00011642.overview: SDSS J082639.19+253553.5 is a low-redshift dwarf galaxy [arXiv:1105.4556]. Although it is fairly isolated, it belongs to a galaxy group and exhibits a somewhat depressed star formation rate [arXiv:1105.4556].

sources.summary: The paper treats SDSS J082639.19+253553.5 as an isolated low-redshift dwarf selected for an apparent 0.55 dex metallicity excess, then argues the new spectra favor ordinary oxygen abundance with high N/O and low excitation.

gmw_00011643.name: SDSS J082633.77+252959.2
gmw_00011643.overview: SDSS J082633.77+252959.2 is a low-redshift dwarf galaxy [arXiv:1105.4556]. It is a member of a galaxy group, though it is fairly isolated with no obvious companions and exhibits a somewhat depressed star formation rate [arXiv:1105.4556].

sources.summary: The paper treats SDSS J082633.77+252959.2 as an isolated low-redshift dwarf selected for an apparent 0.55 dex metallicity excess, then argues the new spectra favor ordinary oxygen abundance with high N/O and low excitation.

quotes.quote: For SDSS J022628.28+010937.7, SDSS J024121.80+000329.2, SDSS J082639.19+253553.5, and SDSS J082633.77+252959.2 respectively (hereafter abbreviated as SDSS- plus the first six digits of the Right Ascension), these oxygen abundances lie 0.37, 0.40, 0.55, and 0.55 dex above (and well outside the 0.1 dex scatter of) the luminosity-metallicity relationship histogram medians presented by P08. gmw_00011644.name: A3128 B 0248
gmw_00011644.overview: A3128 B 0248 is a high-mass, red post-starburst cluster galaxy [arXiv:2003.02274]. It has a simple bulge-plus-disk structure and stopped forming stars at an early epoch, rendering it passive today [arXiv:2003.02274].

sources.summary: The paper describes A3128 B 0248 as a post-starburst cluster galaxy with early-epoch quenching, red color, high mass, and a simple bulge-plus-disk structure.

quotes.quote: Only A3128 B 0248 can be decomposed just in terms of a bulge and a disk, while all the other galaxies present a more complex structure that includes either a bar or a lens and are characterised by a broken disk (Type II).

To summarise the results so far, our sample includes two galaxies (A3128 B 0248, A3158 B 0223) that have EW( ) < 2.8 throughout the entire galaxy disks, indicating they have stopped forming stars at early epochs. These galaxies are also the two reddest galaxies in the sample (see Fig. fig:cf_P17 ) and are among the most massive galaxies.

Figure fig:PSB_SFH_4bins shows the variation of the SFR maps with cosmic time in four age bins. Given that no emission lines are detected, none of the galaxies have star formation in the youngest age bin (last 2 10^7 yr), consistent with the fact that they are all truly passive today. All the galaxies have stopped forming stars at least 20 Myr ago and were highly star forming in the oldest age bin.

sources.summary: For A3128 B 0148, the paper reports an elongated but tight and unique resolved SFR-mass sequence; its residual distribution is described as narrow compared with broader or tailed cases.

quotes.quote: Some galaxies show quite elongated sequences (e.g. A3128 B 0148, P57486), while in some other cases a cloud rather than a sequence is observed (e.g. JO45, JO89).
Only a few galaxies show a really tight and unique sequence (e.g. A3128 B 0148, JO102, A970 B 0338).

In addition to the median values, also the shapes of the distributions give important information: while in some cases they are quite narrow (e.g. A970 B 0338, A3128 B 0148), in many other cases they are broad (e.g. P13384), show asymmetric tails (e.g. JO45) and are even double peaked (e.g. P20769).

gmw_00011645.name: A3158 B 0223
gmw_00011645.overview: A3158 B 0223 is a massive, red post-starburst galaxy that is currently passive and devoid of active star formation [arXiv:2003.02274]. It stopped forming stars at an early epoch, showing strong evidence of outside-in quenching across its entire disk [arXiv:2003.02274].

sources.summary: The paper describes A3158 B 0234 as a blue, high-EW post-starburst galaxy with recent star-formation truncation, flat EW gradients, and no current star formation.

quotes.quote: The sample also includes three galaxies (A500 F 0152, A3158 B 0234 and A3376 B 0214) whose EW( ) measured on the integrated spectra is >> 2.8 , suggesting a recent truncation of their star formation ( <10^9 yr ago). Two of these galaxies have strong negative EW( ) gradients, suggesting the presence of a central burst before quenching. All these galaxies still show blue colors in Fig. fig:cf_P17 .
In contrast, A3158 B 0234 presents a flat trend.

Figure fig:PSB_SFH_4bins shows the variation of the SFR maps with cosmic time in four age bins. Given that no emission lines are detected, none of the galaxies have star formation in the youngest age bin (last 2 10^7 yr), consistent with the fact that they are all truly passive today. All the galaxies have stopped forming stars at least 20 Myr ago and were highly star forming in the oldest age bin.

sources.summary: The paper describes A3158 11 91 as a post-starburst galaxy with slightly above-threshold EW, flat radial EW behavior, regular rotation, and some bar/lens-related kinematic distortion.

quotes.quote: Some distortions are evident in A3158 11 91 and A3376 B 0214 but these are most likely due to the presence of bars and lenses. Stellar velocity dispersions are also generally quite low ( <50 , plots not shown).

We are now in the position of interpreting the integrated EW values shown in Table tab:EW : A3128 B 0248, and A3158 B 0223 have EW( ) measured <2.8 , while A1069 B 0103, A3158 11 91 and A500 22 184 have a value slightly above the threshold. The EW( ) is therefore consistent with a scenario where the star formation of the former galaxies ended more than 10^ 9 yr ago, that in the latter galaxies slightly earlier.
A1069 B 0103 and A3158 11 91 have EW( ) < 3 in the galaxy center, then values are slightly higher in the outskirts, but never exceed EW( ) 3.5 . Overall trends are rather flat.

sources.summary: The paper describes A3158 B 0223 as a red, massive post-starburst galaxy quenched early throughout its disk, with especially strong evidence for outside-in quenching.

quotes.quote: To summarise the results so far, our sample includes two galaxies (A3128 B 0248, A3158 B 0223) that have EW( ) < 2.8 throughout the entire galaxy disks, indicating they have stopped forming stars at early epochs. These galaxies are also the two reddest galaxies in the sample (see Fig. fig:cf_P17 ) and are among the most massive galaxies.

A3158 B 0223 is the galaxy with the strongest evidence of outside-in quenching, while the trends for A328 B 0248 and A1069 B 0103 have to be taken carefully because the former galaxy is very small and so there are not many spaxels in each galactocentric distance bin and the latter has a high inclination and annuli enclose portion of the galaxies that might be physically different.

Figure fig:PSB_SFH_4bins shows the variation of the SFR maps with cosmic time in four age bins. Given that no emission lines are detected, none of the galaxies have star formation in the youngest age bin (last 2 10^7 yr), consistent with the fact that they are all truly passive today. All the galaxies have stopped forming stars at least 20 Myr ago and were highly star forming in the oldest age bin.

gmw_00011646.name: A500 F 0152
gmw_00011646.overview: A500 F 0152 is a post-starburst galaxy that currently exhibits blue colors [arXiv:2003.02274]. It experienced a recent truncation of star formation less than one billion years ago, likely preceded by a central burst [arXiv:2003.02274].

sources.summary: The paper describes A500 22 184 as a post-starburst galaxy with slightly above-threshold EW, redder colors, and a steep negative EW gradient pointing to recent core quenching.

quotes.quote: A500 22 184 is instead characterised by a very steep negative gradient. The EW( ) is 3.8 in the galaxy center and then reaches values <2 in the outskirts. The galaxy could therefore have been passive for longer time in the outskirts and just recently become quickly passive in the core or could have had experienced a recent increase in the SF within the core.

A1069 B 0103 and A3158 B 0234 have flat EW( ) gradients and green colors, while A500 22 184 have a steep negative gradient (EW( ) 3.8 in the core) but redder colors. The last galaxy could therefore have been passive in the outskirts for a longer time, but have had an abrupt truncation of the star formation in the core recently.

Figure fig:PSB_SFH_4bins shows the variation of the SFR maps with cosmic time in four age bins. Given that no emission lines are detected, none of the galaxies have star formation in the youngest age bin (last 2 10^7 yr), consistent with the fact that they are all truly passive today. All the galaxies have stopped forming stars at least 20 Myr ago and were highly star forming in the oldest age bin.

sources.summary: The paper places A500 F 0152 among high-EW recently truncated post-starburst galaxies, still blue and showing evidence for a central burst before quenching.

quotes.quote: The sample also includes three galaxies (A500 F 0152, A3158 B 0234 and A3376 B 0214) whose EW( ) measured on the integrated spectra is >> 2.8 , suggesting a recent truncation of their star formation ( <10^9 yr ago). Two of these galaxies have strong negative EW( ) gradients, suggesting the presence of a central burst before quenching. All these galaxies still show blue colors in Fig. fig:cf_P17 .

The three galaxies with the highest EW( ) (A500 F 0152, A3158 B 0234 and A3376 B 0214) show only mild signs of star formation truncation in the galaxy outskirts in the second age bin (between 2.7 10^7 yr and 5.7 10^8 yr ago) compared to the third age bin (between 5.7 10^8 yr and 5.7 10^9 yr ago), indicating that the bulk of the quenching process occurred more recently than 2.7 10^7 yr ago.

Figure fig:PSB_SFH_4bins shows the variation of the SFR maps with cosmic time in four age bins. Given that no emission lines are detected, none of the galaxies have star formation in the youngest age bin (last 2 10^7 yr), consistent with the fact that they are all truly passive today. All the galaxies have stopped forming stars at least 20 Myr ago and were highly star forming in the oldest age bin.

gmw_00011647.name: A3376 B 0214
gmw_00011647.overview: A3376 B 0214 is a blue post-starburst galaxy that experienced a recent truncation of its star formation [arXiv:2003.02274]. The galaxy exhibits a strong negative equivalent width gradient, indicating a central star formation enhancement before quenching [arXiv:2003.02274].

sources.summary: The paper describes A3376 B 0214 as a blue, high-EW post-starburst galaxy with the strongest central EW and signs of central star-formation enhancement before quenching.

quotes.quote: A3376 B 0214 is the galaxy with the overall highest EW and also the highest EW in the center, with values ranging between 4.1 and 5.9 , followed by A500 F 152 that also shows a strong EW( ) gradient. In contrast, A3158 B 0234 presents a flat trend.

The sample also includes three galaxies (A500 F 0152, A3158 B 0234 and A3376 B 0214) whose EW( ) measured on the integrated spectra is >> 2.8 , suggesting a recent truncation of their star formation ( <10^9 yr ago). Two of these galaxies have strong negative EW( ) gradients, suggesting the presence of a central burst before quenching. All these galaxies still show blue colors in Fig. fig:cf_P17 .
A3376 B 0214 is the only galaxy showing a clear sign of a central SF enhancement between 2.7 10^7 yr and 5.7 10^8 yr ago.

gmw_00011648.name: A1069 B 0103
gmw_00011648.overview: A1069 B 0103 is a post-starburst galaxy with a green color and a flat equivalent width gradient [arXiv:2003.02274]. The galaxy is currently passive, with no active star formation detected within the last 20 million years [arXiv:2003.02274].

sources.summary: The paper describes A1069 B 0103 as a post-starburst galaxy with slightly above-threshold EW, flat EW gradients, green color, and no current star formation.

quotes.quote: We are now in the position of interpreting the integrated EW values shown in Table tab:EW : A3128 B 0248, and A3158 B 0223 have EW( ) measured <2.8 , while A1069 B 0103, A3158 11 91 and A500 22 184 have a value slightly above the threshold. The EW( ) is therefore consistent with a scenario where the star formation of the former galaxies ended more than 10^ 9 yr ago, that in the latter galaxies slightly earlier.
A1069 B 0103 and A3158 B 0234 have flat EW( ) gradients and green colors, while A500 22 184 have a steep negative gradient (EW( ) 3.8 in the core) but redder colors.

Figure fig:PSB_SFH_4bins shows the variation of the SFR maps with cosmic time in four age bins. Given that no emission lines are detected, none of the galaxies have star formation in the youngest age bin (last 2 10^7 yr), consistent with the fact that they are all truly passive today. All the galaxies have stopped forming stars at least 20 Myr ago and were highly star forming in the oldest age bin.

gmw_00011649.name: 2MFGC08638
gmw_00011649.overview: 2MFGC08638 is an extremely fast-rotating giant spiral galaxy [arXiv:2109.03828]. It features an exceptionally large stellar disc extending up to 200 kiloparsecs in diameter and a slowly rising rotation curve [arXiv:2109.03828].

sources.summary: The paper identifies 2MFGC08638 as one of two extremely fast-rotating giant spiral discs whose slowly rising rotation curves, large discs, and scaling-relation offsets make them unusual.

quotes.quote: Notable exceptions are our two fastest rotating discs, 2MFGC08638 and 2MFGC12344 (see first and third rows in fig:maps ). Although the inner rotation velocities of these galaxies could be very uncertain because of their high inclination angles, the rotation curves seem to rise slowly and reach a flat region only in the outermost regions of the stellar disc. This evidence might indicate that the dynamics of these galaxies is dominated by the dark matter halo even in the inner regions. These two galaxies also reach extremely large rotation velocities, with 2MFGC12344 arguably setting the record for the most rapidly rotating spiral galaxy currently known see also UGC12591, Giovanelli+1986 .

The two galaxies in our sample with the highest rotational speeds, 2MFGC08638 and 2MFGC12344, are the only significant outliers in the scaling relations. The extreme rotation velocities ( > 450 ) of these two galaxies place them on the high- \ side of the Tully-Fisher relations ( fig:TF ) and the high- \ side of the Fall relation ( fig:Fall ). They have also exceptionally large stellar discs that extend up to 200 kpc in diameter in z band.

gmw_00011650.name: 2MFGC12344
gmw_00011650.overview: 2MFGC12344 is an extremely fast-rotating giant spiral galaxy [arXiv:2109.03828] with an exceptionally large stellar disc that extends up to 200 kpc in diameter [arXiv:2109.03828]. Its rotation curve rises slowly [arXiv:2109.03828, arXiv:1909.09080], and dark matter dominates its dynamics beyond a radius of 10 kpc [arXiv:1909.09080].

sources.summary: The paper identifies 2MFGC12344 as one of two extremely fast-rotating giant spiral discs whose slowly rising rotation curves, large discs, and scaling-relation offsets make them unusual.

quotes.quote: Notable exceptions are our two fastest rotating discs, 2MFGC08638 and 2MFGC12344 (see first and third rows in fig:maps ). Although the inner rotation velocities of these galaxies could be very uncertain because of their high inclination angles, the rotation curves seem to rise slowly and reach a flat region only in the outermost regions of the stellar disc. This evidence might indicate that the dynamics of these galaxies is dominated by the dark matter halo even in the inner regions. These two galaxies also reach extremely large rotation velocities, with 2MFGC12344 arguably setting the record for the most rapidly rotating spiral galaxy currently known see also UGC12591, Giovanelli+1986 .

The two galaxies in our sample with the highest rotational speeds, 2MFGC08638 and 2MFGC12344, are the only significant outliers in the scaling relations. The extreme rotation velocities ( > 450 ) of these two galaxies place them on the high- \ side of the Tully-Fisher relations ( fig:TF ) and the high- \ side of the Fall relation ( fig:Fall ). They have also exceptionally large stellar discs that extend up to 200 kpc in diameter in z band.

sources.summary: The paper identifies 2MFGC 12344 as the fastest-rotating super spiral, with a rising outer rotation curve, dominant dark-matter contribution beyond 10 kpc, and a large BTFR mismatch.

quotes.quote: The rotation curves of the two largest, most massive galaxies (OGC 0139 and 2MFGC 12344) continue to rise at the outer edge. The contribution of dark matter to the rotation curve of the fastest rotator, 2MFGC 12344, exceeds the contribution from stars at radii >10 kpc and continues to rise out to 41 kpc. The fastest rotator in our sample (2MFGC 12344) would require M_ b / M_ = 12.5 and M_ gas / M_ = 12.4 , factors of 5 and 50 greater than our estimates, respectively, to match the low-mass BTFR.

gmw_00011651.name: ID 191
gmw_00011651.overview: ID 191 is a blue H-alpha emitting galaxy with a detected CO line [arXiv:1403.0040]. It is separated from the nearby galaxy ID 193 by approximately 30 to 32 kiloparsecs, and the pair may be involved in a gas-rich merger [arXiv:1403.0040].

sources.summary: The paper identifies ID 191 as a blue H-alpha emitter detected in CO near ID 193, with infrared luminosity, a 30 kpc separation, and possible involvement in a gas-rich merger.

quotes.quote: ID 191, ID 193, and ID213 are eventually detected in 6.9, 6.3, and 3.8 , respectively. The separation between ID 191 and ID 193 is about 4 arcsec corresponding to 32 kpc in the physical scale and the velocity offset is 300 km s ^ 1 (Figure fig;co_map ).

We estimate a 116 GHz flux density of 4.8 2.6 Jy (1.8 ) and 10.2 2.6 Jy (3.9 ) for ID 191 and ID 193, respectively (Figure 2). Although ID 191 is marginally detected, it is clear that the blended IR emission is dominated by ID 193.

The starburst in ID 191 and ID193 is hard to explain by the first encounter or the final coalescence of a merger between them because the separation of 30 kpc is too large to induce violent star formation 2009PASJ...61..481S .

gmw_00011652.name: ID 193
gmw_00011652.overview: ID 193 is a red H-alpha emitting galaxy with high infrared luminosity [arXiv:1403.0040]. It is a starbursting galaxy driven by a gas-rich merger, exhibiting a high star formation rate of 886 solar masses per year [arXiv:1403.0040].

sources.summary: The paper describes ID 193 as a red CO-emitting H-alpha emitter with high infrared luminosity, an 886 solar-mass-per-year star formation rate, and likely gas-rich merger-driven starburst activity.

quotes.quote: The two (ID 193 and ID 213) out of four red HAEs are actually detected in the CO emission line while only one (ID 191) out of 16 blue HAEs is detected.

Using the luminosity-independent conversion factor 2008ApJ...682..985W , we derive L_ IR =2.5 10^ 12 L_ and L_ IR =5.1 10^ 12 L_ for ID 191 and ID 193, respectively. Assuming that the bulk of the total IR luminosity is powered by star formation, we find with the standard calibration of that ID 193 has a high SFR of 886 M _ yr ^ 1 .

Moreover, ID 193 shows a violent star-formation activity (SFR=886 M_ yr ^ 1 ), high L_ IR /L'_ CO ratio (high SFE) and red optical color. We interpret it as a star-bursting galaxy driven by a gas-rich merger with ID 191 or another galaxy.

gmw_00011653.name: NGC 678
gmw_00011653.overview: NGC 678 is an edge-on spiral galaxy that is a companion to the nearby elliptical galaxy NGC 680 [arXiv:1105.5654]. The galaxy exhibits a dust lane along its disk, an optical warp, and signs of disturbance from a likely interaction [arXiv:1105.5654].

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 678 as an edge-on spiral companion near NGC 680, with similar velocity, dust-lane structure, an optical warp, and signs of disturbance.

quotes.quote: The deep MegaCam images did not reveal any obvious stellar bridge between the elliptical and NGC 678 to the North-West. However both galaxies have similar velocities (see Fig. fig:N680-field-vel ) and are most likely already interacting. NGC 678 is an edge-on spiral showing a prominent dust lane along its disk, best visible in the g-i color map image displayed in Fig. fig:N680-g-i .

To the West, the dust lane seems to bifurcate. To the North-East, a warp is visible on the optical bands. All these features plus the thickness of the disk indicate that the spiral is likely disturbed.

gmw_00011654.name: J0010+06
gmw_00011654.overview: J0010+06 is a high-redshift quasar [arXiv:2303.05531]. It is associated with an extended, luminous Lyman-alpha filament and features extended hydrogen-alpha emission tracing its circumgalactic medium [arXiv:2303.05531].

sources.summary: The paper selects quasar J0010+06 for MOSFIRE follow-up because it has the most extended luminous Ly filament, then detects extended H emission tracing its CGM.

quotes.quote: Such asymmetry is especially prominent for J0010+06 for which the region with the highest SB is elongated towards north-east (NE). J0010+06 contains the most extended and luminous Ly filament at a redshift that would place the associated H emission in the best part of the infrared spectrum in terms of absence of sky lines. The extended emission is not associated with any visible continuum object (see Figure app:WL in the Appendix app:WLimages ) ensuring that the detected emission is indeed circum-galactic or intergalactic. For these reasons, J0010+06 was selected as the target for a MOSFIRE follow-up search for extended H emission as described below.

The H- alpha Emission of the Nebula Figure fig:2DmapHa shows the MOSFIRE slit 2D spectrum on J0010+06 around the expected H wavelength position (converted to velocity) and after the subtraction of the quasar emission. The zero velocity corresponds to the redshift of the Ly peak of the nebula. The central regions, used for the rescaling and subtraction of quasar emission are masked and not used for our scientific analysis. H emission at high signal-to-noise level (above a SNR=3 per spectral and spatial resolution element) is detected near zero velocity and extending by 2.5", corresponding to about 20 kpc, from the quasar (see also Figure app:A1 for a clearer indication of the extent) along the NE side of the slit (denoted by positive y-axis values).

gmw_00011655.name: SDSS J142452.11+263715.1
gmw_00011655.overview: SDSS J142452.11+263715.1 is a broad emission line quasar located at a redshift of z=0.3761 [arXiv:1212.6171]. The source exhibits a soft X-ray excess and a high X-ray luminosity typical of radio-quiet quasars [arXiv:1212.6171].

sources.summary: The paper identifies SDSS J142452.11+263715.1 as a broad-line/type 1 AGN or quasar with soft X-ray excess and high X-ray luminosity.

quotes.quote: Based on optical identification and analysis of the SDSS optical spectrum, we find that the star-like object SDSS J142452.11+263715 is in fact a broad emission line quasar with z=0.3761 (Pandge et al. 2013 in preparation). The source SDSS J142452.11+263715.1 falls in the CCD gap in the EPIC-pn data of Obs.I, hence the EPIC-pn spectrum was not useful. The rest of the five spectra from Obs.I and II were grouped to get minimum counts of 20 per energy bin. As before, we performed joint spectral analysis of available spectral data from different instruments for each observation. We found that the spectra of SDSS J142452.11+263715.1 for each observation are well constrained by a black body plus power-law model modified by the Galactic absorption ( N_H^ Gal =1.6 10^ 20 cm^ 2 ) and the best-fit parameters are listed in Table xray_spec_par_point_sources .

The X-ray spectrum of SDSS J142452.11+263715.1 consisting of soft X-ray excess, described as a black body with kT 150 and a powerlaw of 2 , is typical of the spectra of radio-quiet quasars e.g., PG quasars. The total X-ray luminosity of SDSS J142452.11+263715.1 in the 0.3-10 band is 6.6 0.3 10^ 43 erg s^ 1 and 6.8 0.25 10^ 43 erg s^ 1 for Obs.I and II, respectively.

gmw_00011656.name: SDSS J142443.78+263616.2
gmw_00011656.overview: SDSS J142443.78+263616.2 is an extended object that is likely a galaxy harboring an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1212.6171]. The source exhibits soft X-ray emission, though its redshift remains uncertain [arXiv:1212.6171].

sources.summary: The paper treats SDSS J142443.78+263616.2 as an extended likely galaxy with an AGN, soft X-ray emission, fitted spectra, and uncertain redshift.

quotes.quote: The nature of the other source, SDSS J142443.78+263616.2, is not clear as its optical spectrum is not available. This object is appears as an extended object in the SDSS image and is likely a galaxy harboring an AGN. To further examine the nature of these sources, we performed an X-ray spectral analysis of each source.

The second object SDSS J142443.78+263616.2 has no redshift information. This source shows soft X-ray emission. Initially we used a model wabs wabs( apec+PL ) , where thermal plasma component apec is for the soft excess and powerlaw for the hard continuum. We varied the red shift parameter in apec . The fit resulted in ^2/dof = 132/88 and the best-fit redshift z=1.50 0.05 , which may not be correct.

gmw_00011657.name: CXOU J191100.49-595621.5
gmw_00011657.overview: CXOU J191100.49-595621.5 is a radio galaxy that serves as the brightest cluster galaxy of the CXO1911 cluster [arXiv:2108.10036]. It hosts an active galactic nucleus with a compact radio core and is surrounded by X-ray cavities [arXiv:2108.10036].

sources.summary: The paper identifies CXOU J191100.49-595621.5 as the central AGN of CXO1911, hosted by the cluster BCG, with compact radio emission and nearby X-ray cavities.

quotes.quote: We detected an X-ray point source (CXOU J191100.49-595621.5, =19^ h 11^ m 00^ s .5 and =-59 56 21.5 ) at the geometrical diffuse X-ray emission center of CXO1911, which is overlapped with a radio galaxy (see next section) and thus is identified to be the central AGN of CXO1911. Located in the X-ray emission center of CXO1911, the counterpart galaxy of CXOU J191100.49-595621.5 is also found to have the smallest visual magnitude m_ F606W among all detected galaxies. Therefore, we identified this galaxy as the brightest cluster galaxy (BCG) of CXO1911.

The southeastern part of J191108-595556 is resolved to be a point source, which is coincident with CXOU J191100.49-595621.5 (with superposition accuracy better than 0.5 ) and suggesting a common AGN origin of the X-ray and radio emission from the optical BCG. The spectrum of CXOU J191100.49-595621.5 is found to be flat at 843 MHz, 5.5 GHz, and 9.0 GHz, suggesting that this source is young and the detected radio emission is dominated by the compact core of the radio galaxy. Although we have detected significant X-ray cavities around CXOU J191100.49-595621.5, the corresponding radio lobes are absent from the ATCA 5.5 GHz and 9.0 GHz images.

gmw_00011658.name: CXOU J191105.91-595558.8
gmw_00011658.overview: CXOU J191105.91-595558.8 is an elliptical galaxy that hosts an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2108.10036]. It is classified as a narrow-angle-tail radio galaxy featuring a pair of symmetric, elongated radio tails [arXiv:2108.10036].

sources.summary: The paper identifies CXOU J191105.91-595558.8 as an AGN-host elliptical and narrow-angle-tail radio galaxy with symmetric elongated radio tails in CXO1911.

quotes.quote: Two point sources, including CXOU J191100.49-595621.5 and CXOU J191105.91-595558.8, are found to have a counterpart elliptical galaxy, suggesting that the X-ray sources are AGN of the host galaxies. The radio head is associated with CXOU J191105.91-595558.8 and the optical galaxy, with superposition accuracies better than 1 . These features suggest that CXOU J191105.91-595558.8 is a narrow-angle-tail (NAT) radio galaxy, and the elongated radio tails can be explained as trails left as the radio active galaxy plough through the ICM of the galaxy cluster.

CXOU J191105.91-595558.8 is further identified to be a narrow-angle-tail radio galaxy, with a pair of symmetric and elongated radio tails in the high-resolution ATCA images. While for CXOU J191105.91-595558.8 and CXOU J191015.43-595624.9 (J191015-595615), their fluxes are found to decrease greatly at 9.0 GHz, indicating an older age for the plasma in radio jets.

gmw_00011659.name: CXOU J191015.43-595624.9
gmw_00011659.overview: CXOU J191015.43-595624.9 is an active galactic nucleus candidate associated with a galaxy cluster [arXiv:2108.10036]. The source is linked to an extended radio morphology and exhibits a large flux decrease at 9.0 GHz, which suggests the presence of older plasma in its radio jets [arXiv:2108.10036].

sources.summary: The paper reports CXOU J191015.43-595624.9 as a CXO1910 AGN candidate associated with radio morphology and older radio-jet plasma.

quotes.quote: For CXO1910, the morphology of J191015-595615 is found to split into two extended parts in the 5.5 GHz and 9.0 GHz images. A Chandra point source, CXOU J191015.43-595624.9, is found to be roughly coincident with the eastern part of the radio morphology, indicating that this source is an AGN candidate of this galaxy cluster. The multiband counterparts of the AGN candidates in CXO1911 and CXO1910 are summarized in Table- tab:counterparts . We calculated their fluxes (or magnitudes) and corresponding coordinates in X-ray, optical, and radio bands separately.

J191108-595556 and J191015-595615 are found to have a spectral index of =-1.14 and =-1.13 at 72-231 MHz hurley2017 , which are roughly in agreement with the flux values measured at 843 MHz. While for CXOU J191105.91-595558.8 and CXOU J191015.43-595624.9 (J191015-595615), their fluxes are found to decrease greatly at 9.0 GHz, indicating an older age for the plasma in radio jets. This picture is also consistent with the extended radio morphology observed in these two sources.

gmw_00011660.name: CSWA-141
gmw_00011660.overview: CSWA-141 is a bright, gravitationally lensed galaxy at a redshift of z=1.42 [arXiv:2301.11264]. It is characterized by a high specific star formation rate, extreme optical emission lines, and dense gas that may facilitate the escape of ionizing radiation [arXiv:2301.11264].

sources.summary: The paper highlights CSWA-141 as a bright z=1.42 lensed reionization-era analog with strong CIII], extreme optical emission, dense gas, and likely optically thin neutral gas.

quotes.quote: Included in our sample is the z=1.42 galaxy CSWA-141, one of the brightest known reionization-era analogs at high redshift (g=20.5), with a large sSFR (31.2 Gyr ^ 1 ) and an [ OIII ] +H equivalent width (EW _ [ OIII ] +H =730 ) that is nearly identical to the average value expected at z 7-8 . Whereas most of the sources in our sample show weak UV line emission, we find elevated CIII ] in the spectrum of CSWA-141 (EW _ CIII ] =4.6 1.9 ) together with detections of other prominent emission lines (OIII ] , Si III ] , Fe II ^ , Mg II). The brightness of CSWA-141 enables detailed investigation of the extreme emission line galaxies which become common at z>6 .

We find that gas traced by the CIII ] doublet likely probes higher densities than that traced by [ OII ] and [ SII ] . Characterisation of the spectrally resolved Mg II emission line and several low ionization absorption lines suggests neutral gas around the young stars is likely optically thin, potentially facilitating the escape of ionizing radiation. One source in the ESI sample, CSWA-141, was also observed with the Multi Object Double Spectrograph (MODS, Pogge2010 ) on the LBT.

gmw_00011661.name: SDSS J2220+0025
gmw_00011661.overview: SDSS J2220+0025 is a high-redshift radio-loud quasar with resolved radio components spanning a linear extent of approximately 70 kiloparsecs [arXiv:1810.06572]. Its extended radio component is brighter than its core, which appears unresolved under high-resolution VLBI observations [arXiv:1810.06572].

sources.summary: The paper identifies SDSS J2220+0025 as a high-redshift radio-loud quasar with resolved FIRST radio components over about 70 kpc, an extended component brighter than the core, and an unresolved VLBI core.

quotes.quote: The remaining two (SDSS J0813+3508 and SDSS J2220+0025) are resolved into multiple components Becker1995, Hodge2011 and have a linear extent of 10'' ( 70 kpc). The radio flux of the extended component of SDSS J0813+3508 is about half that of the core, while the extended radio component is brighter than the core for SDSS J2220+0025.

Specifically, SDSS J 0813+3508 and SDSS J 1242+5422 were observed by at 1.6 GHz and 5 GHz, and PMN J 2134-0419 and SDSS J 2220+0025 were observed by at 1.7 GHz and 5 GHz. These observations can resolve structures on the scale of 1.2--25 milliarcseconds ( 8--160 pc). At such small scales, these quasars are often mildly resolved and show a compact core with a one-sided jet (SDSS J 0813+3508 and PMN J 2134-0419 ) or an unresolved core (SDSS J 1242+5422 and SDSS J 2220+0025 ).

gmw_00011662.name: Phoenix dwarf galaxy
gmw_00011662.overview: The Phoenix dwarf galaxy is a Local Group member located approximately 420 kpc away [arXiv:1202.4728]. It exhibits characteristics of both dwarf spheroidal and dwarf irregular galaxies, containing associated neutral hydrogen gas and showing signs of recent star formation [arXiv:1202.4728].

sources.summary: The paper describes Phoenix as a Local Group dwarf with mixed irregular and spheroidal traits, associated H I gas, and no detected persistent HMXB population in the XMM-Newton data.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: The galaxy centre was put at RA=01 h 51 m 8.2 s 6.1 , Dec=-44 ^ 26 55.9 7.6 .

quotes.quote: The Phoenix dwarf galaxy was discovered in 1976 by Schust76 who described it as a very distant ( 100 kpc) globular cluster. It was later identified as a dwarf irregular galaxy (dIrr) by Cant77 . These galaxies are H i rich and show obvious signs of recent star formation. Despite this classification, Phoenix has characteristics of a dwarf spheroid galaxy (dSph) and it has been suggested that it belongs to an intermediate class of galaxy between the dwarf spheroidal and dwarf irregular, along with three other dwarf galaxies (Pegasus, Pisces (LGS3) and Antila). The distance to Phoenix has been determined to be 420 kpc using a variety of different methods and data sets (for example see Rydt91,Mart99 and, more recently, Menz08 ) placing Phoenix well within the Local Group.

We find no evidence for any HMXBs in Phoenix at this time. This rules out the existence of the X-ray persistent supergiant X-ray binary systems. However the transient nature of the Be/X-ray binaries means we cannot rule out a population of these sources but can conclude that it is not extensive.

gmw_00011663.name: J1457 + 2218
gmw_00011663.overview: J1457 + 2218 is a weak emission-line quasar candidate that is classified as radio-intermediate [arXiv:2008.02277]. It is a strong candidate for an intrinsically X-ray weak quasar that does not suffer from strong X-ray absorption [arXiv:2008.02277].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J1457 + 2218 as an intrinsically X-ray weak WLQ candidate, fits its Chandra spectrum, and finds little strong absorption with possible jet-linked emission.

quotes.quote: One of the WLQs, J1457 + 2218, is a good candidate for an intrinsically X-ray weak quasar. It has 70 broad-band counts in the source-extraction region: 47 in the soft band and 23 in the hard band, sufficient for basic spectral fitting. Using the CIAO specextract script, we extracted the broad-band spectrum of J1457 + 2218 from a circular region with a radius of 3.3 pixels centered at the X-ray position, corresponding to a 90 encircled-energy fraction.

It appears that J1457 + 2218 does not suffer from strong X-ray absorption. J1457 + 2218 has _ OX =-0.30 , which corresponds to an X-ray weakness factor of f_ weak =6.1 , and it remains X-ray weak by a factor of 4.7 after being corrected for the intrinsic absorption. J1457 + 2218 is also RI with R=42 and may have some jet-linked X-ray emission, which would indicate even weaker intrinsic coronal X-ray emission. The spectral fitting results suggest that J1457 + 2218 is probably an intrinsically X-ray weak WLQ like PHL 1811.

gmw_00011664.name: BCG in Abell 68
gmw_00011664.overview: The brightest cluster galaxy in Abell 68 is used as a representative model to study the sensitivity of stellar kinematics measurements [arXiv:2006.05706]. It also serves as a test case for analyzing the degeneracies between stellar, central black hole, and dark matter halo mass components [arXiv:2006.05706].

sources.summary: The paper uses the BCG in Abell 68 to test black-hole sensitivity, mass-anisotropy degeneracy, and the effect of including a fixed dark-matter component in JAM modeling.

quotes.quote: We check the sensitivity of our h_ 4 measurements to signal-to-noise ratio ( S/N ), and we illustrate it in Figure SNbias_h4 using the BCG in Abell 68 as an example, as the spectrum is representative of the typical BCG data.
For the BCG in Abell 68, which we use as an example, a black hole 10 times more massive than the black hole mass we used will give a change of 0.02 in _ z and 0.03 in _ DYN .

We show values for the parameters _ z and _ DYN , with the confidence levels indicated, for the fit with just the stellar and central mass components (red), and the stellar, central and dark matter components (blue, with a goodness-of-fit of ^ 2 /DOF =2.99) for Abell 68 in Figure A68SCD as an example, to illustrate the influence of the degeneracy between the two parameters. The figure also illustrate the change of the best-fitting parameters, for Abell 68, when the fixed dark matter component is included.

gmw_00011665.name: BCG in Abell 754
gmw_00011665.overview: The brightest cluster galaxy in Abell 754 is an early-type galaxy [arXiv:2011.05350] with an old stellar population [arXiv:2006.05706, arXiv:2011.05350] and no young stars [arXiv:2006.05706]. It features a low central velocity dispersion [arXiv:2006.05706, arXiv:2011.05350] and one of the lowest central dynamical mass estimates for its class [arXiv:2006.05706, arXiv:2011.05350].

sources.summary: The paper finds the Abell 754 BCG has old stellar populations but low mass-to-light ratio, with independent kinematic agreement and one of the lowest central dynamical masses.

quotes.quote: The two exceptions, that have no young stars and still have a lower stellar mass-to-light ratio, are Abell 754 ( _ DYN = 2.56 ), and Abell 1763 ( _ DYN = 1.89 ) for which we find a (relatively) older SSP-equivalent stellar population age of 7.5 Gyr. For the BCG in Abell 754 (PGC025714), our measurements of the central velocity dispersion, kinematic profile, and stellar populations agree very well with those made by Brough2007 , Spolaor2010 , and Groenewald2014 (from independent data and analysis). Our surface brightness profile derived from MGE agrees with that derived by Bildfell2008 and Bildfell_thesis to within 0.2 mag. Abell 754 has the fourth lowest dynamical mass estimate for the central 15 kpc (see Table DynModsTable ), and a corresponding low central velocity dispersion (295 14 km s ^ 1 ) compared to the average central velocity dispersion of the BCGs modelled here ( _ 0 = 324 3 \ km \ s^ 1 ).

sources.summary: The paper identifies Abell 754 among four low mass-to-light BCGs, noting a low dynamical mass estimate and central velocity dispersion reconciled by averaged models.

quotes.quote: Discussion on constraining the IMF From Figure ML_ML , we can see that, for a Salpeter IMF, the majority (ten) of the BCGs fall above the 1-to-1 line, but there is a subset of four BCGs below the 1-to-1 line (Abell 754, 1689, 1763 and 1942). The BCGs above the line (with _ DYN > _ POP ) are better described by the bottom-heavy Salpeter, or an even `heavier', IMF due to a higher fraction of low mass stars. For relatively old stellar populations (as expected for passively-evolving, massive early-type galaxies), only stars with masses below 1M _ are present (see MartinNavarro2015b, Lyubenova2016 ). This limits stellar populations based IMF analysis of massive early-type galaxies to its low-mass end. As seen from Figure ML_ML , a Kroupa IMF (which has fewer stars with masses below 0.5M _ than a Salpeter IMF) describes the four BCGs below the 1-to-1 line better, and is closer to reconciling the stellar mass-to-light ratios measured from stellar population and kinematic properties.

For Abell 754 ( _ DYN = 2.56 ), with one of the lowest dynamical mass estimates, and a corresponding low central velocity dispersion (295 14 km s ^ 1 ), Figure ML_ML_AVERAGE also shows that the average of two stellar population methods/models reconciles the dynamical and stellar population estimates.

gmw_00011666.name: BCG in Abell 1763
gmw_00011666.overview: The brightest cluster galaxy in Abell 1763 is a wide-angle tail radio galaxy that exhibits a highly peaked surface-brightness profile [arXiv:2006.05706]. It features a high central velocity dispersion, high stellar mass, and one of the lowest central dark matter mass contributions among similar galaxies [arXiv:2006.05706, arXiv:2011.05350].

sources.summary: The paper describes the Abell 1763 BCG as a wide-angle tail radio galaxy with a peaked surface-brightness profile, high central velocity dispersion, and low central dark-matter contribution.

quotes.quote: For the BCG in Abell 1763 (Leda2174167), a wide-angle tail radio galaxy, we find a very peaked surface brightness profile in our MGE analysis (see Appendix masking ), similar to what we typically find for the BCGs with young stellar components (e.g. see also Abell 383 and MS1455+22), but contrary to the surface brightness profile measured by found by Bildfell2008 and Bildfell_thesis .

Abell 1763 does not have a low dynamical mass in the centre (see Table DynModsTable , and a high central velocity dispersion of 362 2 km s ^ 1 ), but it has one of the lowest contributions of dark matter mass in the centre (3.4 per cent) on account of its high stellar mass and brightness (M _ K = -27.33 mag) compared to the average in our sample (M _ K = -26.52 mag). As we show and discuss in Section spherical , changing our cylindrically-aligned JAM models to spherically-aligned JAM models, results in the best-fitting _ DYN parameters changing by up to 15 per cent (higher for BCGs with rising velocity dispersion profiles, and lower for BCGs with decreasing velocity dispersion profiles). This can possibly account for the low _ DYN measured for Abell 1763, but will cause the _ DYN for Abell 754 to be even lower.

sources.summary: The paper places the BCG in Abell 1763 among low mass-to-light outliers, with low central dark-matter contribution, high stellar mass, and peculiar brightness.

quotes.quote: Discussion on constraining the IMF From Figure ML_ML , we can see that, for a Salpeter IMF, the majority (ten) of the BCGs fall above the 1-to-1 line, but there is a subset of four BCGs below the 1-to-1 line (Abell 754, 1689, 1763 and 1942). The BCGs above the line (with _ DYN > _ POP ) are better described by the bottom-heavy Salpeter, or an even `heavier', IMF due to a higher fraction of low mass stars. For relatively old stellar populations (as expected for passively-evolving, massive early-type galaxies), only stars with masses below 1M _ are present (see MartinNavarro2015b, Lyubenova2016 ). This limits stellar populations based IMF analysis of massive early-type galaxies to its low-mass end. As seen from Figure ML_ML , a Kroupa IMF (which has fewer stars with masses below 0.5M _ than a Salpeter IMF) describes the four BCGs below the 1-to-1 line better, and is closer to reconciling the stellar mass-to-light ratios measured from stellar population and kinematic properties.

For the BCG in Abell 1763 ( _ DYN = 1.89 ), we also find that it has one of the lowest contributions of dark matter mass to total mass in the centre (3.4 per cent) on account of its brightness (M _ K = --27.33 mag) and high stellar mass. Therefore, the BCGs of Abell 1942 and 1763 are peculiar in that they are very bright (i.e. high stellar mass, and low _ DYN ) compared to other BCGs of similar velocity dispersion.

gmw_00011667.overview: The active galactic nucleus 2050+364 is a radio source historically classified as a compact double [arXiv:astro-ph/0510440]. However, multi-frequency imaging suggests it may actually be a one-sided core-jet system containing a radio core, shocks, and a northern extended emission region [arXiv:astro-ph/0510440].

sources.summary: The paper presents 2050+364 as a compact double radio galaxy, resolving its absorption structure and interpreting the source as a one-sided core-jet system with shocks and extended northern emission.

quotes.quote: European VLBI Network spectral imaging of the ``compact double'' radio source 2050+364 in the UHF band at 1049 MHz has resolved the \ absorbing region, and has shown a faint continuum component to the North (N), in addition to the well-known East-West double (E, W). Re-examination of VLBI continuum images at multiple frequencies suggests that 2050+364 may well be a one-sided core-jet source, which appears as a double over a limited frequency range. One of the dominant features, W, would then be the innermost visible portion of the jet, and could be at or adjacent to the canonical radio core. The other, E, is probably related to shocks at a sudden bend of the jet, towards extended steep-spectrum region N. A remarkably deep and narrow \ absorption line component extends over the entire projected extent of 2050+364 .

Galaxies: active -- Galaxies: ISM -- Galaxies: individual: 2050+364 - Galaxies: jets -- Radio lines: galaxies 2050+364 ( J2052+3635 , DA 529 ; J2000 coordinates 20^ h 52^ m 52.0549^ s +36^ 35'35.300'' , Beasley et al. ), is one the original members of the group of radio sources described as ``Compact Doubles'' (CDs) by Phillips \ Mutel ( , ), and then studied in more detail by Mutel, Hodges, \ Phillips ( ).

gmw_00011668.name: UGC 12182
gmw_00011668.overview: UGC 12182 is a spiral galaxy [arXiv:1603.07084] with a heliocentric recessional velocity of 1490 km/s [arXiv:1603.07084]. It has an adopted distance of 24.4 Mpc [arXiv:1603.07084] and served as the host galaxy for the supernova SN 2014ck [arXiv:1603.07084].

sources.summary: The paper identifies UGC 12182 as the spiral host of SN 2014ck, giving the supernova offset from the galaxy center plus the galaxy recessional velocity and adopted distance modulus.

quotes.quote: The SN is located 4 3 E and 0 5 S from the centre of the spiral galaxy UGC 12182 (Figure 1). An heliocentric recessional velocity of 1490 km s ^ 1 for UGC 12182 is listed in the NASA/IPAC Extragalactic Database (NED), as taken from ``The Updated Zwicky Catalogue'' falco:1999 . The distance and distance modulus (adopting H_0 = 73 5 km s ^ 1 Mpc ^ 1 ), corrected for the Virgo, Great Attractor and Shapley infall, are 24.4 1.7 Mpc and = 31.94 0.15 mag, respectively mould:2000 .

gmw_00011669.name: ID720309
gmw_00011669.overview: ID720309 is a high-redshift galaxy candidate with an estimated redshift of approximately 9.46 to 9.47 [arXiv:2207.11740]. Photometric analysis suggests the source in the COSMOS2020 catalog may be a superposition of a real source and a cross-talk artifact from a nearby bright star [arXiv:2207.11740].

sources.summary: The paper identifies ID720309 as a z about 10 candidate, reassesses possible cross-talk contamination, recomputes its photometry, and retains it as a high-redshift candidate with a low-redshift peak.

quotes.quote: The source ID720309 has the highest among the candidates extracted from the COSMOS2020 catalogue. However, we identified a bright star at the coordinates R.A.=09h59m10.81s, Dec.=+2d11m04.29s which could potentially generate a cross-talk signal (see Appendix sec:appendix_cross-talks ) at a position 09h59m10.81s +2d06m42.40s (assuming a native pixel scale of 0.341 expected for the detector 7 of the VIRCAM camera). This source presents two components in one paw (paw 1), and just one component in the other (paw 2). It suggests that ID720309 in COSMOS2020 is the superposition of a cross-talk and a real source separated by 0.7 .

For the source ID720309, all indicate a z>9 solution with the original COSMOS2020 photometry. However, our photometry could be contaminated by a cross-talk artefact (see Sect. sec:ID720309 ). We recompute the with using the new stack not contaminated by the cross-talk. The redshifts which minimise the ^2 are 9.46_ 6.59 ^ +0.05 and 9.47_ 0.18 ^ +1.53 for the COSMOS and BC03 templates, respectively.

gmw_00011670.name: ID441697
gmw_00011670.overview: ID441697 is a high-redshift galaxy candidate with a photometric redshift of approximately 9.51 [arXiv:2207.11740]. It is a highly magnified object, experiencing a cumulative gravitational lensing magnification of 2.36 from five foreground lenses [arXiv:2207.11740].

sources.summary: The paper treats ID441697 as a robust z greater than 9.5 candidate and also finds it is the sample's most magnified object after lensing correction.

quotes.quote: The candidate ID441697 is robustly detected in the J band, its photometric redshift with is z_ phot =9.51_ 0.15 ^ +0.12 . In this case, the photometric redshift z_ phot =9.09_ 0.68 ^ +0.35 suggests a lower redshift, nonetheless all the weight is at z>8 in both catalogues. The most magnified candidate is ID441697, with a cumulative magnification of =2.36 0.80 from five lenses within 14 , representing a boost of 0.9 mag. The main contribution comes from a z=0.50_ 0.01 ^ +0.01 galaxy located within 4.6 and with a _v=231 km s ^ 1 velocity dispersion, leading to a =1.37 0.20 magnification.

gmw_00011671.name: ID978062
gmw_00011671.overview: ID978062 is a bright high-redshift galaxy candidate with an absolute ultraviolet magnitude of -22.56 [arXiv:2207.11740]. It has a photometric redshift of 8.86, though spectroscopic observations from the REBELS ALMA survey place its redshift at 7.675 [arXiv:2207.11740].

sources.summary: The paper describes ID978062 as a particularly bright recovered candidate with a broad redshift probability distribution, brighter UV magnitude, and REBELS spectroscopic redshift.

quotes.quote: Candidate ID978062 is particularly bright, with a H band magnitude of 24.5 . Its relatively broad , with a full-width at half maximum (FWHM) larger than 1 , results from the Lyman break located between the Y and the J band. The resulting photometric redshift is higher ( z=8.86 instead of 8.19 ) than in bowler_lack_2020 , and the absolute magnitude brighter ( M_ UV =-22.56 instead of 22.36 ). This source has a spectroscopic redshift at 7.675 from the REBELS ALMA survey Bouwens21_REBELS .

gmw_00011672.name: Y8
gmw_00011672.overview: Y8 is a faint high-redshift galaxy candidate with a magnitude of H > 25.6 [arXiv:2207.11740]. The galaxy is gravitationally lensed by a foreground system, resulting in an estimated magnification of 1.21 [arXiv:2207.11740].

sources.summary: The paper discusses Y8 as a faint candidate affected by lensing and uncertain photometry, with most galaxy-template fits still placing it at high redshift.

quotes.quote: The candidate Y8 (ID1209618) has a z=1.26_ 0.02 ^ +0.02 galaxy at a 4.4 distance with a velocity dispersion of _v=215 km s ^ 1 , which gives a =1.21 0.11 magnification. This candidate was already identified as magnified by stefanon_brightest_2019 , who found a 40 km s ^ 1 higher velocity dispersion and a 0.3 smaller angular separation We find an astrometric shift of 0 34 in declination compared to stefanon_brightest_2019 for the candidate Y8 (ID1209618). This is more than the shift of about 0 15 induced by the change of astrometric calibration from UltraVISTA DR3 to DR4. We note that this candidate remains faint with H>25.6 , which may have led to uncertain coordinates in stefanon_brightest_2019 who used DR3. , resulting in a higher magnification =1.39 .

The candidate Y8 (ID1209618) has problematic IRACLEAN photometry with an extremely low [ 3.6 ] flux, which is not the case in catalogue. In addition, the IRACLEAN photometric uncertainties in both [ 3.6 ] and [ 4.5 ] are much smaller than in the near-infrared bands for the catalogue, so that the main constraint on the SED is an unexpectedly red IRAC colour. All but one best-fit galaxy templates produce a redshift at z>8 , except with the catalogue.

gmw_00011673.name: PSQ J0210-0903
gmw_00011673.overview: PSQ J0210-0903 is a nearby post-starburst quasar located approximately 170 Mpc away [arXiv:1210.1208]. The quasar is hosted by an Sa-type barred spiral galaxy that exhibits both a rotating gas component and a nuclear outflow [arXiv:1210.1208].

sources.summary: The paper maps PSQ J0210-0903 as a nearby post-starburst quasar in an Sa barred spiral, finding central AGN emission, young surrounding stars, rotation, and nuclear outflow.

quotes.quote: In this paper we present the results obtained from observations of the PSQ SDSS J021011.5-090335.5 (hereafter PSQ J0210-0903), selected for its proximity, at a distance of only 170 Mpc (from NED The NASA/IPAC Extragalactic Database (NED) is operated by the Jet Propulsion Laboratory, California Institute of Technology, under contract with the National Aeronautics and Space Administration. , for H_ o = 73.0 5 km sec ^ 1 Mpc ^ 1 ), allowing the study of the spatial distribution of its stellar population and gas emission characteristics. The PSQ J0210-0903 is also one of the brightest PSQs with z< 0.1, and its spectrum clearly reveals the presence of the Balmer jump and high-order absorption lines of the Balmer series. The PSQ J0210-0903 is hosted by a barred spiral galaxy with Hubble type Sa graham09 and its SDSS Petrosian absolute magnitude is M_ i = -22.32 0.50 .

1.0

Gas Kinematics The gaseous velocity fields of \ and H presented in Fig. fig:cinematica both show similar rotation patterns, but with the rotation displaced from the continuum peak position by circa 1 0 to the South. These blueshifts support the presence of a nuclear outflow, in agreement also with the high velocity dispersions observed in this region (right panels of Fig. fig:cinematica ). Considering both the centroid velocities and the channel maps, the kinematic of the emitting gas can be understood as the combination of gas in rotation in the plane of the galaxy combined with an outflow component.

gmw_00011674.name: host galaxy of PS1-10ahf
gmw_00011674.overview: The host galaxy of PS1-10ahf is a faint, high-redshift galaxy located at a redshift of z=1.1 [arXiv:1402.1631]. Due to this high redshift, the galaxy appears approximately point-like and has an observed i-band magnitude of 24.17 [arXiv:1402.1631].

sources.summary: The paper detects a faint, nearly point-like host for PS1-10ahf, measures its absolute magnitude, and finds the transient coincident with the host centroid within errors.

quotes.quote: We carried out a similar search for the host galaxy of PS1-10ahf as for PS1-10pm to align it with the position of the transient. As can be seen in Fig. fig:10ahfim an object can clearly be seen at the expected coordinates and an observed i band magnitude of i =24.17 0.07 was determined using the photometry procedures available in the gaia software package naylor97 . Taking z=1.1 and A_ i =0.07 gives an absolute magnitude of M _ 3400 19.5 . Note that a point source was again chosen as a reference PSF for the galaxy however, due to high redshift involved, the target appears approximately point-like.

A separation of 0.148 '' was determined (using a pixel scale for the ACAM instrument of 0.25 arcsec/pixel http://wwwg.iac.es/Astronomy/observing/instruments.html ). This was found to be 0.214 '' at the pixel scale of ACAM, hence there is no evidence for an offset between the transient and the centroid of its host galaxy. PS1-10ahf is coincident, within the errors, to the centroid of the host galaxy which doesn't offer the same strong argument of it not being QSO variability when compared with PS1-10pm being offset.

gmw_00011675.name: Horologium I
gmw_00011675.overview: Horologium I is a dwarf spheroidal galaxy whose orbit is consistent with co-rotation along the Milky Way's polar structure [arXiv:1804.10230]. Backward integration of its orbit suggests that Horologium I is likely associated with the Magellanic system [arXiv:1804.10230].

sources.summary: The paper compiles Horologium I spectroscopy, identifies six bright confirmed members, and finds its orbit consistent with Magellanic association and the polar structure.

quotes.quote: Almost immediately after its discovery, koposov15b published VLT/FLAMES spectroscopy of Horologium I (Hor I). nagasaw18 used VLT/UVES to analyze the chemical abundance patterns of two of the member stars identified by koposov15b as well as a third star selected photometrically (which they confirmed as a Hor I member).

The results of the pawlowski13 and pawlowski15 predictions are mixed: several satellites are consistent with being co-rotating with the polar structure (Hor I, Ret II, Willman 1, and Tuc II), Segue 1 is counter-rotating, and four other dwarfs (UMa II, Com Ber, Boo II, and Boo I) are outside the expected proper motion ranges. Viewed in three dimensions and integrated backward in time, the orbits of Hor I, Hyi I, Car III, and Tuc II track that of the LMC quite closely, suggesting that these objects are probably associated with the Magellanic system. deason15 and sales17 both consider Hor I and Tuc II to be the most likely dwarfs of Magellanic origin, which is consistent with the results reported here.

gmw_00011676.name: Reticulum II
gmw_00011676.overview: Reticulum II is a dwarf spheroidal galaxy that contains at least 22 known member stars brighter than a g-band magnitude of 20 [arXiv:1804.10230]. It is a satellite galaxy with a possible ancient infall time of more than 12 billion years [arXiv:1804.10230].

sources.summary: Reticulum II is discussed through M2FS, GMOS, and FLAMES spectroscopy, chemical-abundance follow-up, 22 bright members, and a possible ancient infall time.

quotes.quote: Combined, these two samples include 6 spectroscopically-confirmed Hor I members and 13 confirmed non-members brighter than g=20 . simon15 observed Reticulum II (Ret II) with Magellan/M2FS and Gemini-S/GMOS, which they combined with public VLT/FLAMES spectroscopy from the Gaia ESO survey. A number of authors have studied the chemical abundances of some of the many bright Ret II members with Magellan/MIKE or Magellan/M2FS ji16,ji16c,roederer16,jf18 . In total, there are 22 known member stars and 23 non-members in Ret II at g < 20 . li18 medium-resolution spectroscopy of Carina II (Car II) with Magellan/IMACS, AAT/AAOmega, and VLT/FLAMES and found 18 member stars.

The results of the pawlowski13 and pawlowski15 predictions are mixed: several satellites are consistent with being co-rotating with the polar structure (Hor I, Ret II, Willman 1, and Tuc II), Segue 1 is counter-rotating, and four other dwarfs (UMa II, Com Ber, Boo II, and Boo I) are outside the expected proper motion ranges. Among the galaxies for which the binding energy is well-behaved, the rocha12 relation suggests infall times of 7  Gyr for Tri II, Segue 2, and Willman 1, 8  Gyr for Draco II and Tuc III, and >12  Gyr for Ret II.

sources.summary: Reticulum II has the largest local excess in the analysis, with TS exceeding blank fields at the favored masses, and its marginal excess had appeared in earlier studies.

quotes.quote: The largest excess is for Reticulum II, with a local significance peak in the mass range 290 < M_ / GeV < 588 ( 27 < M_ / GeV < 70 ) for the b b ( ^+ ^- ) annihilation channel, and at these masses the TS exceeds all of the blank fields. The marginal excess in Reticulum II has been observed in a number of previous studies . Ref. finds a local significance of 2.6-2.8 over a similar range of masses as this study for each channel, while found local excesses ranging from 2.4-3.2 .
From these we obtain for the b b channel p_ global = 0.016 ( 2.1 ) for Reticulum II.

The most significant local excess is for Reticulum II with a local significance >3 ( 2 global) at masses of 290 < M_ / GeV < 588 for the b b annihilation channel and 27 < M_ / GeV < 70 for the ^+ ^- annihilation channel.

gmw_00011677.name: Ursa Major II
gmw_00011677.overview: Ursa Major II is a dwarf spheroidal galaxy located at a distance of approximately 35 kpc [arXiv:1804.10230]. The galaxy contains at least one RR Lyrae variable star and is currently positioned near its orbital pericenter [arXiv:1804.10230].

sources.summary: Ursa Major II is discussed through DEIMOS spectroscopy, RR Lyrae imaging, eight bright known members, and an orbit placing it near pericenter.

quotes.quote: Car III has 3 confirmed member stars brighter than g=20 . martin07 and hereafter SG07 sg07 both observed Ursa Major II (UMa II) with Keck/DEIMOS. Because of the large angular size of the galaxy there is not much overlap between the two studies, and combined they identified a total of 29 member stars. frebel10 followed up the four brightest members on the RGB at high-resolution with Keck/HIRES, finding that one of the four, SDSSJ08500184+6313330, is actually a foreground main sequence star. Time-series imaging by dallora12 uncovered one RR Lyrae variable in UMa II, which is relatively bright (mean V magnitude of 18.39) given the distance of only 35 kpc to UMa II. UMa II has 8 known members and 52 confirmed non-members brighter than g=20 .

The results of the pawlowski13 and pawlowski15 predictions are mixed: several satellites are consistent with being co-rotating with the polar structure (Hor I, Ret II, Willman 1, and Tuc II), Segue 1 is counter-rotating, and four other dwarfs (UMa II, Com Ber, Boo II, and Boo I) are outside the expected proper motion ranges. More than half of the sample (Hyi I, Hor I, Ret II, Car III, UMa II, UMa I, Com Ber, Boo II, and Dra II) is within 4 kpc of the derived pericenter, and most of the satellites are no more than 100 Myr away from passing or reaching pericenter.

gmw_00011678.name: Ursa Major I
gmw_00011678.overview: Ursa Major I is a dwarf spheroidal galaxy located at a high Galactic latitude [arXiv:1804.10230]. It is a near-pericenter object with eight spectroscopically confirmed bright member stars [arXiv:1804.10230].

sources.summary: Ursa Major I is treated as a high-latitude velocity exception and a near-pericenter object, with three spectroscopic studies yielding eight confirmed bright members.

quotes.quote: The vertical velocities are largely directed toward the north pole of the Galaxy, with the exceptions being Segue 1, UMa I, Boo II, and Dra II, most of which are located at high Galactic latitude. More than half of the sample (Hyi I, Hor I, Ret II, Car III, UMa II, UMa I, Com Ber, Boo II, and Dra II) is within 4 kpc of the derived pericenter, and most of the satellites are no more than 100 Myr away from passing or reaching pericenter.

Ursa Major I (UMa I) has been the subject of three spectroscopic studies. Immediately after its discovery, kleyna05 obtained low S/N, high-resolution spectroscopy of seven stars with the HIRES spectrograph at Keck, five of which they determined were UMa I members. martin07 observed a larger sample of stars at medium resolution with Keck/DEIMOS, including two of the kleyna05 members. Finally, SG07 observed UMa I again with DEIMOS and more than doubled the sample of member stars, as well as re-observing all of the stars targeted by kleyna05 and most of the members identified by martin07 . All of the known bright ( g < 20 ) members of UMa I are in the SG07 data set, so I use that sample as my starting point for member selection. In total, there are eight spectroscopically confirmed UMa I members and 33 confirmed non-members at g < 20 .

gmw_00011679.name: Willman 1
gmw_00011679.overview: Willman 1 is a dwarf galaxy with an early infall time estimated at approximately 7 billion years ago [arXiv:1804.10230]. It is characterized by a robustly circular orbit and contains four known stellar members brighter than a g-magnitude of 20 [arXiv:1804.10230].

sources.summary: The paper describes Willman 1 as a low-velocity, robustly circular-orbit dwarf with early infall and a four-member bright spectroscopic sample.

quotes.quote: Among the galaxies for which the binding energy is well-behaved, the rocha12 relation suggests infall times of 7  Gyr for Tri II, Segue 2, and Willman 1, 8  Gyr for Draco II and Tuc III, and >12  Gyr for Ret II. Only Willman 1 is robustly on a circular orbit; the orbit of Segue 2 is consistent with being relatively round but with large uncertainties.

The first spectroscopy of Willman 1 was obtained by martin07 with Keck/DEIMOS. They classified 14 stars as Willman 1 members, including two red giants brighter than g=20 . However, high-resolution spectroscopy of one of the putative bright members with the Hobby-Eberly Telescope by siegel08 showed that it is instead a foreground main sequence star. siegel08 confirmed the second RGB star as a member and obtained spectra of 3 non-members at large radii. willman11 observed a much larger sample with Keck/DEIMOS, including both candidates from the martin07 study, and discovered an additional RGB member and two horizontal branch stars. Willman 1 contains four known members and 22 confirmed non-members at g<20 .

gmw_00011680.name: Coma Berenices
gmw_00011680.overview: Coma Berenices is a dwarf spheroidal galaxy [arXiv:1804.10230]. Spectroscopic observations of the galaxy have identified dozens of member stars, including two confirmed RR Lyrae variable stars [arXiv:1804.10230].

sources.summary: Coma Berenices is compared with polar-structure predictions, placed near pericenter, and described as having Keck spectroscopy plus RR Lyrae-confirmed members.

quotes.quote: The results of the pawlowski13 and pawlowski15 predictions are mixed: several satellites are consistent with being co-rotating with the polar structure (Hor I, Ret II, Willman 1, and Tuc II), Segue 1 is counter-rotating, and four other dwarfs (UMa II, Com Ber, Boo II, and Boo I) are outside the expected proper motion ranges. More than half of the sample (Hyi I, Hor I, Ret II, Car III, UMa II, UMa I, Com Ber, Boo II, and Dra II) is within 4 kpc of the derived pericenter, and most of the satellites are no more than 100 Myr away from passing or reaching pericenter.

Coma Berenices (Com Ber) has been observed spectroscopically with Keck/DEIMOS by SG07, who found 59 member stars, and brown14 , who identified an additional 14 members. frebel10 obtained high-resolution spectroscopy of the brightest three SG07 members. Two RR Lyrae variables in Com Ber were discovered by musella09 , both of which are included in the combined spectroscopic sample from SG07 and brown14 . There are 12 known stars in Com Ber brighter than g=20 and 29 spectroscopically confirmed non-members brighter than that limit.

gmw_00011681.name: Bo o tes II
gmw_00011681.overview: Bo o tes II is an ultra-faint dwarf galaxy that is currently poorly characterized [arXiv:1804.10230]. It is situated within 4 kpc of its orbital pericenter [arXiv:1804.10230].

sources.summary: Bo o tes II is discussed as a poorly characterized ultra-faint dwarf with Gemini, HIRES, and MIKE spectroscopy, four bright members, and a near-pericenter orbit.

quotes.quote: The results of the pawlowski13 and pawlowski15 predictions are mixed: several satellites are consistent with being co-rotating with the polar structure (Hor I, Ret II, Willman 1, and Tuc II), Segue 1 is counter-rotating, and four other dwarfs (UMa II, Com Ber, Boo II, and Boo I) are outside the expected proper motion ranges. More than half of the sample (Hyi I, Hor I, Ret II, Car III, UMa II, UMa I, Com Ber, Boo II, and Dra II) is within 4 kpc of the derived pericenter, and most of the satellites are no more than 100 Myr away from passing or reaching pericenter.

The only multi-object spectroscopic study of Bo o tes II (Boo II) published so far is that of koch09 , who identified a handful of member stars with Gemini/GMOS. kr14 followed up the brightest koch09 member star with Keck/HIRES, and ji16b obtained high-resolution Magellan/MIKE spectra of three koch09 members (including the star analyzed by kr14 ) and a fourth bright member identified via yet-unpublished spectroscopy. The chemical abundances of two of these member stars were also studied by francois16 with VLT/X-Shooter spectra. Despite being one of the least well characterized ultra-faint dwarfs at present, Boo II still contains 4 confirmed member stars and 4 confirmed non-members brighter than g=20 .

gmw_00011682.name: z=6.740 0.003 galaxy behind the Bullet Cluster
aliases_local: z=6.740 0.003 galaxy behind the Bullet Cluster
aliases_external: z850 band dropout 10 behind the Bullet Cluster
gmw_00011682.overview: The z=6.740 0.003 galaxy behind the Bullet Cluster is an intrinsically faint Lyman-break galaxy [arXiv:1207.5516]. It is gravitationally lensed by a magnification factor of approximately 3.0, allowing the detection of its faint Lyman-alpha emission line [arXiv:1207.5516].

sources.original_name: z=6.740 0.003 galaxy behind the Bullet Cluster
sources.summary: The paper spectroscopically confirms a faint lensed Lyman-break galaxy behind the Bullet Cluster, identifying its 9412 Angstrom emission as Lyman-alpha at z=6.740.

quotes.quote: We report the spectroscopic confirmation of an intrinsically faint Lyman Break Galaxy (LBG) identified as a band dropout behind the Bullet Cluster. We detect an emission line at =9412 at >5 significance using a 16hrs long exposure with FORS2 VLT. Based on the absence of flux in bluer broad-band filters, the blue color of the source, and the absence of additional lines, we identify the line as Lyman- at z=6.740 0.003 . The integrated line flux is f=(0.7 0.1 0.3) 10^ 17 (the uncertainties are due to random and flux calibration errors, respectively) making it the faintest Lyman- flux detected at these redshifts. Given the magnification of =3.0 0.2 the intrinsic (corrected for lensing) flux is f^ int =(0.23 0.03 0.10 0.02) 10^ 17 (additional uncertainty due to magnification), which is 2-3 times fainter than other such measurements in z 7 galaxies.

gmw_00011683.name: Underlying Arc
aliases_external: Abell 2744 z=2.6501 underlying arc at transient position
gmw_00011683.overview: The Underlying Arc is a lensed galaxy located at a spectroscopic redshift of 2.6501 [arXiv:2207.11658]. It is positioned across a fold caustic that creates mirrored images of the galaxy [arXiv:2207.11658].

sources.summary: The paper describes the z=2.6501 underlying arc as a fold-caustic lensed galaxy hosting the transient, measuring its photometry, stellar mass, and star-formation rate.

quotes.quote: The spectroscopic redshift of the underlying arc is 2.6501 from observations acquired using the Multi Unit Spectroscopic Explorer (MUSE) 2018MNRAS.473..663M . A so-called fold caustic creates mirror, merging images of a galaxy that intersects it. Such a pair of mirrored images allow us to estimate the position of the critical curve. For the arc where this event has been detected, we are able to identify the critical curve's location based on the arc's morphology, as shown by the green dashed line in the inserted panel in Fig. fig:mosaic , where A and B label the positions of the transient and its mirrored image on opposite sides of the critical curve, respectively.

We measure the photometry of the arc based on the official coadded images taken from the Hubble Frontier Field survey lotzkoekemoercoe17 . We find the total stellar mass and the star-formation rate of the arc are 7.4 10^7 M_ and 4.5 M_ yr ^ 1 , respectively, without correction for lensing magnification.

gmw_00011684.name: PLCK G092.5+42.9
gmw_00011684.overview: PLCK G092.5+42.9 is a strongly lensed, high-redshift starburst galaxy that forms a giant arc [arXiv:2009.13538]. It contains a turbulent gas reservoir with distinct blue and red kinematic components, featuring both a cool, extended gas phase and a warmer, compact phase [arXiv:2009.13538, arXiv:1811.11215].

sources.summary: The paper treats PLCK G092.5+42.9 as one of the brightest lensed GEMS, a giant-arc dusty starburst with low dense-gas ratios but CO-like dense-gas kinematics.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: The phase centers of the observations were located at =16:09:17.76 and =+60:45:21.0 for PLCK G092.5+42.9, and at =10:53:22.56 and =+60:51:49.0 for PLCK G145.2+50.9.

quotes.quote: In particular, most of this paper will focus on the three brightest GEMS, namely PLCK G092.5+42.9, PLCK G145.2+50.9, and PLCK G244.8+54.9 (the ``Ruby''), the latter being a maximal starburst Forming most of its stellar mass within one to a few dynamical times, with star-formation intensities approaching the highest values expected for a vertically-supported, self-gravitating gas disk e.g., thompson05 . at z=3 with local star-formation intensities of up to 2000 M _ yr ^ 1 kpc ^ 2 , but rather moderate SFEs of 1--10 Defined as the fraction of the available gas mass converted into stars per free-fall time. akin to giant molecular clouds in the Milky Way canameras17b . Our high-resolution PdBI, SMA, and ALMA ^ 12 CO and dust continuum interferometry indicate that PLCK G092.5+42.9 and PLCK G145.2+50.9 form giant arcs canameras15 , while PLCK G244.8+54.9 forms a partial Einstein ring canameras17a .

In the subsequent analysis and discussion, we only consider line properties and line ratios in PLCK G092.5+42.9, PLCK G145.2+50.9, and PLCK G244.8+54.9, the GEMS with the deepest integrations, while the others are not detected individually in dense-gas tracers. For these three sources, rms < 0.4 mK on EMIR spectra provide particularly tight flux upper limits. Line profiles Figures fig:emir , fig:noema , and fig:alma , show the profiles of emission lines detected at more than 3 with the IRAM-30m/EMIR, NOEMA, and ALMA. All spectra except those showing the complete NOEMA spectral windows have been continuum subtracted using first-order polynomials fitted to line-free spectral channels. Due to their limited S/N, the EMIR spectra of dense-gas tracers cannot distinguish individual velocity components.
