Given this angular scale and the disk-like morphology it is most likely that the two detected objects are galaxies which are in the process of merging. This is supported by the low ( <0.003 ) integrated probability Gardner2000.1 of having two objects of this brightness within 2 sq. arc-seconds. The faint neighboring patches are therefore likely to be smaller galaxy fragments belonging to the merging system.

gmw_00011792.overview: 40812 is a high-redshift galaxy with an estimated redshift of approximately 2.54 [arXiv:1702.00380]. It has an estimated stellar population age of 0.90 Gyr, and two-population modeling suggests it may be experiencing residual starburst activity [arXiv:1702.00380].

sources.summary: The paper analyzes galaxy 40812 with photometry and spectroscopy, estimating z=2.54 and age about 0.90 Gyr; two-population fits suggest residual star formation.

quotes.quote: The total exposure time was 2670 s for galaxy 40812, and 8010 s for galaxy 78891. The spectral resolution with this configuration was 7.75 /pixel; but, in order to gain signal/noise ratio (S/N), we binned the spectrum in bins of three and ten pixels respectively for both galaxies, so finally we got spectra with resolutions of 23.26 /pixel and 77.35 /pixel respectively (with a redshift of 2.5; this is equivalent to 6.6 and 22.1 /pixel at rest), with average S/N of 3.0 and 2.2 respectively.

By averaging the results of both models (the data are not independent so the error bars are not reduced proportionally to N by combining N data), we obtain 0.90^ +0.10 _ 0.12 Gyr and 0.37^ +0.48 _ 0.30 Gyr respectively for galaxies 40812 and 78891. With the same kind of combination, the most likely redshifts are 2.54 0.12 for galaxy 40812 and 2.48 0.21 for galaxy 78891. Other photometric redshifts have been published: for galaxy 40812: z=2.57 (CL12), z=2.63 (Rowan Robinson et al. 2008), z=2.16 (Rowan Robinson et al. 2013), z=2.53 (Williams et al. 2009); for galaxy 78891: z=2.71 (CL12), z=2.38 (Rowan Robinson et al. 2008), z=2.23 (Rowan Robinson et al. 2013), z=2.94 (Williams et al. 2009).

The fits with two SSPs are much better (they have lower average reduced ^2 ), especially when using the BC03 model, which allows us to see that the far UV, _ rest <1 680 , cannot be fitted simultaneously with the mid-infrared data for a single stellar population. It is clear that at least two components are necessary. The plausibility of this second solution with two SSPs may be related to the possibility that these galaxies are experiencing some starbursts.

gmw_00011793.overview: 78891 is a high-redshift galaxy with an estimated redshift of z=2.48 [arXiv:1702.00380]. Its stellar population has an estimated age of approximately 0.37 Gyr, and two-population fits suggest the presence of residual starburst activity [arXiv:1702.00380].

sources.summary: The paper analyzes galaxy 78891 with photometry and spectroscopy, estimating z=2.48 and age about 0.37 Gyr; two-population fits suggest residual star formation.

quotes.quote: The total exposure time was 2670 s for galaxy 40812, and 8010 s for galaxy 78891. The spectral resolution with this configuration was 7.75 /pixel; but, in order to gain signal/noise ratio (S/N), we binned the spectrum in bins of three and ten pixels respectively for both galaxies, so finally we got spectra with resolutions of 23.26 /pixel and 77.35 /pixel respectively (with a redshift of 2.5; this is equivalent to 6.6 and 22.1 /pixel at rest), with average S/N of 3.0 and 2.2 respectively.

By averaging the results of both models (the data are not independent so the error bars are not reduced proportionally to N by combining N data), we obtain 0.90^ +0.10 _ 0.12 Gyr and 0.37^ +0.48 _ 0.30 Gyr respectively for galaxies 40812 and 78891. With the same kind of combination, the most likely redshifts are 2.54 0.12 for galaxy 40812 and 2.48 0.21 for galaxy 78891. Other photometric redshifts have been published: for galaxy 40812: z=2.57 (CL12), z=2.63 (Rowan Robinson et al. 2008), z=2.16 (Rowan Robinson et al. 2013), z=2.53 (Williams et al. 2009); for galaxy 78891: z=2.71 (CL12), z=2.38 (Rowan Robinson et al. 2008), z=2.23 (Rowan Robinson et al. 2013), z=2.94 (Williams et al. 2009).

The fits with two SSPs are much better (they have lower average reduced ^2 ), especially when using the BC03 model, which allows us to see that the far UV, _ rest <1 680 , cannot be fitted simultaneously with the mid-infrared data for a single stellar population. It is clear that at least two components are necessary. The plausibility of this second solution with two SSPs may be related to the possibility that these galaxies are experiencing some starbursts.

gmw_00011794.name: J0230
gmw_00011794.overview: The starburst galaxy J0230 exhibits discrepant hydrogen-line velocity dispersions [arXiv:2208.10876]. Its low-surface-brightness emission wings are responsible for its most extreme velocities, resulting in a broader profile than its corrected dispersion [arXiv:2208.10876].

sources.summary: The paper finds J0230 has discrepant hydrogen-line velocity dispersions, with broader low-surface-brightness emission wings and low corrected dispersion relative to integrated measurements.

quotes.quote: For J0230 we measured a difference of 6 \ between the arc close to \ and close to \ and this does not explain the measured differences in velocity between. J0230 is the only galaxy which shows a difference between the _ tot measurements of \ (53 ) and \ (28 ). For all the other galaxies, the velocity dispersion of \ and \ are in good agreement with each other. Inspection of \ emission line map and spatially resolved kinematics (Fig. fig:2D_J0230 ) suggests that the most extreme velocities (responsible for the broader emission wings) are at lower surface brightness.
The only outlier, where _ m,corr is larger than _ tot is J0230 (Fig. fig:2D_J0230 ), where both values are low.

gmw_00011795.name: J1400
gmw_00011795.overview: J1400 is a face-on galaxy with a regular rotating disk profile that resembles a spiral galaxy [arXiv:2208.10876]. Its stars exhibit a larger average velocity dispersion than its gas, which is driven by a small area in the galaxy's center [arXiv:2208.10876].

sources.summary: The paper describes J1400 as a face-on spiral-like rotating disk whose stellar velocity dispersion exceeds the gas, likely from asymmetric drift or a central bulge.

quotes.quote: In J1400, which looks like a spiral galaxies viewed almost face on, we find that average the stars have a larger velocity dispersion than the gas. This could be caused by asymmetric drift, where the velocity dispersion of the stars is increased due to random motions, resulting in a larger velocity dispersion than that of the gas Oh22 .
In our sample, J1400 and J1211 show a regular rotating disk profile.

In two galaxies, J1211 and J1400, the large stellar velocity dispersion is driven by a small area in the centre of the galaxy. They are the brightest pixels and therefore dominate the integrated measurements.

gmw_00011796.name: J1525
gmw_00011796.overview: J1525 is a starburst galaxy characterized by a central starburst region [arXiv:2208.10876]. In this central area, the nebular velocity dispersion of the gas is enhanced and exceeds the stellar velocity dispersion, indicating localized stellar feedback [arXiv:2208.10876].

sources.summary: The paper highlights J1525 as a semi-regular starburst where central nebular velocity dispersion exceeds stellar dispersion, providing evidence for localized stellar-feedback turbulence.

quotes.quote: In J1525, we found that some of the Voronoi cells have a significant difference between the gas and stellar dispersion. Fig. fig:sigma_J1525 shows a spatially resolved map of the nebular (top) and stellar (bottom) velocity dispersion. These maps show that the nebular velocity dispersion is enhanced in the central knot of the galaxy with values up to 90 . This increase is not present in the stellar velocity map, where values of 50-60 \ are measured in the same area. The spatially-resolved emission line map of \ (Fig. fig:mapJ1525 , see also Sect. sec:resolved ) shows that this area has the highest \ flux.

Only in one galaxy, J1525, we found direct evidence that stellar feedback affects the velocity dispersion of the gas (Fig. fig:sigma_J1525 ), where the central starburst in the galaxy as a higher dispersion in the gas than in the stars.

gmw_00011797.name: J1211
gmw_00011797.overview: J1211 is a spiral galaxy viewed under a moderate inclination [arXiv:2208.10876]. It features a regular rotating disk profile and a central bulge, with no evidence of active galactic nucleus activity [arXiv:2208.10876].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J1211 as a regular rotating disk with central stellar dispersion larger than gas dispersion, consistent with a bulge rather than AGN-like gas motions.

quotes.quote: In J1211, which does not show double-peaked emission, we also find that _ star is larger than the _ gas towards the central area of the galaxy. The rest of the data points in J1211 follow roughly the one-to-one line, The SDSS image stamp in Fig. fig:SDSS_images of J1211 suggests that this galaxy is a spiral viewed under moderate inclination. The central voronoi cell is located towards the bulge of the galaxy. An AGN origin would be excluded as the gas does not show the high typically seen in AGN emission lines.
In our sample, J1400 and J1211 show a regular rotating disk profile.

gmw_00011798.name: J1353
gmw_00011798.overview: J1353 is an ongoing galaxy merger that exhibits a warped morphology and a diffuse southern tidal arm [arXiv:2208.10876]. The system hosts an ongoing starburst, with its infrared star formation rate measuring more than three times higher than its optical star formation rate [arXiv:2208.10876].

sources.summary: The paper treats J1353 as a merger with double-peaked emission lines, high velocity dispersion, a warped/tidal morphology, and separable blue and red kinematic components.

quotes.quote: Especially the merging galaxy J1353 is an extreme example, where the infrared SFR is more than three times higher than the optical SFR. The merger in J1353 could have initiated an starburst which is still partially embedded.

This behaviour can also be seen in J1353 and J1545 which have among the highest _ tot in the sample. The SDSS image of J1353 in Fig. fig:SDSS_images shows that this galaxy has a bright central region (targeted by the spectroscopy) with a bit of a warped morphology and a more diffuse component south of the bright region, suggestive of a tidal arm. This irregular appearance could be caused by the ongoing merger event.

The flux maps of the two components reveal that the morphology of the emission line maps is slightly different. The blue component shows a more elongated flux distribution, while the red component is more circular. Additionally, the red component is shifted with respect to the blue component towards the north-east by 0.7 kpc.

gmw_00011799.name: J1545
gmw_00011799.overview: J1545 is an interacting or merging galaxy [arXiv:2208.10876]. Its Sloan Digital Sky Survey image shows a hint of two nuclear components, and it exhibits double-peaked emission lines that suggest a merger origin [arXiv:2208.10876].

sources.summary: The paper describes J1545 as an interacting or merging galaxy with double-peaked emission, high velocity dispersion, and possible two nuclear components.

quotes.quote: Two galaxies show double-peaked emission lines: J1353 and J1545 (Fig. fig:doublepeaked ). For these galaxies we fitted the emission lines with a double Gaussian profile and report the central velocity and dispersion for the two components in Table. tab:fitresults . The two components of J1353 (Fig fig:doublepeaked , left panel) are separated by 208 , while the separation for J1545 (right panel) is only 120 . This makes the fitting of the latter galaxy more challenging and we fix the peak flux ratio to 1 in order to obtained a reasonable fit to the line profile.

The peak separation of J1545 is much smaller (120 ) and the emission line is of lower S/N than in the case of J1353. Therefore a spatially resolved two component fitting was not possible. The SDSS image of J1545 shows a hint of two nuclear components, suggesting a merger origin.

gmw_00011800.name: NGC 4782
gmw_00011800.overview: NGC 4782 is a central dumbbell galaxy located nearly at rest within the LGG 316 group potential [arXiv:astro-ph/0610904]. It is strongly interacting with NGC 4783 and hosts the radio source 3C278, which features X-ray cavities and bent radio jets [arXiv:astro-ph/0610904].

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 4782 as the central dumbbell galaxy and 3C278 host, nearly at rest in LGG 316, with X-ray cavities and jet bending shaped by surrounding gas motions.

quotes.quote: We present results from a 49.3 ks Chandra X-ray observation of the strongly interacting dumbbell galaxies NGC 4782(3C278) and NGC 4783 that constrain the kinematics of the interaction and models for bending the radio jets associated with NGC 4782. The galaxies are embedded in an approximately spherical distribution of group gas, centered on NGC 4782, that, away from the interaction region, is nearly isothermal with mean temperature kT 1.4 0.4 keV. The X-ray morphology suggests that NGC 4783 is infalling into a single, massive galaxy group (LGG 316) with NGC 4782 nearly at rest at the center of the group potential.

A 45 Myr old X-ray cavity, with enthalpy of 4.4 10^ 57 ergs, coincides with the eastern radio lobe of 3C278. X-ray knots are found on both the radio jet and counter-jet, coincident with peaks in the radio emission. If we assume a light, mildly relativistic jet in 3C278, then ram pressure velocities of 100-200 impacting the eastern jet and 170 acting on the western radio lobe are sufficient to produce their observed bending.

gmw_00011801.name: NGC 4783
gmw_00011801.overview: NGC 4783 is an infalling dumbbell galaxy within the LGG 316 galaxy group [arXiv:astro-ph/0610904]. The galaxy features a sharp X-ray cold front to its east and a 15-kiloparsec ram-pressure-stripped tail extending to the west [arXiv:astro-ph/0610904].

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 4783 as an infalling dumbbell galaxy whose cold front, stripped X-ray tail, and measured velocity constrain the interaction geometry in LGG 316.

quotes.quote: The X-ray morphology suggests that NGC 4783 is infalling into a single, massive galaxy group (LGG 316) with NGC 4782 nearly at rest at the center of the group potential. NGC 4783 shows a sharp X-ray surface brightness edge (cold front) to the east and a 15 kpc ram-pressure-stripped tail to the west. Analysis of this cold front indicates NGC 4783 is moving east with a total velocity 870^ +270 _ 400 (Mach 1.4^ +0.5 _ 0.7 ) at an inclination angle 46^ (>33^ ) towards us with respect to the plane of the sky.

gmw_00011802.name: J0850 + 5159
gmw_00011802.overview: J0850 + 5159 is a quasar that exhibits dust reddening and a 2175 Angstrom ultraviolet extinction bump [arXiv:1910.14054]. This spectroscopic feature is accompanied by strong metal absorption lines, with its spectral energy distribution best modeled by an LMC2 supershell extinction curve [arXiv:1910.14054].

sources.summary: The paper selected J0850 + 5159 for OH absorption because of strong metal absorption, dust reddening, a 2175 Angstrom bump, weak radio emission, and a modeled LMC2 extinction curve.

quotes.quote: We selected J0850 + 5159 and J0852 + 3435 to search for OH 8 \ absorption because they both show strong \ 1 \ and metal absorption lines, dust reddening [ \ 0.3-0.4 ] and the 2175 \ ultraviolet (UV) extinction bump srianand2008 . Note that the broad absorption bump at 2175 \ is a spectroscopic feature due to extinction by interstellar dust, most likely polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons, that is seen in the Milky Way, Magellanic Clouds, as well as high- z absorption-line systems draine2003,wang2004,jiang2011 . Hence, while the reason for the reddening of the optical continuum of the quasar could be degenerate (see Section sec_introduction ), the 2175 \ bump confirms the presence of dust unambiguously. The above two quasars were part of a systematic survey of \ 1 \ absorption in z 1.3 strong \ systems gupta2009 . Strong absorption from \ [ rest equivalent width (REW) of 2796, \ = 4.6 \ and 2.9 \ for J0850 + 5159 and J0852 + 3435, respectively ] , \ (REW of 2600, \ = 2.3 \ and 2.1 \ for J0850 + 5159 and J0852 + 3435, respectively), as well as weaker transitions of , , \ and , are detected at z=1.3 in the Sloan Digital Sky Survey SDSS; york2000 spectra of the quasars.

srianand2008 have modeled the SED of the quasar J0850 + 5159 using the SDSS composite spectrum of vandenberk2001 and different dust extinction curves redshifted to the rest-frame of the intervening \ absorption. The LMC2 supershell extinction curve is found to give best fit to the quasar SED, with \ 0.3.

gmw_00011803.name: J0852 + 3435
gmw_00011803.overview: J0852 + 3435 is a background quasar with a relatively weak 1.4 GHz radio flux density of 69 mJy [arXiv:1910.14054]. Its spectral energy distribution is best fit by an LMC2 extinction curve, showing dust reddening and a 9.7 micron silicate absorption feature [arXiv:1910.14054].

sources.summary: The paper selected J0852 + 3435 for OH searches, citing its strong absorption systems, 2175 Angstrom bump, weak radio flux, LMC2-like SED, and total optical depth.

quotes.quote: We selected J0850 + 5159 and J0852 + 3435 to search for OH 8 \ absorption because they both show strong \ 1 \ and metal absorption lines, dust reddening [ \ 0.3-0.4 ] and the 2175 \ ultraviolet (UV) extinction bump srianand2008 .

In addition, the 9.7 silicate feature is detected in absorption from the z=1.3 system towards J0852 + 3435 kulkarni2011 . The background radio sources are relatively weak, with 1.4 GHz flux density of 63 mJy and 69 mJy for J0850 + 5159 and J0852 + 3435, respectively.

The SED of the quasar J0852 + 3435, similar to J0850 + 5159, is best fit with LMC2 extinction curve and \ 0.4 srianand2008 . The \ derived from SED modeling is 7 10^ 21 ^ 1 . The \ 1 \ absorption from the \ system comprises a narrow (FWHM = 23 ) and a broad (FWHM = 63 ) component, separated by 34 . The total optical depth of the absorption is 7 .

gmw_00011804.name: J1501 + 1822
gmw_00011804.overview: J1501 + 1822 is a red, radio-loud quasar located at a redshift of approximately 1.1859 [arXiv:1910.14054]. Its spectrum is characterized by weak emission lines, detected reddening, and strong associated absorption, though it lacks detected 21-cm absorption [arXiv:1910.14054].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J1501 + 1822 as a red radio-loud quasar with strong associated absorption, weak emission lines, detected reddening, and no associated 21-cm absorption.

quotes.quote: We identified the red quasars, J1501 + 1822 and J1521 + 5508, from the SDSS Data Release 12 DR12; alam2015 , specifically using the \ catalog of zhu2013 . We searched for strong \ absorption [ \ 1 ] whose redshifted \ 1 \ line can be observed with uGMRT, towards radio-loud quasars [ 1.4 GHz flux 100 mJy in the Faint Images of the Radio Sky at Twenty-Centimeters FIRST; white1997 catalog ] .

The SDSS spectrum of the quasar J1501 + 1822 is intriguing as it is devoid of any prominent broad emission lines. We identify the redshift of the quasar from \ emission line and associated \ absorption line as \ = 1.1859 0.0007. There is a second \ absorption 570 \ blueward at z =1.18177 (see panel (a) of Fig. fig:mgii ). The 2796 transition at z =1.18594 is blended with the 2803 transition at z =1.18177, leading to \ 2.3 .
We also do not detect \ absorption from the strong \ systems associated with the quasar J1501 + 1822 [ \ 3 10^ 19 ( /100 K) (1/ ) ] .

gmw_00011805.name: J1521 + 5508
gmw_00011805.overview: J1521 + 5508 is a red, radio-loud quasar with broad emission lines in its SDSS spectrum [arXiv:1910.14054]. It features three blueshifted Mg II absorption systems and exhibits systemic 21-cm absorption [arXiv:1910.14054].

sources.summary: The paper reports J1521 + 5508 as a red radio-loud quasar with multiple blueshifted absorption systems and detected systemic 21-cm absorption offset from the Mg II absorbers.

quotes.quote: We identified the red quasars, J1501 + 1822 and J1521 + 5508, from the SDSS Data Release 12 DR12; alam2015 , specifically using the \ catalog of zhu2013 . We searched for strong \ absorption [ \ 1 ] whose redshifted \ 1 \ line can be observed with uGMRT, towards radio-loud quasars [ 1.4 GHz flux 100 mJy in the Faint Images of the Radio Sky at Twenty-Centimeters FIRST; white1997 catalog ] .

The red quasar J1521 + 5508 shows broad emission lines in its SDSS spectrum at \ = 1.0701 0.0004. This is an interesting quasar which has three blueshifted \ absorption at z= 1.04504, 1.05926 and 1.06540 (see panel (b) of Fig. fig:mgii ). The \ absorption lines could be arising from strong outflows from the quasar or from an over-density of galaxies clustering around the quasar.

We have detected \ 1 \ absorption at the systemic redshift of the quasar J1521 + 5508, with \ =(1.2 0.2) 10^ 20 ( /100 K) (1/ ) . However, we do not detect \ absorption from the blueshifted strong \ absorption systems along this line-of-sight [ \ 5 10^ 19 ( /100 K) (1/ ) ] .

gmw_00011806.name: UGC 11973
gmw_00011806.overview: UGC 11973 is a massive, late-type giant spiral galaxy with a radius reaching 30 kpc [arXiv:2004.04701]. It hosts several massive young stellar complexes that are younger than 300 million years [arXiv:2004.04701].

sources.summary: The paper presents photometry and spectroscopy of young stellar complexes in the late giant spiral UGC 11973, describing it as a very bright, massive system with a 30 kpc radius.

quotes.quote: We present results of the analysis of photometric and spectroscopic observations of the young stellar complexes in the late giant spiral galaxy UGC 11973. Photometric analysis in the UBVRI bands have been carried out for the 13 largest complexes. For one of them, metallicity of the surrounding gas Z=0.013 0.005 , the mass M=(4.6 1.6) 10^6 M_ , and the age of the stellar complex t=(2.0 1.1) 10^6 yr were evaluated, using spectroscopic data. It is shown that all complexes are massive ( M 1.7 10^5 M_ ) stellar groups younger than 3 10^8 yr.

Among relatively close galaxies, UGC 11973 is one of the brightest, largest, and most massive stellar systems (see Table table:gal ). Its radius reaches 30 kpc and absolute magnitude M(B)_0^i < -22^m . The analysis of the rotation curve has shown that the total mass of the galaxy within only 10 kpc from the center (1/3 of the radius D_ 25 ) is 2.4 10^ 11 M_ afanasiev1988 . The total stellar mass of UGC 11973, as estimated from the luminosity in the B and K bands, is 9.4 10^ 10 M_ graur2017 .

gmw_00011807.name: wide-angle tail radio galaxy (WAT)
gmw_00011807.overview: The wide-angle tail radio galaxy (WAT) is located within the moderately massive galaxy cluster Abell 1033 [arXiv:2207.11040]. It is connected to an elongated, ultra-steep radio source that exhibits a stalling spectral index along its tail [arXiv:2207.11040].

sources.summary: The paper treats WAT as the radio galaxy connected to Abell 1033’s GReET, measuring spectral steepening and curvature that cannot be explained by pure aging alone.

quotes.quote: Abell 1033 (PSZ2 G189.31+59.24, hereafter A1033) is a moderately massive ( M_ 500 = 3.24^ +0.30 _ 0.32 10^ 14 M_ ) galaxy cluster at a redshift of z = 0.126 Planck2016 which shows recent merger activity and hosts a steep spectrum source previously classified as a radio phoenix deGasperin2015 . In addition, the cluster contains an elongated, ultra-steep ( 4.0 ) source connected to a wide-angle tail radio galaxy (WAT), a mechanism of gentle re-acceleration was suggested to explain the peculiar spectral properties deGasperin2017 .

At the position of the WAT host galaxy, we measure a spectral index of =-0.65 in both frequency intervals. Along the tail, the spectral index gradually steepens, the steepening is stronger at higher frequency. At the location of the bend in the tail, the spectral index does not steepen further and remains approximately constant along the GReET for both frequency ranges.

However, at around 350 kpc form the injection point, the spectrum does not steepen further and a plateau in spectral index associated with the GReET is reached. This constant and even flattening spectral index cannot be explained with the extrapolation of a pure aging model, even when considering a change in the WAT's velocity. The same stalling in the spectral index trend can also be observed in the color-color diagram in fig:colorcolor , where after initial JP-like aging, the spectral evolution stops and appears frozen for several 100 kpc.

gmw_00011808.name: EA01A
gmw_00011808.overview: EA01A is a young post-starburst galaxy [arXiv:0807.0440] that is interacting with its companion, EA01B [arXiv:0807.0440]. It exhibits a disturbed, low-surface-brightness morphology with numerous stellar clusters [arXiv:0807.0440] and a receding gaseous tidal arm [arXiv:0807.0440].

sources.summary: The paper describes EA01A as a young post-starburst galaxy in an interacting binary, with disturbed low-surface-brightness morphology and a receding H I tidal arm traced in VLA channel maps.

quotes.quote: EA01A is the bluest galaxy of the PSG sample of , suggesting it is also the youngest sample member. HST/WFPC2 images in the F435W and F702W bands yang04 provide strong evidence that the couple is interacting. EA01A, positioned about 11 '' east of EA01B, contains many stellar clusters that are very conspicuous compared with the overall rather low surface brightness of this galaxy. This diffuse appearance suggests it is on the verge of disintegrating due to the injected orbital energy.

A plausible interpretation of the \ flux integrated map and the channel maps is that we are seeing one receding gaseous tidal arm emanating from EA01A and two tidal arms connected to EA01B, with the east arm approaching us and the west arm moving away from us. Little of the detected gas appears to be directly connected with the galaxies themselves, which show up as minima in the \ column density. This absence of dense gas may be related to the fact that these galaxies are in a post-starburst phase. The EA01A arm runs in a south-eastern direction and can be traced through 3 consecutive channel maps from 22372.3 km s ^ 1 to 22394.5 km s ^ 1 .

gmw_00011809.name: EA01B
gmw_00011809.overview: EA01B is a bulge-dominated early-type spiral galaxy that features strongly asymmetric stellar arms [arXiv:0807.0440]. It is interacting with EA01A, with the two galaxies connected by a stellar bridge and associated with two gaseous tidal arms [arXiv:0807.0440].

sources.summary: The paper presents EA01B as a bulge-dominated early-type spiral interacting with EA01A, with asymmetric stellar arms and two associated gaseous tidal arms in the H I maps.

quotes.quote: EA01B, on the other hand, is a bulge dominated early-type spiral galaxy; it sports strongly asymmetric stellar arms. Both galaxies appear to be connected by a stellar bridge. Previous HI observations chang01 had already uncovered sizable quantities of neutral gas in or near these galaxies but they lacked the spatial resolution to locate the gas with any precision within this galactic binary system.

A plausible interpretation of the \ flux integrated map and the channel maps is that we are seeing one receding gaseous tidal arm emanating from EA01A and two tidal arms connected to EA01B, with the east arm approaching us and the west arm moving away from us. Little of the detected gas appears to be directly connected with the galaxies themselves, which show up as minima in the \ column density. This absence of dense gas may be related to the fact that these galaxies are in a post-starburst phase. The EA01A arm runs in a south-eastern direction and can be traced through 3 consecutive channel maps from 22372.3 km s ^ 1 to 22394.5 km s ^ 1 . The west EA01B ``arm'' shows up as emission in 3 consecutive channel maps from 22372.3 km s ^ 1 to 22394.5 km s ^ 1 .

gmw_00011810.name: WGD2021-4115
gmw_00011810.overview: WGD2021-4115 is a gravitationally lensed system consisting of two quasar images [arXiv:1711.03971]. These images are positioned on either side of a round, faint, red deflector galaxy [arXiv:1711.03971].

sources.summary: The paper identifies WGD2021-4115 as a two-image lensed quasar with a faint red deflector galaxy; simple models require a high quadrupole component.

quotes.quote: In this paper, we report the discovery of two previously unknown lensed quasars, WGD2038-4008 (.a.=20:38:02.65, dec.=-40:08:14.64) and WGD2021-4115 (.a.=20:21:39.45, dec.=--41:15:57.11), from the joint use of WISE colour selection, Gaia multiplet detection, and DES image inspection.

WGD2021-4115: system configuration and gravitational lens models consists of two quasar images at either side of a round, faint, red galaxy (fig. fig:WGD2021 ). We denote the farther image (with shorter arrival time) as A. Its counterimage B lies very close to the deflector galaxy. The same approach as above has been followed for , obtaining object positions and grizY magnitudes from the DES image cutouts and performing simple lens models.

In this case, G is smaller, fainter, and very close to image B, which makes the cutout models highly degenerate. The structural parameters of G cannot be recovered reliably, and its magnitudes have significant uncertainties. From simple lens models, a high quadrupole component (shear _ s =0.20 0.04 or flattening q=0.50 0.08 ) is needed to account for the off-centering of images A, B with respect to the lens galaxy, still producing only two quasar images due to the highly asymmetric configuration.

gmw_00011811.name: SWAG-X J084927.5--045721
gmw_00011811.overview: SWAG-X J084927.5--045721 is a radio source with a near-circular shape and a diameter of 50 arcseconds [arXiv:2208.13997]. It has an integrated 888 MHz flux density of 228 mJy and is surrounded by five nearby optical and infrared sources [arXiv:2208.13997].

sources.summary: The paper identifies SWAG-X J084927.5--045721 as a near-circular ORC candidate in the 888 MHz SWAG-X survey, with 228 mJy integrated flux and multiple optical/infrared sources nearby.

quotes.quote: The source has a near circular shape with a diameter of 50^ . The integrated 888 MHz flux density is 228 mJy. This source is also known as PMN J0849-0457 Parkes-MIT-NRAO Surveys; wright94 . We identify five optical/infrared sources near the ORC candidate.

gmw_00011812.name: EMU-PS J222339.5--483449
gmw_00011812.overview: EMU-PS J222339.5--483449 is an odd radio circle candidate discovered in the EMU-PS survey field [arXiv:2208.13997]. The source exhibits a near-circular radio morphology with a diameter of 80 arcseconds and is isolated from nearby extended radio structures [arXiv:2208.13997].

sources.summary: The paper independently rediscovers EMU-PS J222339.5--483449 as an EMU-PS ORC candidate with a near-circular 80 arcsec radio morphology isolated from nearby extended structures.

quotes.quote: This ORC candidate is in the EMU-PS survey field and was also discovered serendipitously norris22prep . We independently rediscover this source using our machine learning technique. It has a near circular morphology with diameter of 80^ . From left to right, the top panels of Figure FIG:ORCCandidates1 show radio continuum image, radio contours overlaid on WISE-W1 infrared image, and a smaller cutout with the same size that is used to train the SOM. The 12^ 12^ radio continuum image shows that it has no association with any of the extended radio structures in its vicinity.

gmw_00011813.name: EMU-PS J213409.5--533631
gmw_00011813.overview: EMU-PS J213409.5--533631 is a peculiar radio source located in the galaxy cluster Abell 3785 [arXiv:2208.13997]. Nicknamed the "dancing ghosts," its twisted structure is likely shaped by the interaction of an intergalactic wind with radio jets from two lenticular galaxies [arXiv:2208.13997].

sources.summary: The paper describes EMU-PS J213409.5--533631 as the highest-distance peculiar EMU-PS source, the “dancing ghosts” in Abell 3785, likely shaped by intergalactic wind and radio jets.

quotes.quote: EMU-PS J213409.5--533631 This peculiar radio source found in the EMU-PS consists of a group of distorted radio components, collectively known as PKS 2130--538 otrupcek91 , and nicknamed ''the dancing ghosts" see Figure 21 in norris21 . This radio source has the highest Euclidean distance which means that our algorithm classifies it as the most peculiar source in EMU-PS. The top panels of Figure FIG:unusual_radio_shapes-1 show radio and infrared images. The top left panel of Figure FIG:unusual_radio_shapes-1-3big shows radio continuum contours overlaid on the DES 3-color ( gri ) composite image ( 12^ 7^ ).

These ''dancing ghosts" are in galaxy cluster ABELL 3785 abell89 . The twisted shape of this structure is possibly due to an interaction of a intergalactic wind with radio jets from two super massive black holes in lenticular galaxies ``A" and ``C" norris21 . The two galaxies ``A" and ``C" shown in Figure FIG:unusual_radio_shapes-1-3big have reported z_ spec =0.0763 and 0.07836 , respectively lauer14 .

gmw_00011814.name: EMU-PS J220026.3--561030
gmw_00011814.overview: EMU-PS J220026.3--561030 is a peculiar radio source associated with a bright elliptical galaxy [arXiv:2208.13997]. Its radio jets exhibit a circular morphology that may be shaped by the nearby Abell 3826 galaxy cluster environment [arXiv:2208.13997].

sources.summary: The paper presents EMU-PS J220026.3--561030 as a high-distance peculiar radio source with circular jet morphology near two galaxies and likely shaped by the Abell 3826 cluster environment.

quotes.quote: EMU-PS J220026.3--561030 This peculiar radio source also has a high Euclidean distance in the EMU-PS. The middle panels of Figure FIG:unusual_radio_shapes-1 show radio and infrared images. These images imply a circular morphology where radio jets are emitted from the galaxy nucleus and may have caused the jets to have bent in nearly half circles (analogous to a rotating garden sprinkler). We identify two galaxies near this radio source.

Near the geometrical centre of the structure, we find a bright elliptical galaxy 2MASX J22002408-5610413 (WISEA J220024.11-561041.7) labelled as ``A" with z_ spec =0.0757 jones09 . Another galaxy located towards the east, labelled ``B", is 2MASX J22003234--5610273 (WISEA J220032.19-561026.0), with z_ ph = 0.08 0.01 . The rich galaxy cluster ABELL 3826 abell89 at z=0.075 is centred 4.2^ (or 0.36 Mpc) north-west of the elliptical galaxy ``A". This suggests that the shape of this radio source is induced by the cluster environment.

gmw_00011815.name: EMU-PS J215026.5--621006
gmw_00011815.overview: EMU-PS J215026.5--621006 is a peculiar radio source that features a distinct radio core [arXiv:2208.13997]. It exhibits extended radio emission directed toward both the west and the northeast, with multiple candidate host galaxies identified nearby [arXiv:2208.13997].

sources.summary: The paper describes EMU-PS J215026.5--621006 as a peculiar radio source with a core, west and northeast extended emission, and multiple nearby candidate host galaxies.

quotes.quote: EMU-PS J215026.5--621006 This peculiar radio source has a radio core and an extended emission towards west and north-east. The bottom panels of Figure FIG:unusual_radio_shapes-1 show radio and infrared images. The middle left panel of Figure FIG:unusual_radio_shapes-1-3big shows radio continuum contours overlaid on the DES 3-color ( gri ) composite image ( 12^ 12^ ). We identify three galaxies near the radio source.

The galaxy labelled as ``A" in the center of circular structure is 2MASX J21502732-6210129 (WISEA J215027.29-621013.3) at z_ ph =0.07 0.01 . There is a lenticular galaxy (``B") in the south-west direction where the extended emission towards the west starts and whose jet passes over the circular emission towards north-east. This galaxy is 2MASX J21501790-6211070 (WISEA J215017.86-621106.4) with z_ ph =0.06 0.01 .

gmw_00011816.name: SWAG-X J093803.4--015247
gmw_00011816.overview: SWAG-X J093803.4--015247 is an isolated, peculiar radio source [arXiv:2208.13997]. It is possibly an end-on remnant radio galaxy that displays indications of a partial outer ring similar to odd radio circles [arXiv:2208.13997].

sources.summary: The paper identifies SWAG-X J093803.4--015247 as an isolated peculiar SWAG-X radio source, possibly an end-on remnant radio galaxy with a partial ORC-like outer ring.

quotes.quote: SWAG-X J093803.4--015247 This peculiar radio source is in the SWAG-X field. The top panels of Figure FIG:unusual_radio_shapes-2 show the radio continuum image (left), radio contours overlaid on WISE-W1 infrared image (middle), and a smaller cutout with the same size that is used to train the SOM (right). The 12^ 12^ radio continuum image shows that it has no association with any of the nearby extended radio sources.

The middle right panel of Figure FIG:unusual_radio_shapes-1-3big shows radio continuum contours overlaid on an SDSS 3-color ( gri ) composite image. We find an optical/infrared object labelled as ``A"(2MASS J09380334-0152480, WISEA J093803.35-015247.9) with z_ ph =0.22 0.01 near the geometrical center of the source. This radio structure with a bright source at its center is possibly an end-on remnant radio galaxy, though it shows indications of a partial outer ring in radio emission similar to ORCs.

gmw_00011817.name: SWAG-X J085234.4+062801
gmw_00011817.overview: SWAG-X J085234.4+062801 is a radio source characterized by a peculiar radio morphology [arXiv:2208.13997]. Its radio emission is dominated by two overlapping bright galaxies, with three companion galaxies identified near the edges of the structure [arXiv:2208.13997].

sources.summary: The paper describes SWAG-X J085234.4+062801 as a peculiar radio morphology with nearby edge galaxies, dominated by two overlapping bright galaxies and a possible bent-tail jet.

quotes.quote: SWAG-X J085234.4+062801 This peculiar radio morphology is also in the SWAG-X field. From left to right, the bottom panels of Figure FIG:unusual_radio_shapes-2 show the radio continuum image, radio contours overlaid on WISE-W1 infrared image, and a smaller cutout with the same size that is used to train the SOM. We identify three galaxies near the edges of this structure.

Towards the north edge, we find a galaxy ``A" (2MASX J08523573+0628209, WISEA J085235.74+062821.1) at z_ spec = 0.15958 (from SDSS). Near the south-west edge, we identify a galaxy ``B" (SDSS J085232.56+062737.6, WISEA J085232.90+062731.9) at z_ ph =0.18 0.06 . Another optical/infrared object ``C" lies due south-east (2MASS J08523531+0627206, WISEA J085235.31+062720.2) and has z_ ph =0.26 0.05 . The Gaia parallax ( 3.5 0.1 mas) and proper motion ( 42.9 0.1 mas/year) measurements suggests it to be a star. The radio emission appears to be dominated by the two overlapping bright galaxies ``A" and ``B”.

gmw_00011818.name: gravitational lens (J2000: 20^ h 33^ m 41 . ^ s 9 , 47^ 23 43 4 )
gmw_00011818.overview: The gravitational lens system J2000: 20h 33m 41.9s, 47d 23m 43.4s consists of a quadruply lensed quasar [arXiv:1905.09338]. The main deflector of this system is a massive elliptical galaxy with a line-of-sight velocity dispersion of 250 km/s [arXiv:1905.09338].

sources.summary: The paper models a coordinate-identified quadruply lensed quasar whose main deflector is a massive elliptical galaxy, using imaging, time delays, environment data, and lens-galaxy velocity dispersion.

quotes.quote: In this paper, we present the results of a detailed lens modeling analysis of the gravitational lens (J2000: 20^ h 33^ m 41 . ^ s 9 , 47^ 23 43 4 ), a quadruply-lensed quasar discovered by Morgan2004 . The source redshift is = Sluse2012 , and the main deflector is a massive elliptical galaxy at a redshift of = 0.0002 hereafter H0LiCOW X sluse2019 , updating the = 0.661 0.001 measurement from Eigenbrod2006b ). bonvin2019b (hereafter COSMOGRAIL XVIII) measure the time delays between the quasar images based on 14 years of monitoring, and H0LiCOW X study the environment and LOS to the lens, based on multi-band imaging and targeted spectroscopy.

To deblend the lensing galaxy and the quasar images, we modeled each monochromatic slice with a model of the system composed of four Moffat moffat1969 components for the quasar lensed images, and one de Vaucouleurs vaucouleurs1948 model for the lensing galaxy. After removing the quasar images from the datacube, we extracted the lensing galaxy spectrum within a square aperture of 9 pixels = 1 8 side-length. The velocity dispersion was obtained following the same procedure as suyu2010b,suyu2013 , resulting in an inference of _ LOS = 250 km s ^ 1 with a statistical uncertainty of 10 km s^ 1 .

gmw_00011819.name: IGR J04059+5416
gmw_00011819.overview: IGR J04059+5416 is an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1502.05054] that is identified as a galaxy in the Sloan Digital Sky Survey [arXiv:1502.05054]. Its X-ray spectrum and extended near-infrared emission suggest that it is a moderately variable Seyfert galaxy [arXiv:1502.05054].

sources.summary: The paper identifies IGR J04059+5416 as an AGN/Seyfert galaxy using its extended near-infrared counterpart, SDSS galaxy identification, and absorbed X-ray spectrum.

quotes.quote: Both sources have counterparts in the catalog of extended 2 Micron All-Sky Survey sources, and the counterpart to IGR J04059+5416 has been previously identified as a galaxy. Thus, we place IGR J04059+5416 in the class of Active Galactic Nuclei (AGN), and we suggest that IGR J08297--4250 is also an AGN. If this identification is correct, the near-IR images suggest that the host galaxy of IGR J08297--4250 may be merging with a smaller nearby galaxy. For IGR J04059+5416, the 0.3--86 keV spectrum from Chandra and INTEGRAL is consistent with an absorbed power-law with a column density of N_ H = (3.1^ +2.0 _ 1.5 ) 10^ 22 cm ^ 2 and a photon index of = 1.4 0.7 , and we suggest that it is a Seyfert galaxy.

Figure fig:j04059_kband shows the UKIDSS K band image for IGR J04059+5416, and the extended emission around 2MASX J04055765+5418446 is visible. The fact that this is an extended near-IR source provides evidence that it is an AGN. In addition, the source is present in the Sloan Digitized Sky Survey, SDSS J040557.62+541844.8, and is identified as a galaxy.

For IGR J04059+5416, the optical and near-IR information provides the strongest evidence that it is an AGN, but the type of AGN is not immediately clear. The Chandra + INTEGRAL spectral fit for the N_ INTEGRAL /N_ Chandra = 1 gives a very hard spectrum ( = 0.9 0.2 ), which might argue that the source is a blazar. However, the non-detection in the 57--86 keV band would require a sharp spectral cutoff, and this would not be expected for a blazar at this energy. It is more likely that the source is moderately variable and that it is a Seyfert galaxy.

gmw_00011820.name: UGC 3091
gmw_00011820.overview: UGC 3091 is a low-surface-brightness spiral galaxy [arXiv:1511.06365]. Its spiral structure is difficult to discern by visual inspection, which presents challenges for measuring its spiral pitch angle [arXiv:1511.06365].

sources.summary: The paper flags UGC 3091 as a low-surface-brightness spiral where Spirality and 2DFFT disagree and neither method is confident.

quotes.quote: UGC 3091 With a disagreement factor of 1.5, Spirality's result disagrees significantly with 2DFFT's result. The respective error bars of 5.9 and 4.9 show that neither code is particularly confident in its measurement. This galaxy has a low surface brightness, and its spiral structure is difficult to make out by visual inspection. Such galaxies pose a challenge to any pitch angle measurement code.

gmw_00011821.name: UGC 3997
gmw_00011821.overview: UGC 3997 is a spiral galaxy [arXiv:1511.06365]. It features distinct spiral arms in both its inner and outer disk regions, which exhibit different pitch angles [arXiv:1511.06365].

sources.summary: The paper uses UGC 3997 to show that pitch-angle results can depend on whether inner or outer disk spirals are measured.

quotes.quote: UGC 3997 This is a particularly interesting case. When one of our researchers measured this galaxy using Spirality, and another using 2DFFT, without communicating with one another, the researchers reached a disagreement factor of 1.12, as indicated in Table tab:DM30 . Afterward, when the researchers compared results, they found that 2DFFT had been instructed to measure the spirals in the outer disk, while Spirality had been instructed to measure the spirals in the inner disk. When Spirality's measurement annulus was adjusted to match 2DFFT's, Spirality produced a result of -12.4 0.9 , which is consistent with 2DFFT's result.

gmw_00011822.overview: The galaxy 10909 is a double-lobed radio active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1901.10168]. It exhibits a twisted, X-shaped radio structure that suggests a restarted active phase caused by jet interruption or axis re-orientation [arXiv:1901.10168].

sources.summary: The paper classifies 10909 as a restarted double-lobed radio AGN with X-shaped structure and possible VLBA double emission peaks.

quotes.quote: Within the sources in our multi-component sample the following objects fall in the restarted AGN category due to their radio structure at 3 GHz: 10909, 10915, 10933, 10935. 10909 is a double lobed radio AGN with the furthest away lobes being the oldest, while closer to the core it exhibits another double-lobed structure. This twisted radio structure is most probably not the result of movement of the source in the ICM as in that case we would see a WAT or head-tail source, depending on orientation. This strong resemblance to an X-shaped source might be due to a restarted AGN phase due to jet interruption or axis re-orientation, rather than a projection effect.
10909 presents to strong emission peaks at VLBA separated by 0.05 arcsec. These lie on top of the fringes in the image, thus we refrain from making a strong statement about a double core.

gmw_00011823.overview: The galaxy 10933 is an active galactic nucleus that hosts a symmetric, Z-shaped radio structure close to its nucleus [arXiv:1901.10168]. This double-double radio morphology displays a rotation of the jet and lobe structure, which likely results from an axis reorientation [arXiv:1901.10168].

sources.summary: The paper describes 10933 as a restarted Z-shaped radio AGN whose symmetric jet/lobe rotation likely reflects axis reorientation.

quotes.quote: 10933 displays a rotation of the jet/lobe structure close to the centre of emission (marked by the black cross in Fig. fig:xshaped ). This does not look like a projection effect as the two jet/lobe structures look alike and symmetric. Thus the radio structure is most probably a result of axis reorientation.

A striking example in the multi-component sources is 10933, where we see this Z-shaped symmetric radio structure close to the nucleus (see Figs. fig:xshaped fig:maps2 ). To identify possible signatures of binary black-hole systems that give rise to this double-double radio structure, cross-matched these sources with the 1.4-GHz VLBA data available for the COSMOS field noelia17 . We find 5 matches between X-shaped 3-GHz objects and VLBA data: 10909, 10914, 10918, 10919, 10933 and 10958.

gmw_00011824.overview: 10944 is a face-on star-forming galaxy at a redshift of 0.07905 that shows no evidence of an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1901.10168]. It is classified as a polar-ring galaxy, likely resulting from a galaxy merger, and features a radio synchrotron ring that follows its spiral arms [arXiv:1901.10168].

sources.summary: The paper presents 10944 as a face-on star-forming radio ring/polar-ring galaxy in COSMOS, with no AGN evidence and ring-dominated radio emission.

quotes.quote: The most striking object in this sub-sample is 10944. This is the only object in COSMOS that shows a well resolved radio ring and a compact core radio emission. Nevertheless, it is not associated with an AGN, based on multi-wavelength data.

fig:10944 ), 10944 is a star-forming radio ring with a compact radio core in the centre, associated with the nucleus of the galaxy. This ring-like radio synchrotron emission follows well the spiral arms of this face-on galaxy, as seen in the overlay image of radio and HST i-band image koekemoer07 . From the HST image we classify this particular galaxy as a polar-ring galaxy e.g. moiseev11 , because it exhibits an elongation along in the NW-SE direction, almost perpendicular to the disk. Polar-ring galaxies are the result of galaxy mergers, and this would indicate that 10944 is the result of a galaxy merger.

The host galaxy is a SF galaxy at low redshift, z_ spec = 0.07905, with no signs of AGN at any observed wavelengths, and it does not display any radio excess over the star-formation-rate. Our multi-wavelength AGN diagnostics do not show any evidence for the existence of an AGN. It is not a mid-IR AGN nor an X-ray AGN and is classified as a SFG smolcic17b . Only 4 of the flux is located in the "core", while the rest is in the ring structure.

gmw_00011825.overview: The galaxy 10965 is classified as a star-forming galaxy [arXiv:1901.10168]. Its 3 GHz radio emission displays a twisted blob that follows a spiral arm, which can cause it to be mistaken for a bent-tail active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1901.10168].

sources.summary: The paper classifies 10965 as a star-forming galaxy whose twisted 3 GHz emission follows a spiral arm and could be mistaken for a bent-tail AGN.

quotes.quote: Furthermore, 10965 is quite an interesting object when it comes to disentangling radio AGN from SFGs. Classifying this object solely by looking at its radio map can be misleading. Depending on one's point of view, this can look either as an AGN where the jets are bent due to interaction with the environment, or as a disk of a star-forming galaxy.

As we have demonstrated with the example of 10965 (see Fig. fig:maps2 ), classifying AGN and SFGs solely by their radio images can lead to mis-classifications. 10965 resembles a bent-tail radio AGN at 3 GHz, when in fact what we observe is emission from the face-on disk of the SFG.

10965 displays a large twisted blob at 3 GHz, which follows the spiral arm of the galaxy shown in infrared in Fig. fig:sfgs , and also has several smaller blobs associated with the galaxy disk. This is why we classify it as SFG.

gmw_00011826.name: IRAS 10026+4347
gmw_00011826.overview: IRAS 10026+4347 is an ultraluminous infrared galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/9811171] and a narrow-line quasar located at a redshift of z=0.179 [arXiv:astro-ph/9811171]. The galaxy exhibits strong iron emission [arXiv:astro-ph/9811171] and displays soft X-ray variability on both short and long timescales [arXiv:astro-ph/9811171].

sources.summary: The paper studies IRAS 10026+4347 as an ultraluminous narrow-line QSO with strong FeII emission, high infrared luminosity, redshift z=0.179, and variable soft X-rays.

quotes.quote: In this paper, we study the ultraluminous IRAS galaxy 10026+4347, a narrow-line QSO selected from the QDOT redshift survey. We show that this source is an unusual object: it has some common characteristics with NLS1s, such as a very soft X-ray spectrum, strong FeII emissions and rapid time variabilities. On the other hand, it has a larger FWHM ( 2500 ) in the H line than most NLS1s. The optical and X-ray properties of this source are presented in 2 and 3. In section 4, we discuss the nature of this object. Throughout this paper we use a Hubble constant of H_0=50 Mpc ^ 1 and _0=1 . IRAS 10026+4347 (RA=10:05:43.5, DEC=43:32:33.2 in J2000) is a narrow-line QSO discovered in the QDOT redshift survey with infrared luminosity L_ IR = 1.77 10^ 12 L_ (Rowan-Robinson et al. 1990). This galaxy has a redshift of z=0.179 (as determined from our spectra shown in Fig.

Therefore, IRAS 10026+4347 qualifies as a very strong FeII emitter. As can be seen from Table 1, that there is no statistically significant line variability during the three observations. There are three data sets of ROSAT observation for IRAS 10026+4347 in the ROSAT archive: the source was first detected in the ROSAT All Sky Survey (RASS, Tr umper 1983; Bade et al. 1995) and later observed using ROSAT PSPC in May 1994 and ROSAT HRI in Nov. 1995. We have reduced the X-ray data using the EXSAS software at MPE (Zimmermann et al. 1992). Table 2 lists the exposure time, count rate, flux and the X-ray luminosity. Note that the flux and luminosity are model-dependent, and we have used a power-law fit in deriving these quantities (see below). From table 2 it is obvious that the source varied during the three observations: the count rate in RASS is about 8 times larger than the PSPC observation conducted after 4 years. We also performed time variability test using the RASS data. We bin the data in an interval of 4000s. Notice that the ROSAT wobbling is on the scale of 400 s, and so has no effect on our variability test. We find that the count rates exhibit about a factor of two variability within two days. An examination of the HRI data indicates similar behaviors. So IRAS 10026+4347 is a variable object in the soft X-ray on both short and long time scales.

gmw_00011827.name: KUG 0214-057
gmw_00011827.overview: KUG 0214-057 is a late-type spiral galaxy experiencing intense starburst activity [arXiv:astro-ph/0504355]. It shows signs of disruption in its outer spiral arms and may be interacting with a small companion galaxy to the southwest [arXiv:astro-ph/0504355].

sources.summary: The paper describes KUG 0214-057 as a barred late-type spiral with strong recent star formation, possible interaction signatures, and up to four luminous ULX candidates spread through its spiral arms.

quotes.quote: We report the serendipitous discovery of several unresolved X-ray sources lying in the prominent spiral arms of the galaxy KUG 0214-057 in XMM-Newton observations. The location of these X-ray sources strongly suggests that at least three, and possibly four, of these may be physically related to the galaxy. The luminosity of each of these sources at the distance of KUG 0214-057 is > 5 10^ 39 \ erg \ s^ 1 (0.3-10 keV), making each a strong candidate ultraluminous X-ray source (ULX). Using the ULX objects as a metric implies that this relatively low-mass galaxy may be experiencing rather intense starburst activity.

From its optical appearance, KUG 0214-057 is clearly a late-type galaxy with very significant recent star formation. Its inclusion in the KUG catalogue of UV-excess galaxies reinforces this view as UV-excess is most commonly an indicator of relatively strong star formation. There is some indication of disruption to the outer spiral arms. It is also interesting to note the small companion galaxy ( APMUKS(BJ) B021449.37-054240.9 ) 1.5 arcmin. to the SW which may be interacting with KUG 0214-057 , and the very faint nebulosity lying a similar distance to the SE (see Fig. 1c).

gmw_00011828.name: G^*
aliases_external: G* / putative host galaxy of GRB 060502B
G*, putative early-type host galaxy of GRB 060502B
gmw_00011828.overview: The galaxy G^* is a bright early-type galaxy [arXiv:1910.14505, arXiv:2310.19318] that has been classified as a giant elliptical [arXiv:1110.4209], though imaging reveals a disk component with spiral arms [arXiv:1910.14505]. Located at a redshift of z = 0.287 [arXiv:1910.14505, arXiv:2310.19318], it is the putative host of the gamma-ray burst GRB 060502B [arXiv:1110.4209, arXiv:2310.19318].

sources.summary: The paper treats the putative GRB 060502B host as a giant elliptical whose modeled offset distribution cannot explain the burst at three-sigma confidence, and considers misidentification or host evolution as explanations.

quotes.quote: The possibility of a burst occurring at such a large offset around this host galaxy is excluded at the three-sigma level, although the errors on the burst position are rather large as no optical afterglow was detected. There are several possible explanations for the failure of the field binary scenario to reproduce the large offset of GRB 060502B. Firstly, it is possible that the host galaxy has been mis-identified. We follow bloom2007 in selecting the giant elliptical considered here as the host with a large offset. The probability of a chance co-incidence with this galaxy is only a few per cent; on the other hand there are smaller, fainter galaxies within the XRT error circle bergerhiz .
The nearest elliptical galaxy of similar size to the putative host of GRB 060502B is M87, which has roughly 14 000 globular clusters harris09 in a very extended distribution.

sources.summary: The paper discusses G^* as the proposed bright host galaxy of GRB 060502B, noting its large offset, uncertain association, redshift, and lack of other nearby redshift-confirmed candidates.

quotes.quote: 2007ApJ...654..878B proposed that a bright galaxy (referred to as G^* ), situated south of the Swift/XRT localization of the GRB with an angular separation of 17.5 arcsec (approximately 73 kpc if assuming a redshift of z 0.287 ), is the host galaxy of GRB 060502B (chance probability of 0.03). 2011MNRAS.413.2004C noted that such a large offset is inconsistent with the scenario that GRB 060502B originates from a compact binary merger in the galaxy. They suggest that either the precursor of GRB 060502B was a binary neutron star system that formed within a globular cluster inside G^* , giving this binary a large initial kick, or G^* is not the actual host galaxy of GRB 060502B.

Except for the G^* , within 1 arcmin of the GRB 060502B position, we find no other possible host galaxy candidates for GRB 060502B with redshift measurements Here, we search for the candidates from the Extragalactic Database https://doi.org/10.26132/ned1 and Sloan Digital Sky Survey https://www.sdss.org/dr17/ https://www.sdss.org/dr17/ . . Furthermore, the results obtained from the relation between the spectral peak energy ( E_p ) and the isotropic gamma-ray energy ( E_ , iso ), namely the Amati relation 2002A&A...390...81A,2018ApJ...859..160W , indicate that when adopting a redshift of G^* ( z = 0.287 2009ApJ...703.1696Z ) the GRB 060502B well lies within the SGRB's Amati relation. Therefore, we also consider the bright galaxy G^* as the host galaxy for GRB 060502B.

sources.summary: The paper identifies the likely GRB 060502B host candidate as a z=0.287 bright early-type galaxy, but Gemini images reveal a disk with spiral arms and modest emission-line star formation.

quotes.quote: According to these authors, the most likely host-galaxy candidate is a relatively bright early-type galaxy galaxy ( R 18.7 mag, z =0.287) 17.5 arcsec south of the XRT error circle (see their figure 1). However, publicly available Gemini-N/GMOS images (programme ID GN-2006A-Q-14 PI: B. Schmidt) taken in May 2006 clearly reveal a disk component with at least one spiral arm in both directions. Based on the emission line, calculated a SFR of 0.4--0.8 M _ yr ^ 1 .

gmw_00011829.name: J1420 0545
gmw_00011829.overview: J1420 0545 is a giant radio galaxy characterized by very narrow radio lobes [arXiv:1107.5449]. Its radio structure is relatively young, with a high expansion velocity that may be driven by restarted jet activity [arXiv:1107.5449].

sources.summary: The paper models J1420 0545 as a giant radio galaxy with narrow lobes, young age, high expansion speed, powerful jets, and an unusually low-density environment.

quotes.quote: The Case of J1420 0545 The high-resolution GMRT image of at 617 MHz confirms very narrow lobes of the source, as already suggested by the 1400 MHz map available from the FIRST survey. The axial ratio of the lobes, R_ T 12 , is of the same order or even bit higher than that characterizing the inner lobes in basically all the well-studied DDRGs, and much higher than that established for normal-size FR IIs, outer lobes of DDRGs, or GRGs. Also the age of the observed radio structure of found from the preliminary model fitting presented by mach08 , namely 50 Myr, as well as the average expansion speed evaluated by the authors, 0.1 , are totally discordant with the corresponding values determined for the other GRGs. Therefore, the idea that the observed structure of might be due to a restarted rather than a primary jet activity seems well justified.

Both ``Primary'' and ``Inner'' models return comparable physical parameters of the studied object --- in particular Q_ j 4 10^ 45 erg s ^ 1 , _ inj 0.55 , t 35 Myr, v_ h (t)/ D/(2 t) 0.2 , p_ (0.1-0.4) 10^ 13 dyn cm ^ 2 , and B 0.6 G --- except of the central density of the ambient medium _ 0 . We note here relatively young age of the source implied by the model fits (in between of the ages evaluated for the inner and outer lobes of the analyzed DDRGs), its relatively high expansion velocity (ten times higher that the corresponding values found for the outer lobes in the control sample of DDRGs), and the magnetic field intensity exactly within the range established for other GRGs through the X-ray observations kon09,iso09,iso11a,iso11b .

gmw_00011830.name: S5 0716+714
gmw_00011830.overview: S5 0716+714 is a highly variable, bright BL Lacertae object and blazar located at a redshift of 0.31 [arXiv:1507.06473]. It is classified as an intermediate energy peaked BL Lac object and has exhibited multi-wavelength outbursts accompanied by rapid polarization variations [arXiv:1507.06473].

sources.summary: The paper studies S5 0716+714 as a bright BL Lac/blazar, modeling its 2015 multi-wavelength outburst, polarization swings, and likely magnetic reconnection in the jet.

quotes.quote: The blazar , at a red-shift, z of 0.31 Nilsson2008 is one of the brightest BL Lac objects that is highly variable from radio to rays with a very high duty cycle WagnerWitzel1996 . In the SED based classification scheme, is sub-classified as an intermediate energy peaked BL Lac object (IBL) Giommi1999 , and is also confirmed based on the concave shape of its X-ray (0.1-10.0 keV) spectrum Foschini2006, Ferrero2006 . The concave shape of the spectrum in X-rays is indicative of the presence of a tail from the synchrotron emission (falling tail) and a flatter part from the inverse Compton (IC) spectrum (rising tail). This object has been detected in the MeV energy range several times at different flux levels by the Energetic Gamma Ray Experiment Telescope (EGRET) detector on board the Compton Gamma-ray Observatory ( CGRO ) Hartman1999,Nandikotkur2007 .

exhibited a multi-wavelength outburst in January 2015, with two well-resolved sub-flares in gamma-rays and optical. The event was accompanied by rapid PD variations and a systematic PA swing, and followed by a variable VHE detection ( F_ 150 GeV = 4-7 10^ 11 ph cm^ 2 s^ 1 ) by the MAGIC group.

gmw_00011831.name: A0140-1152
gmw_00011831.overview: A0140-1152 is a gravitationally lensed double quasar system with a source redshift of 1.806 [arXiv:1711.07492]. The lensing deflector is a red galaxy located at a redshift of 0.277 [arXiv:1711.07492].

sources.summary: The paper confirms A0140-1152 as a new quasar lens with a z=0.277 deflector galaxy, nearly identical quasar spectra, measured flux ratios, and simple lens models.

quotes.quote: A0140-1152, a new quasar lens A0140 was observed at the NTT and Magellan independently (fig.  fig:A0140 ), having been respectively targeted in the ATLAS-DR3 catalog and separately found via cutout modelling in the whole footprint. Both data-sets revealed z_ s =1.806 0.001 nearly-identical quasar spectra, plus a red `excess' between the two traces that the Magellan spectra and acquisition images confirmed to be a galaxy at z_ l =0.277 with prominent Ca (G,H,K) absorption features. The flux-ratio between the two quasar images, as measured on the continua, is f_ =1.05 between C lines on the blue side and f_ =1.12 on the red side; the NTT spectra gave f_ =1.2 between the C  iii /Fe complex and Mg  ii . The small discrepancy may be ascribed to lower S/N, atmospheric dispersion corrections and slight slit misalignment.

For this system, we also run simple lens models. From the IMACS i- band acquisition image, aligned with the slit (fig.  fig:A0140 ), the relative positions and magnitudes of the quasar images and deflector have been obtained, as listed in table  tab:astromA0140 . The flux ratio between images A and B varies depending on whether it is measured on the continua or on (continuum-subtracted) emission lines, or on the broad-band IMACS image.

gmw_00011832.overview: The object 1117+146 is a gigahertz-peaked spectrum radio galaxy located at a redshift of z = 0.362 [arXiv:astro-ph/0701619]. Radio observations of the galaxy reveal a 100 milliarcsecond double radio structure, while optical imaging shows an unresolved near-ultraviolet source [arXiv:astro-ph/0701619].

sources.summary: The paper identifies 1117+146 as a GPS radio galaxy at z = 0.362, notes its double radio structure, and reports an unresolved near-UV source in the HST image.

quotes.quote: 1117+146 : Identified as a GPS by O'Dea91 . The counterpart of the radio source corresponds to a m _R =20.1 galaxy Vries95 at z = 0.362 Vries98 . Radio observations e.g., Fey97 show a 100 mas double radio source. Our image shows an unresolved 24.06 magnitude source.

gmw_00011833.name: GGL-B
gmw_00011833.overview: GGL-B is a late-type galaxy located at a redshift of z = 1.032 [arXiv:2202.04663]. One of its spiral arms is multiply imaged by a cluster member, allowing the system to serve as a mass constraint in lens models [arXiv:2202.04663].

sources.summary: The paper describes GGL-B as a late-type z=1.032 galaxy whose spiral arm is multiply imaged and used as a mass constraint in A370 lens models.

quotes.quote: Nevertheless, there are two previously-identified GGLs contained within the cluster multiple-image region. The first (GGL-A) is a singly-imaged but highly distorted ring-like galaxy at z = 1.062 , first identified by sou99 . The second (GGL-B) is a late-type galaxy at z = 1.032 , where a spiral arm is multiply-imaged by a cluster member. Due to its multiplicity, this system is used as a mass constraint in several lens models (e.g., System 42 in L19).

Mirroring an ongoing galaxy-galaxy lens (GGL) search in the BUFFALO fields, we perform a follow-up search in the spectroscopic data. Of the five identified GGL candidates in the A370 HST imaging, four are at least partially contained within the MUSE footprint. We easily identify lens redshifts for all four candidates (all confirmed cluster members) and source redshifts for two: GGL-A ( z_s = 1.062) and GGL-B ( z_s = 1.032); see Fig.

gmw_00011834.name: J0812+2007
gmw_00011834.overview: J0812+2007 is a system composed of an active galactic nucleus and a companion object located 0.66 arcseconds away [arXiv:2305.07396]. This companion is characterized by a featureless continuum and is most probably a star [arXiv:2305.07396].

sources.summary: The paper classifies J0812+2007 as an AGN/star system: Keck detects an AGN component plus a featureless likely stellar companion, consistent with integrated spectra.

quotes.quote: Six out of seven targets are 'isolated', i.e., have no companion in the Gaia catalog, while J0812+2007 has a known companion 0.66 apart.

J0812+2007 is constituted by an AGN, revealed by the broad \ line, and a second object characterized by a featureless continuum. The most probable interpretation for the latter is a star, which is expected in about 30 of the systems Mannucci22 .

It appears that there is an excellent agreement between all available classifications: J0812--0040 and J1510+5959 are classified as AGN/star associations by the available methods, i.e, colors and integrated spectra; J0812+2007 is an AGN/star systems based on the Keck spectrum, in agreement with the integrated spectrum. The large error on its H-K_s color does not allow us to obtain a definite classification from the colors, which nevertheless are compatible with the presence of a star.

gmw_00011835.name: J0950+4329
gmw_00011835.overview: J0950+4329 is a highly luminous active galactic nucleus at a redshift of z=1.77 [arXiv:2305.07396]. It is classified as a gravitationally lensed quasar with two components that exhibit virtually identical spectra [arXiv:2305.07396].

sources.summary: The paper classifies J0950+4329 as a lensed QSO, supported by QSO-like colors, very similar resolved spectra, high luminosity, and inferred lensing magnification.

quotes.quote: Both components of J0950+4329 (system 5) have colors that are only compatible with QSOs, and therefore this is either a dual or a lensed AGN. This is a very bright object at z=1.77 , at the upper luminosity envelope of the bright QSOs at this distance in , and several magnitudes brighter than the typical COSMOS QSOs at that redshift Civano16 . For this reason, J0950+4329 is most likely a lensed AGN, whose luminosity is boosted by lensing magnification. The slightly different observed H-K_s colors of the two components could be due to different amounts of extinction in the lensing galaxy.

Both components of J0950+4320 show QSO spectra, characterized by broad \ and \ and bright [ OIII ] 4959,5007 lines. The two spectra become virtually identical by applying a small correction for dust extinction to component A for A_V=0.4 , and a normalization factor of 2.65. The similarity of the spectra and the luminosity of the object (see Fig. fig:NIRcolors ) suggest that this is a lensed QSO, with the different dust extinction produced by the different light paths for the two images. The lensing galaxy is not detected but this is compatible with the relatively low sensitivity of our spectra to extended and continuum-dominated objects close to two bright point sources.

gmw_00011836.name: J1048+4541
gmw_00011836.overview: J1048+4541 is a dual active galactic nucleus consisting of two distinct components [arXiv:2305.07396]. Both components exhibit bright, very broad emission lines with significantly different line widths and central wavelengths [arXiv:2305.07396].

sources.summary: The paper classifies J1048+4541 as a dual AGN: both components show broad lines, but their line centers and widths differ enough to reject lensing.

quotes.quote: They show that: J0732+3533, J0950+4329, J1048+4541, and J1103+2348 can be fitted well by a single AGN spectrum and do not show any stellar feature.

Both J1048+4541 and J1103+2348 are constituted by two components each, and all the spectra show the presence of bright, very broad \ lines. The line profiles in each pair are very different, both in center and shapes. To quantify the difference, we fit a Gaussian line and a constant continuum to each broad emission line. The formal errors on both line center and FWHM are small, a few km/sec, but the real uncertainties, due to the non-Gaussian profiles of the line and the partial coverage of some of the lines, are larger. For this reason we estimate typical uncertainties of 100-200 km/sec on the centers and 200-500 km/sec on the FWHM.

The \ central wavelengths of the two components of J1048+4541 differ by 500 300 km/s, and the two lines have very different line widths, i.e. FWHM = 2500 200 km/s for component A and FWHM=5300 500 km/s for component B. The significance of the velocity offset is hampered by the lack of most of the blue part of the line of component B, while the difference in line width is highly significant.

gmw_00011837.name: J1103+2348
gmw_00011837.overview: J1103+2348 is a dual active galactic nucleus that can be fitted well by a single active galactic nucleus spectrum [arXiv:2305.07396]. The system is constituted by two components with similar broad-line widths but offset central wavelengths and opposite line asymmetries [arXiv:2305.07396].

sources.summary: The paper classifies J1103+2348 as a dual AGN, with two broad-line components whose velocity offset and opposite line asymmetries are incompatible with lensed images.

quotes.quote: They show that: J0732+3533, J0950+4329, J1048+4541, and J1103+2348 can be fitted well by a single AGN spectrum and do not show any stellar feature.

Both J1048+4541 and J1103+2348 are constituted by two components each, and all the spectra show the presence of bright, very broad \ lines. The line profiles in each pair are very different, both in center and shapes. To quantify the difference, we fit a Gaussian line and a constant continuum to each broad emission line. The formal errors on both line center and FWHM are small, a few km/sec, but the real uncertainties, due to the non-Gaussian profiles of the line and the partial coverage of some of the lines, are larger. For this reason we estimate typical uncertainties of 100-200 km/sec on the centers and 200-500 km/sec on the FWHM.

The two components of J1103+2348 have similar line widths (FWHM= 2950 400 km/s in both cases) but the central wavelengths are offset by 430 200 km/s. In addition, the line shapes are very different, with opposite asymmetries. These differences are not compatible with two lensed images of one variable source but with different delays: indeed, in both cases the expected time delay between the two components is a few days at most, while the luminosities of the objects imply BLR sizes of hundreds of days Bentz13 .

gmw_00011838.name: J1318-0136
gmw_00011838.overview: J1318-0136 is an active galactic nucleus aligned with a foreground star [arXiv:2305.07396]. High-resolution imaging resolves these two close components, detecting bright hydrogen emission only in the active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2305.07396].

sources.summary: The paper classifies J1318-0136 as an AGN/star alignment, with ERIS resolving two close components and detecting AGN H emission only in the brightest one.

quotes.quote: J1318-0136 has a low-S/N, uncalibrated spectrum from 2QZ survey Croom04 , with problematic features. Our estimate of the redshift is based on the presence of a line at 3850 , identified as CIV ] 1549 based on the presence of two low-significance features at 4800 and 6950 , possibly identified as CIII and MgII. Even if our estimate is in agreement with that originally provided by 2QZ, the redshift determination is not certain. Also, the quality of the spectrum does not allow us to look for stellar features, therefore this total spectrum does not provide any classification.

In Fig. fig:J1318-0136 , we show an image of the system, showing the presence of two, well-separated components at 0.27 of separation, and the spectra of the two sources extracted using circular apertures of 200 mas-diameter, after removing a second-pass background in each spectral plane of the cube. The bright H emission line centered at 1.6375 m corresponds to redshift z=1.495 and is in good agreement with the tabulated value of z=1.486 obtained from rest-frame UV lines, clearly revealing the AGN nature of the brightest component A. On the contrary, component B shows no emission lines and a few absorption features, revealing its nature as a foreground star.

gmw_00011839.name: SDSS J1106+1939
gmw_00011839.overview: SDSS J1106+1939 is a broad-absorption line quasar that hosts a supermassive black hole [arXiv:1304.1691]. This black hole has an estimated mass of 1.3 to 1.5 billion solar masses and drives a galaxy wind outflow [arXiv:1304.1691].

sources.summary: The paper presents SDSS J1106+1939 as a powerful BAL QSO whose observed outflow velocity, kinetic luminosity, Eddington accretion rate, and black-hole mass match the authors' wind model.

quotes.quote: A recently discovered powerful BAL QSO outflow in SDSS J1106+1939 Borguet2012arXiv has a mass flow rate of 400 yr ^ 1 and velocity v 8000 km s ^ 1 . From equation ( eq:vwind ) we find that this velocity corresponds to m = 14 , while eq. ( eq:lkin ) gives L_ kin = 0.05 L_ Edd for this system, exactly as observed. The Eddington ratio also implies an Eddington accretion rate of 29 yr ^ 1 , giving an SMBH mass M_ SMBH = 1.3 10^9 , almost exactly equal to the 1.5 10^9 SMBH mass calculated from the observed kinetic luminosity.
Our picture correctly predicts the velocities and ionization states of the observed winds, including the recently-discovered case of SDSS J1106+1939.

gmw_00011840.name: OGC 0139
gmw_00011840.overview: OGC 0139 is classified as one of the largest and most massive super spiral galaxies [arXiv:1909.09080]. It possesses a maximum baryonic mass of 6.3 billion solar masses and features an outer rotation curve that continues to rise [arXiv:1909.09080].

sources.summary: The paper describes OGC 0139 as one of the largest super spirals, with a still-rising outer rotation curve, uncertain maximum velocity from edge blobs, and the sample’s maximum baryonic mass.

quotes.quote: The rotation curves of the two largest, most massive galaxies (OGC 0139 and 2MFGC 12344) continue to rise at the outer edge. This may lead to an underestimate of the maximum circular velocity. The galaxy OGC 0139 also has a high uncertainty of 90 km s ^ 1 in v_ max because of the large velocity dispersion in two blobs at either edge of its rotation curve. Including our sample and the super spiral sample of , we find a maximum baryonic mass of M_ b,max = 6.3 10^ 11 M_ ( M_ b,max / M_ = 11.8 ) for super spiral OGC 0139, which is slightly more massive than the previous record-holder, ISOHDFS: [ RFA2002 ] S27 rfa02 .

gmw_00011841.name: PKS 2215+020
gmw_00011841.overview: PKS 2215+020 is a high-redshift flat-spectrum radio quasar [arXiv:2402.10722] located at a redshift of approximately 3.57 to 3.59 [arXiv:2402.10722, arXiv:1202.2193]. The source features an unusually extended parsec-scale radio jet [arXiv:1202.2193] that exhibits a change in direction from east to northeast [arXiv:1202.2193].

sources.summary: The paper analyzes PKS 2215+020, a high-redshift flat-spectrum radio quasar, using multi-epoch VLBI to characterize its radio jet and component proper motions.

quotes.quote: The subject of this paper, PKS 2215+020 (J2217+0220), is a powerful flat-spectrum radio quasar at z=3.572 . A more recent redshift determination from the Sloan Digital Sky Survey is z=3.5909 , the optical spectrum with emission lines identified, can be seen at https://skyserver.sdss.org/dr17/en/get/SpecById.ashx?id=10322413208997222400 (accessed on 2 February 2024). Quasi-simultaneous multi-colour optical and near-infrared photometric measurements of PKS 2215+020 were reported in . The coordinates of this quasar in the International Celestial Reference Frame right ascension are RA = 22^ h 17^ min (48.2379348 0.0000051)^ s and declination Dec = 02^ 20^ (10.71185 0.00011)^ . Its radio spectral index is _ 2.7 GHz ^ 5 GHz =-0.15 (following convention S ^ , where S is the flux density and the observing frequency), and its rest-frame monochromatic power at 5 GHz is P_ 5 GHz 2 10^ 28 W Hz ^ 1 .

To reveal the physical and geometric properties of the radio jet in PKS 2215+020, here, we present an analysis of multi-epoch VLBI imaging data taken at multiple frequencies. We use archival data from observations spanning more than 25 years, from 1995 to 2020. While this quasar has been observed with VLBI earlier , this is the first study of its jet component proper motions, allowing us determination of the bulk Lorentz factor and the inclination angle of the inner sub-mas-scale section of the jet and revelation of the possibly stationary nature of the distant ( 60 mas) extended radio feature.
