We note that, on the basis of the EW of the emission lines, this object seems to be of intermediate nature between a BL Lac and a polarized QSO, as already suggested also by veron94 .

gmw_00011957.name: PKS 0338-214
gmw_00011957.overview: PKS 0338-214 is a BL Lacertae object and active galactic nucleus [arXiv:astro-ph/0410692]. It has a redshift of z=0.223, and its host galaxy has an absolute magnitude of M_R=-21.7 [arXiv:astro-ph/0410692].

sources.summary: The paper rejects earlier tentative features, uses host detection and VLT emission lines to set z=0.223, and derives an absolute host magnitude of M_R=-21.7.

quotes.quote: PKS 0338-214 wright77 reported a tentative redshift estimate of z=0.048, based on the detection of H _ , Mg H and H _ . These features were not confirmed by falomo94 and falomo00 . Based on the host galaxy detection (m _ R =18.86), falomo00 proposed a photometric redshift of 0.45. Our VLT spectrum clearly shows the [ OII ] 3727 \ and [ OIII ] 5007 emission lines at z=0.223. At this redshift the host galaxy has an absolute magnitude M _ R =-21.7.

gmw_00011958.name: 1RXS J055806.6 383829
gmw_00011958.overview: 1RXS J055806.6 383829 is a BL Lacertae object and active galactic nucleus located at a redshift of z=0.302 [arXiv:astro-ph/0410692]. Its optical spectrum exhibits host-galaxy absorption features, including CaII, G band, and MgI lines [arXiv:astro-ph/0410692].

sources.summary: The paper measures host-galaxy absorption features at z=0.302 for 1RXS J055806.6 383829 and finds a flatter spectral index than a previous observation.

quotes.quote: 1RXS J055806.6 383829 The observations of this source performed by giommi89 gave a featureless optical spectrum. Our spectrum clearly shows the host galaxy spectral features (CaII 3934, 3968 , G band 4305 \ and MgI 5175 \ absorption lines) at z=0.302. We see also several absorption features from the ISM of our galaxy: DIB at 4726 \ and 5772 , and the NaI 5892 \ absorption line. The spectral index found by giommi89 is = 1.8, steeper than our result of 1.2.

gmw_00011959.name: PKS 0808+019
gmw_00011959.overview: PKS 0808+019 is a BL Lacertae object and active galactic nucleus [arXiv:astro-ph/0410692]. It is located at a redshift of z=1.148, determined from the detection of CIII] and MgII emission lines [arXiv:astro-ph/0410692].

sources.summary: The paper replaces a tentative z=0.93 estimate with a higher-S/N VLT redshift of z=1.148 from CIII and MgII emission lines.

quotes.quote: PKS 0808+019 The spectrum obtained by veron03 shows no spectral features. A tentative redshift z=0.93 based on a possible detection of MgII 2798 \ was proposed by jackson02 see also baldwin81, strittmatter74 . In our higher S/N spectrum we detect CIII ] 1909 \ and MgII 2798 \ emission lines at z=1.148. CaII 3934, 3968 , NaI 5892 \ and the DIB at 5772 \ absorption features originated by our galaxy ISM are also observed.

gmw_00011960.name: 1WGAJ1012.2+063
gmw_00011960.overview: 1WGAJ1012.2+063 is a BL Lacertae object and active galactic nucleus located at a redshift of z=0.727 [arXiv:astro-ph/0410692]. Its optical spectrum exhibits faint MgII and [OII] emission lines, as well as an intervening MgII absorption line at z=0.518 [arXiv:astro-ph/0410692].

sources.summary: The VLT spectrum detects MgII and OII emission lines at z=0.727 for 1WGAJ1012.2+063, plus an intervening MgII absorber at z=0.518.

quotes.quote: 1WGAJ1012.2+063 The optical spectrum obtained by wolter97 failed to detect any feature in this source. Our higher S/N spectrum clearly shows two faint emission lines that we identify with MgII 2798 \ and [ OII ] 3727 \ at z=0.727. We found also an intervening absorption line at 4246 \ which we identify as MgII 2798 \ at z=0.518. This spectrum presents also several absorption features from our galaxy ISM: we observe DIB at 4726 and 5772 , along with CaII 3934, 3968 \ and NaI 5892 \ atomic lines.

gmw_00011961.name: PKS 1250-330
gmw_00011961.overview: PKS 1250-330 is an active galactic nucleus located at a redshift of z=0.856 [arXiv:astro-ph/0410692]. Spectroscopic observations of the source confirm a broad MgII emission line, suggesting an intermediate classification between a BL Lac object and a quasar [arXiv:astro-ph/0410692].

sources.summary: The paper confirms a broad MgII line at z=0.856 for PKS 1250-330 and says its equivalent width suggests an intermediate BL Lac/QSO classification.

quotes.quote: PKS 1250-330 Previous spectroscopic observations of this source by hook03 suggested the presence of a weak emission line at 5202 . Our spectroscopy confirms with high confidence this broad emission line which we identify with MgII 2798 at z=0.856. As in the case of PKS 0306+102, the EW of the emission line suggests an intermediate classification between a BL Lac and a QSO.

gmw_00011962.name: PKS 1256-229
gmw_00011962.overview: PKS 1256-229 is a BL Lacertae object and active galactic nucleus [arXiv:astro-ph/0410692]. It has a redshift of 0.481, determined from narrow oxygen emission lines, and its continuum shape suggests the presence of a calcium II break [arXiv:astro-ph/0410692].

sources.summary: The paper sets PKS 1256-229 at z=0.481 from OII and OIII lines, contradicting a prior z=1.365 claim and noting possible CaII-break continuum shape.

quotes.quote: PKS 1256-229 We detect [ OII ] 3727 and [ OIII ] 5007 \ narrow emission lines at z=0.481. This contrasts with the claim of z=1.365 proposed by drinkwater97 without any specification of the observed lines. Our visual inspection of the spectrum of drinkwater97 , published on a micro-fiche, does not reveal any significant spectral feature. The continuum shape around 3900 \ suggests the presence of the CaII break.

gmw_00011963.name: IC 5092
gmw_00011963.overview: IC 5092 is a nearby spiral galaxy classified as an SBc type [arXiv:2010.09764]. It hosted the supernova SN 2018cuf, and its distance has been estimated at approximately 32.0 Mpc using the Tully-Fisher relation [arXiv:2010.09764].

sources.summary: The paper identifies IC 5092 as the nearby SBc host of SN 2018cuf, uses MUSE data to study the SN environment, and discusses host distance uncertainty.

quotes.quote: SN 2018cuf was discovered at RA(2000) = 21 ^h 16 ^m 11 ^s .58, Dec(2000) =-64 28 57 30 in the nearby SBc galaxy IC 5092 (see Figure fig:sn_image ) on 2018 June 23 ( Valenti2018 , JD 2458292.86093, = 17.4), during the course of the DLT40 SN search Tartaglia2018 , utilizing the 0.4-m PROMPT5 telescope Reichart2005 at the Cerro Tololo Inter-American Observatory (CTIO).

Host Properties Multi Unit Spectroscopic Explorer (MUSE) Bacon2010 integral field unit (IFU) observations of IC 5092 were taken on 2019 April 12, as a part of the All-weather MUse Supernova Integral-field Nearby Galaxies (AMUSING; Galbany2016 ) survey. MUSE is mounted to the 8.2m Yepun UT4 Very Large Telescope, with a field-of-view of 1 1 and 0.2 0.2 spatial elements, small enough to sample the PSF. See Figure fig:sn_image for an outline of the MUSE footprint. The spectral coverage is from 4750 to 9300 , with a spectral resolution that ranges from R 3500 in the blue end, 1700 in the red end. Four 580s exposures (2320s total exposure time), rotating 90 deg between frames, were taken centered on the South-West side of the galaxy, which covered the SN position and its environment. We extracted a 3.6 aperture spectrum centered at the SN position (corresponding to a 800 pc diameter) to study the properties of the environment.

The distance to IC 5092 is not well constrained since it has only been measured using the Tully-Fisher relation Mathewson1992,Willick1997 to be 32.0 5.8 Mpc. While the Tully-Fisher relation can be used to measure distances to most spiral galaxies, the intrinsic scatter hinders the accuracy of the measurement for a single galaxy Czerny2018 .

gmw_00011964.name: IC 5285
gmw_00011964.overview: IC 5285 is a luminous S0/a galaxy that exhibits a double-ringed structure in the continuum, featuring an outer ring completely detached from its main disk [arXiv:1907.07261]. In emission, it reveals a pair of gaseous spirals, non-circular central gas motions, and a gaseous disk that corotates with its stellar component [arXiv:1907.07261].

sources.summary: The paper describes IC 5285 as a luminous S0/a with continuum rings, gaseous spirals in emission, non-circular central gas motions, and a gaseous disk corotating with the stars.

quotes.quote: IC 5285. This luminous S0/a galaxy looks a double-ringed one in continuum, with the outer ring being completely detached from the main disk. Interestingly, being mapped in the emission line , instead of the outer ring, it reveals a pair of gaseous spirals started from the tips of the inner ring (Fig. fig_fpi ). Perhaps, the inner ring borders a triaxial bulge because the major axis of the inner ring is turned with respect to the kinematical major axis; evident non-circular gas motions are seen in the center of the galaxy. In general, the gaseous disk corotates the stellar component (Fig. fig_ls ).

gmw_00011965.name: NGC 774
gmw_00011965.overview: NGC 774 is a lenticular galaxy that features a ultraviolet ring [arXiv:1907.07261]. This ring exhibits active star formation, as evidenced by a recent core-collapse supernova, while the galaxy's ionized gas is asymmetrically distributed and primarily excited by shock waves [arXiv:1907.07261].

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 774 as an S0 with a UV ring, recent core-collapse supernova evidence for star formation, asymmetric ionized gas, and mostly shock-dominated gas excitation.

quotes.quote: NGC 774. It is another S0 galaxy with a UV-ring galex ; it must possess even current star formation because recently a core-collapse supernova is detected within the ring (SN2006ee, 13 to SE from the center). The ionized gas is asymmetrically distributed over the central part of the galaxy, within R<20^ , or R< 6 \ (Fig. fig_fpi ); beyond the borders of the gaseous disk the stellar rotation velocity curve falls visibly almost to zero (Fig. fig_ls ).
NGC 774 lies at the border between the HII-region-like excitation and DIG-like or shock-like excitation.
The UV-ring in NGC 774 though suffering current star formation (a SNII is detected recently in this galaxy) reveals the gas excitation mostly dominated by shock waves.

gmw_00011966.name: NGC 2697
gmw_00011966.overview: NGC 2697 is a low-luminosity S0 lenticular galaxy [arXiv:1907.07261]. It features a well-sampled ionized-gas velocity field that demonstrates regular circular gas rotation [arXiv:1907.07261].

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 2697 as a low-luminosity S0 with new gri photometry, long-slit kinematics, and a well-sampled ionized-gas velocity field showing regular circular gas rotation.

quotes.quote: NGC 2697. It is a rather low-luminosity S0 galaxy for which no SDSS data were found. We have made our own gri photometry which results are to be presented elsewhere saltrings ; in the same paper we analyze also the long-slit kinematics. Here we note only a well-sampled ionized-gas velocity field (Fig. fig_fpi ) demonstrating regular circular gas rotation.

gmw_00011967.name: NGC 3182
gmw_00011967.overview: NGC 3182 is a mostly isolated, unbarred lenticular galaxy [arXiv:2206.12263, arXiv:1907.07261]. It features a nuclear star-forming ring [arXiv:2206.12263, arXiv:1907.07261] and exhibits an unexpectedly large difference between its gas and stellar rotation velocities [arXiv:1907.07261].

sources.summary: NGC 3182 is a mostly isolated S0/Seyfert galaxy with no bar but a nuclear star-forming ring, where recent ring star formation may result from AGN positive feedback.

quotes.quote: NGC 3182 is a lenticular (S0) galaxy (SA()0+, But15 ) with stellar mass of 10^ 10.3 M _ , rather isolated, without any morphological signs of interaction recently. The intriguing feature of NGC 3182 is that it has a nuclear star-forming ring, despite the fact that it shows no evidence of a bar (Figure F11 ).

We have investigated the spatially resolved stellar populations, kinematics and ionized gas properties of NGC 3182 galaxy in the CALIFA survey using full spectral fitting and absorption line population indicators. The SFR and the sSFR estimated from H flux shows some enhancement in the nuclear ring of NGC 3182. From the stellar age and [ /Fe ] gradient from index measurements, we conclude that abrupt star formation activity has occurred recently at the ring in NGC 3182.

Such enhancement of gas metallicity may be a natural result of additional star formation from the in-situ gas, possibly triggered by an AGN feedback. Although there is no evidence of kinetic feedback by strong outflows in NGC 3182, a luminous AGN is known to cause symmetric outflows by radiation pressure. Therefore, as the most plausible scenario that can be deduced from our observational analyses, we conclude that the nuclear star-forming ring in NGC 3182 may be formed by a positive feedback from the AGN, through its radiation pressure.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 3182 as an unbarred S0 with a circumnuclear blue ring, unexpectedly different gas and stellar rotation velocities, and a suspected thick diffuse ionized-gas disk.

quotes.quote: NGC 3182. Though the galaxy surely lacks a bar, it demonstrates a circumnuclear blue (starforming?) ring with a radius of 5.5^ (0.8 ). Beyond the ring, different emission lines show different line-of-sight velocity profiles, the low-ionization [ NII ] 6583 being the less rapid, and as a whole the difference between the projected rotation velocities of the gas and stars is unexpectedly large for such a low-mass galaxy. We can suspect that this S0 galaxy may possess thick disk of diffuse ionized gas. Such thick gaseous disks are usually lag the fast equatorial-plane rotation of galactic stellar disks and demonstrate enhanced low-ionization emission lines [ NII ] and [ SII ] .

gmw_00011968.name: UGC 12840
gmw_00011968.overview: UGC 12840 is a giant, isolated, and gas-rich lenticular galaxy [arXiv:1907.07261]. It features a giant outer emission-line ring with extended star formation, where the ionized gas corotates with the stellar component [arXiv:1907.07261].

sources.summary: The paper presents UGC 12840 as a giant isolated gas-rich lenticular with extended star formation in a giant outer emission-line ring and ionized gas corotating with the stars.

quotes.quote: UGC 12840. This galaxy is a giant, isolated kig , gas-rich lenticular one. Just in this galaxy pogge_esk93 had found extended star formation in the outer ring. We also detect a giant ring, with the radius of some 12.5 , emitting in the emission line (Fig. fig_fpi ). Though the galaxy looks almost round, b/a>0.8 , the projection of the rotation velocity is large, about of 100 - and the ionized gas corotates strictly the stellar component. Some non-circular gas motions are possible in the very center of the galaxy.

gmw_00011969.name: potential host galaxy for PTF10iya
gmw_00011969.overview: The potential host galaxy for PTF10iya is an emission-line galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.224 [arXiv:1103.0779]. Its composite spectrum displays underlying stellar absorption features alongside narrow nebular and typical galaxy emission lines [arXiv:1103.0779].

sources.summary: The paper treats the potential host galaxy for PTF10iya as a z=0.224 emission-line galaxy aligned with the transient nucleus; its spectrum shows underlying stellar absorption plus narrow nebular and typical galaxy emission features.

quotes.quote: The software further noted the presence of a coincident catalogued extended source, \ (hereafter ; Figure fig:finder1 ), identifying this object as a potential host galaxy for PTF10iya.

On the other hand, in the NIRC2 image we measure a position for \ of = 14^ h 38^ m 40.980^ s , = +37^ 39 33 47 . This falls only 40 mas from the location of PTF10iya, fully consistent within the errors. This is clearly illustrated in the right panel of Figure fig:finder2 , where the centroid of \ (indicated with a plus sign) falls within the 99.7 confidence (350 mas adopting our conservative estimate) localisation of PTF10iya.

Host galaxy The July 8 spectrum of , after dereddening (see below), is plotted in Figure fig:host . The spectrum is clearly a composite, containing both absorption features from an underlying stellar population and a series of narrow nebular emission lines. In particular, we detect Ca II H K, as well as H , H , H , H , H , H , and H , all resolved in absorption. Likewise, typical galaxy emission features, including [ O II ] 3727, H , [ O III ] 5007, [ N II ] 6548, H , [ N II ] 6584, and [ S II ] 6716, 6731, are all clearly detected from .

gmw_00011970.name: PS1
gmw_00011970.overview: PS1 is a spectroscopically confirmed cluster member galaxy that lies near the red sequence [arXiv:1005.4692]. It hosts an active galactic nucleus detected as an X-ray point source and exhibits [O II] emission without broad lines [arXiv:1005.4692].

sources.summary: The paper identifies PS1 as one of three Chandra X-ray point sources with AGN-like spectra; PS1 is a spectroscopically confirmed cluster member with [O II] emission near the red sequence.

quotes.quote: The three sources are detected with only 23, 27 and 48 counts respectively, but despite this it is possible to fit their spectra with simple power law models including photoelectric absorption (WABS; using the XSPEC package, Arnaud_1996 ) in order to constrain their properties.

The spectral properties are consistent with the sources being AGN, with power law indices in the typical range for these objects. The fluxes of the sources span the range (1.0-1.3) 10^ 15 erg s ^ 1 cm ^ 2 in the (0.5--2.0) keV band. Sources PS1 and PS2 are spectroscopically confirmed cluster members which show 3727 [ O ii ] emission but not broad lines (see Section s_OpticalSpectroscopy ).

The cluster member X-ray sources (PS1 and PS2; Section s_XRayAnalysis ) are both within <2 of the red-sequence, whereas the high redshift, obscured QSO in the background of the cluster is considerably bluer than the red-sequence.

gmw_00011971.name: PS2
gmw_00011971.overview: PS2 is a spectroscopically confirmed cluster member galaxy that lies near the cluster's red sequence [arXiv:1005.4692]. It is identified as an active galactic nucleus host with detected X-ray emission and [O II] emission lines [arXiv:1005.4692].

sources.summary: The paper identifies PS2 as one of three Chandra X-ray point sources with AGN-like spectra; PS2 is a spectroscopically confirmed cluster member with [O II] emission near the red sequence.

quotes.quote: The three sources are detected with only 23, 27 and 48 counts respectively, but despite this it is possible to fit their spectra with simple power law models including photoelectric absorption (WABS; using the XSPEC package, Arnaud_1996 ) in order to constrain their properties.

The spectral properties are consistent with the sources being AGN, with power law indices in the typical range for these objects. The fluxes of the sources span the range (1.0-1.3) 10^ 15 erg s ^ 1 cm ^ 2 in the (0.5--2.0) keV band. Sources PS1 and PS2 are spectroscopically confirmed cluster members which show 3727 [ O ii ] emission but not broad lines (see Section s_OpticalSpectroscopy ).

The cluster member X-ray sources (PS1 and PS2; Section s_XRayAnalysis ) are both within <2 of the red-sequence, whereas the high redshift, obscured QSO in the background of the cluster is considerably bluer than the red-sequence.

gmw_00011972.name: PS3
gmw_00011972.overview: PS3 is an X-ray and 24-micron source with a photometric redshift of approximately 2.5 [arXiv:1005.4692]. Located behind a galaxy cluster, it is interpreted as a high-redshift obscured active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1005.4692].

sources.summary: The paper identifies PS3 as an X-ray/24 micron source without spectroscopic confirmation, places it at photometric redshift about 2.5, and interprets it as a high-redshift obscured AGN behind the cluster.

quotes.quote: Source PS3 is also a 24 source (see Section s_MIPSAnalysis ), but is not spectroscopically confirmed despite 8 hours of integration on this object being obtained at Gemini (see Section s_OpticalSpectroscopy , below). The object does have a low confidence photometric redshift from the analysis of Hilton_2009 placing the object in the background of the cluster at z_p 2.5 . Assuming these redshifts, we estimate luminosities for the X-ray point sources after removing the effects of intrinsic absorption within the host galaxies. We find that the sources have unabsorbed luminosities spanning the range L_ X(2-10 \ keV) (0.1-6) 10^ 44 erg s ^ 1 , typical of AGN rather than starburst galaxies.

However, one bright 24 source with flux density f_ 24 = 820 10 Jy (SSTU J221600.14-173836.8) is associated with the Chandra source PS3 (see Table tab1 ), which is located only 0.7 from the cluster center. As mentioned in Section s_jointXRay , this object is likely to be a high redshift ( z_p 2.5 ) obscured AGN behind the cluster.

gmw_00011973.name: Eye of Horus
gmw_00011973.overview: The Eye of Horus is a rare double-source-plane gravitational lens system [arXiv:1704.01585]. It is the only spectroscopically confirmed double-source-plane lens discovered within the Hyper Suprime-Cam (HSC) survey [arXiv:1704.01585].

sources.summary: The Eye of Horus is used as the HSC survey's spectroscopically confirmed double-source-plane lens, a rare system whose multi-source arcs test automatic lens classification.

quotes.quote: In fig:horus we show ' analysis on the double source plane lens the Eye of Horus. Double source plane lenses are very interesting objects because they offer more constraints with respect to typical lenses, and can be used for detailed studies of the mass distribution of the foreground galaxy (e.g. ), or to infer cosmological parameters Gav++08, C+A14, Sch14 . Since they are also very rare, it is extremely important that lens finding algorithms do recognize them as lenses. This can be challenging for automatic algorithms because the presence of arcs from more than one source complicate their morphology. Therefore, we made sure when designing \ that it could properly classify the Eye of Horus, the only spectroscopically confirmed double source plane lens in the HSC survey, as a lens.

gmw_00011974.name: HSCJ090613 + 032939
gmw_00011974.overview: HSCJ090613 + 032939 is a strong gravitational lens candidate [arXiv:1704.01585]. The system features a main arc that is relatively faint in the g band [arXiv:1704.01585].

sources.summary: HSCJ090613 + 032939 is a lens whose faint g-band main arc was detected but rejected because a ring model fit better; z/i modeling recovers the candidate.

quotes.quote: HSCJ090613 + 032939. This lens was also identified by . The main arc was detected by \ but was then discarded after the modeling step, since the ring model produced a better fit to the data compared to the lens model. The arc is relatively faint in the g band, which is used for the modeling step. If z and i band are used for the modeling of the lens and arc light respectively, \ recovers this candidate.

gmw_00011975.name: HSCJ115214 + 003126
gmw_00011975.overview: HSCJ115214 + 003126 is a strong gravitational lens candidate [arXiv:1704.01585]. The system features a very faint candidate arc located 5 arcseconds away from the lens, though the lack of convincing emission lines suggests this alignment may be a coincidence [arXiv:1704.01585].

sources.summary: HSCJ115214 + 003126 is a lens candidate with a very faint arc 5 arcsec from the lens and no convincing emission lines, leaving possible chance alignment.

quotes.quote: HSCJ115214 + 003126. The main candidate arc is too faint and was not detected by . Incidentally, although this system was found by the emission line search algorithm, the candidate arc is very faint and is located 5'' away from the lens. It is then unlikely that a signal from the source galaxy was detected in the BOSS spectrum. Indeed, visual inspection of the spectrum did not show any convincing emission lines. It is then possible that the presence of the arc is a fortuitous coincidence.

gmw_00011976.name: MSDM29.5+5
gmw_00011976.overview: MSDM29.5+5 is a high-redshift Lyman-alpha emitting galaxy [arXiv:0802.2393]. High-resolution spectroscopy reveals that the galaxy has a red-skewed line asymmetry and a source size considerably smaller than the slit [arXiv:0802.2393].

sources.summary: The paper identifies MSDM29.5+5 as a confirmed high-redshift Lyman-alpha emitter with a red-skewed Keck line profile, measured flux, and a single-component line-profile fit.

quotes.quote: Lyman-alpha-emitter MSDM29.5+5 from the 15H field shows the classic red-skewed line asymmetry of a LAE in the high-resolution, Keck spectrum; see panel () of Figure fig:msdm29.5+5 . The line width is 235 FWHM. The low resolution spectrum, panels (a) and (b), is not sufficient to recognize the asymmetry. The line in the 200-l spectrum is narrower than the overlaid sky line, indicating a source size considerably smaller than the slit. The attenuated \ flux is 1.8 0.2 10^ 17 \ in the 2005 May discovery spectrum.

The profile of 29.5+5 is well fitted with a single component. The fitted instrumental smoothing, 110 FWHM, is consistent with the resolution of the Keck-LRIS, follow-up spectrum. The model indicates a FWHM prior to attenuation of 400 .

gmw_00011977.name: MSDM80+3
gmw_00011977.overview: MSDM80+3 is a Lyman-alpha emitting galaxy that was first discovered in 2004 [arXiv:0802.2393]. High-resolution spectroscopy of the galaxy reveals multiple line components, and it is likely the same object as Murayama object 55 [arXiv:0802.2393].

sources.summary: The paper describes MSDM80+3 as the COSMOS-field confirmed LAE, noting its 2004 discovery, asymmetric and multi-component line profile, flux/equivalent-width limits, and possible match to Murayama object 55.

quotes.quote: Figure fig:msdm80+3 shows LAE MSDM80+3, discovered in the 2004 search of the 10H field. The broad bandpass spectrum shows a slight red asymmetry of questionable significance. The higher resolution spectrum shows multiple line components spread over about 400 FWHM. The flux is 1.4 0.2 10^ 17 . The 3 limiting z^ ' flux (for 2 \ diameter apertures) gives a lower limit on the observed-frame equivalent width of 144 .

We believe 80+3 is the same object as 55 detected by Muramyama (2007) using Subaru/Suprime-Cam narrowband imaging. The declinations agree to 0 2. Their right ascension is nearly 1 0 west of ours, consistent with the zeropoint shift we found relative to the Subaru image in sec:observations .

gmw_00011978.overview: The object 71-5 is a marginally confirmed Lyman-alpha emitting galaxy located in the 15H field [arXiv:0802.2393]. Low-resolution spectra hint at a broad, red wing, whereas high-resolution Keck spectroscopy reveals a narrow line [arXiv:0802.2393].

sources.summary: The paper labels 71-5 a marginally confirmed LAE in the 15H field, reporting ambiguous broad-wing evidence, a narrow Keck line, and no LCIRS B or z object at its position.

quotes.quote: Another line-emitter, 71-5, marginally meets our criteria to be labeled ``confirmed.'' The shape of the line-profile leaves some ambiguity. Figure fig:msdm71-5 shows follow-up spectra of 71-5 taken with the 200-l and the 150-l grisms. Even at such low resolution, both of these spectra hint at a broad, red wing. Curiously, our high resolution Keck, LRIS spectrum shows a narrow line.

The B and z LCIRS images show no objects near the position of 71-5. We detect an object slightly less than 1 5 to the southeast in our deeper WB48-78 image; but, as explained in sec:15Himages , a weak detection in this band does not veto a line identification as .

gmw_00011979.name: Radio source A
gmw_00011979.overview: Radio source A is a central bent-tail radio galaxy with a significantly bent northern jet and a disturbed southern jet [arXiv:2006.15155]. It is also classified as a possible wide-angle tail or hybrid morphology radio source due to its asymmetric Fanaroff-Riley I and II appearances [arXiv:2006.15155].

sources.summary: The paper classifies Radio source A as the central bent-tail source and possible WAT/HyMoRS, with a bent northern jet, disturbed southern jet, and asymmetric FR I/FR II-like structure.

quotes.quote: Radio source A is the most central source and is classified first and foremost as a bent-tail (BT) source and secondarily as a WAT/HyMoRS. Both the northern and southern jets contribute to the bent-tail classification. However, the northern jet of the source is significantly bent whereas the morphology of the southern jet is less clear. In the northern jet, there is clear evidence of a bent jet that has an extension northward (possibly a plume) giving it the classification of bent-tail. Combining the C-band data (6.0 GHz), which highlights the direction of the jet, and the L-band data (1.4 GHz), which highlights the extent of the jet, there is evidence that the southern jet is disturbed as well.

Source A could also be classified as a HyMoRS due to its FR I appearance in the northern jet and its FR II appearance of the southern jet. However, we acknowledge that a HyMoRS morphology may arise due to projection effects Harwood20 . Additionally, there is an asymmetry in the length of the northern jet versus the southern jet with each being bent by roughly the same amount, but the southern jet extends further along the inferred jet axis than the northern jet.

gmw_00011980.name: source B
gmw_00011980.overview: Source B is a bent-tail radio galaxy located to the east of a central radio source [arXiv:2006.15155]. Its jets are symmetrically bent with a large opening angle, indicating a lower velocity that may be driven by a merger or infalling motion [arXiv:2006.15155].

sources.summary: The paper describes source B as an eastern bent-tail radio galaxy with symmetrically bent jets, a large opening angle indicating lower velocity, and motion possibly tied to merger activity or infall.

quotes.quote: The source to the east of the central radio source (marked as source B) is classified as a bent-tail. Showcased by the 1.4 GHz data, the jets are symmetrically bent. This symmetrical behavior is expected for a classical radio source in a uniform medium Falle91,Kaiser97 . The angle that the bent tails subtend is quite large and thus it is not classified as a narrow-angle tail or head-tail radio galaxy.

The rather large opening angle demonstrated by this source thus indicates a lower velocity. The motion of this galaxy could be due to the merger or simply infalling due to typical motions of galaxies near the cluster center.

gmw_00011981.name: source D
gmw_00011981.overview: Source D is a Fanaroff-Riley class II radio galaxy characterized by evenly spaced radio lobes [arXiv:2006.15155]. The galaxy does not exhibit a bent morphology, and its host counterpart has not been detected at the inferred origin of the radio emission [arXiv:2006.15155].

sources.summary: The paper identifies source D as the western FR II radio galaxy, with evenly spaced lobes, no bent morphology, and no detected counterpart at the inferred radio-origin position.

quotes.quote: The most western source (marked as source D) is an FR II source. It has the characteristic evenly spaced lobes and does not exhibit any bent characteristics.

We note that the nearest match for the FR II radio galaxy (source D in Figure fig:spz_cps ) is 2 1 from the inferred origin of the radio emission and is associated with the lobe instead of the origin of emission. We thus conclude that we do not detect a counterpart for source D.

gmw_00011982.name: SDSS J150612.81+513707.0
gmw_00011982.overview: SDSS J150612.81+513707.0 is a low-redshift Fanaroff-Riley class II radio galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.611 [arXiv:2006.15155]. Its optical spectrum shows early-type galaxy features alongside narrow emission lines that indicate the presence of a buried active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2006.15155].

sources.summary: The appendix identifies SDSS J150612.81+513707.0/source F as a low-redshift FR II radio galaxy at z=0.611, with early-type spectral features and narrow-line evidence for a buried AGN.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: At high frequencies the core is seen ( _ 2000 = 15h06m12s.81, _ 2000 = +51 37 7 3 ), but at low frequency in the LoTSS data, the full FR II structure can be seen.

quotes.quote: Through our observations we discovered a low redshift source to the west of the cluster (source F in Figure fig:source_F ). This source is a known low-redshift FR II source. At high frequencies the core is seen ( _ 2000 = 15h06m12s.81, _ 2000 = +51 37 7 3 ), but at low frequency in the LoTSS data, the full FR II structure can be seen.

The core has an optical counterpart that is detected in SDSS (SDSS J150612.81+513707.0) and has a spectroscopic redshift of z =0.611 http://skyserver.sdss.org/dr15/en/tools/explore/Summary.aspx?id=1237659324947431986 SDSSDR15 . The SDSS spectrum shows an early-type spectrum with prominent Calcium, Hydrogen, and Potassium absorption and a 4000 \ break. The spectrum also shows strong indications of a buried AGN with narrow [ OII ] and [ OIII ] emission lines, but no evident H emission.

gmw_00011983.name: SDSS J074240.37+651021.4
gmw_00011983.overview: SDSS J074240.37+651021.4 is a distant background galaxy located near the active galaxy Mrk 78 [arXiv:1908.01376]. Spectroscopic observations of its weak emission lines indicate a redshift of z=0.382, confirming it is not a dwarf companion or tidal remnant of Mrk 78 [arXiv:1908.01376].

sources.summary: The paper tests whether SDSS J074240.37+651021.4 is a Mrk 78 companion or tidal remnant, but its emission lines imply z=0.382, consistent with a distant background galaxy.

quotes.quote: The SDSS DR15 and SCORPIO R band images reveal the galaxy SDSS J074240.37+651021.4 with an unknown redshift in the nearest neighbourhood of the C1 and C2 clouds at a distance of 18 '' from the Mrk 78 nucleus (marked as `G' in Fig. fields ). We have checked the possibility that this galaxy being a dwarf companion of Mrk 78 or remnant of a tidal structure. The obtained long-slit spectra (see the slit position in Fig. fields , PA=127 ) show the continuum of `G' with weak emission lines ( =5230 and =7016 ) marked with arrows in Fig. spec . We interpreted them as [ OII ] 3727 and [ OIII ] 5007 at the redshift z=0.382 .
This spectroscopic redshift is comparable to the photometric SDSS DR15 SDSSDR15 redshift: z=0.367 0.130 . Therefore, it is a distant background galaxy.

gmw_00011984.name: ESO-LV 4460170
gmw_00011984.overview: ESO-LV 4460170 is a high-surface-brightness spiral galaxy that hosts a modeled central bar [arXiv:1501.07178]. The stellar population gradients measured across its bulge-dominated region are consistent with those typically found in other spiral galaxy bulges [arXiv:1501.07178].

sources.summary: The paper analyzes ESO-LV 4460170 as a high-surface-brightness spiral with a modeled bar, a photometric bar signature, and bulge stellar-population gradients consistent with earlier spiral-bulge studies.

quotes.quote: ESO-LV 1890070, ESO-LV 4460170, ESO-LV 4500200, and ESO-LV 5140100 turned out to host a high surface-brightness disk and therefore were not considered in the subsequent analysis morelli2012 . Since the long-slit spectra of the four neglected galaxies were available to us, we decided to derive the stellar population properties of their bulges.

A bar was detected and modelled in ESO-LV 4460170 and ESO-LV 4500200, whereas no bar was needed to improve the surface-brightness models of ESO-LV 1890070 and ESO-LV 5140100. The bump in the surface-brightness profile associated to both a significant peak in the ellipticity and a roughly constant position angle is the photometric signature of the bar in ESO-LV 4460170 and ESO-LV 4500200 (see aguetal09 for a detailed discussion).

The properties of the stellar populations of ESO-LV 1890070, ESO-LV 4460170, and ESO-LV 5140100 are consistent with the previous results obtained for the bulges of spiral galaxies jabletal07, gandetal07, moreetal08 . The gradients of the stellar population properties along the major axis of all the galaxies were measured in their bulge-dominated regions. The results are consistent with previous findings for the bulges of spiral galaxies jabletal07, moreetal08, morelli2012 .

gmw_00011985.name: DDO 226
gmw_00011985.overview: DDO 226 is a dwarf irregular galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0603073, arXiv:astro-ph/0302045] located at a distance of approximately 4.92 Mpc [arXiv:astro-ph/0302045]. Its stellar population includes both blue and red stars [arXiv:astro-ph/0302045], featuring a substantial asymptotic giant branch population and numerous supergiant stars [arXiv:astro-ph/0603073].

sources.summary: DDO 226 is a difficult dwarf-irregular case with substantial AGB and supergiant populations, broadened RGB/AGB sequences near the limit, and a TRGB recovered using modeled photometric errors.

quotes.quote: The color-magnitude diagram of DDO 226 is demonstrated in Fig. f:ddo226 . Exposures of 600 sec were obtained in both F606W and F814W filters. This dwarf irregular galaxy has a substantial AGB population, which is clearly seen in the CMD, and a number of blue and red supergiant stars. The red giant branch and AGB are widened by errors because they are close to the photometric limit. The TRGB is situated about one magnitude above the photometric limit.

In this case the influence of photometric errors and incompleteness makes the TRGB detection especially difficult. However, using the photometric error modeling from the artificial star experiments still allows us to obtain reliable results: F814W _ TRGB = 24.39 0.08 at the 68 confidence level.

sources.summary: The paper identifies DDO 226 as a dIrr galaxy with high Local Group-frame velocity, mixed blue and red populations, a weak red giant branch, and a TRGB distance of 4.92 Mpc.

quotes.quote: This dIrr galaxy has a relatively high radial velocity, V_ LG = 408 km s ^ 1 . The CMD of DDO 226 shows blue and red stellar populations. There is no strong discontinuity in the luminosity function but there is only a slight hint of a red giant branch. Two peaks are seen in the Sobel--filtered luminosity function. The first lower peak at I = 23.8 appears to be caused by AGB stars, and the second one, at I = 24 44 0 24 , which we interpret as the TRGB, yields a linear distance of 4.92 0.58 Mpc.

gmw_00011986.name: COSMOS-1908
gmw_00011986.overview: COSMOS-1908 is a low-mass, highly star-forming galaxy at a redshift of z=3.08 [arXiv:1606.04107]. It hosts a young stellar population with an age of approximately 160 million years and exhibits a high ionization parameter with little nebular reddening [arXiv:1606.04107].

sources.summary: The paper measures COSMOS-1908 as a z=3.08 low-mass, highly star-forming galaxy with direct-method oxygen abundance, young stars, high ionization parameter, little gas reddening, and high electron density.

quotes.quote: We present measurements of the electron-temperature based oxygen abundance for a highly star-forming galaxy at z=3.08 , COSMOS-1908. This is the highest redshift at which [ O iii ] 4363 has been detected, and the first time that this line has been measured at z>2 . We estimate an oxygen abundance of 12+log(O/H) =8.00^ +0.13 _ 0.14 . This galaxy is a low-mass ( 10^ 9.3 ), highly star-forming ( 50 yr ^ 1 ) system that hosts a young stellar population ( 160 Myr). We investigate the physical conditions of the ionized gas in COSMOS-1908 and find that this galaxy has a high ionization parameter, little nebular reddening ( E(B-V)_ gas <0.14 ), and a high electron density ( n_e 500 cm ^ 3 ).

gmw_00011987.name: Hydra A
gmw_00011987.overview: Hydra A is a dominant, bright radio galaxy [arXiv:1203.5790]. While it is extended at high resolutions, it can be treated as a point source at lower resolutions and exhibits an unexpectedly high low-frequency flux [arXiv:1203.5790].

sources.summary: The paper uses Hydra A as the field's strongest calibration source, treats it as a point source at MWA-32T resolution, and measures it as a dominant bright radio galaxy with unexpectedly high low-frequency flux.

quotes.quote: For the two fields presented in this work, Hydra A is the strongest source in the field. Lane2004 present low-frequency images that show that while it is quite extended at the VLA's resolution, most of the flux is contained within a region that is a few arcminutes in radius. Since this extent is smaller than the angular resolution of the MWA-32T, we were able to treat Hydra A as a point source in our calibration analysis. One might expect that for a large field-of-view instrument such as the MWA, we would also need to include several or even many additional strong sources in the calibration model with known direction-dependent gains. We therefore experimented with calibration models that included several point sources in addition to Hydra A, but we found that the complex tile gain solutions were not significantly changed. We therefore simply used Hydra A as the only calibration source in subsequent analysis.

The bright radio galaxy Hydra A is the dominant source in these maps. The flux of the source is measured to be 710 210 Jy in the full band average map of the Hydra A field, and 550 170 Jy in the full band average map of the EoR2 field. These measurements of Hydra A are significantly brighter than expected based on previous measurements: the Culgoora 160 MHz measurements give a flux of 243 Jy, and a prediction based off of the Culgoora and MRC measurements (as described in Section sec:astfluxcal ) gives a flux of 284 Jy. Although Hydra A is slightly extended in the MWA-32T maps, the structures seen in the previous low frequency maps of Hydra A presented by Lane2004 are below the scale of the MWA synthesized beam.

gmw_00011988.name: SA 0426-002
gmw_00011988.overview: SA 0426-002 is a dwarf elliptical galaxy located in the Perseus Cluster [arXiv:1407.2368]. It exhibits a bar-like morphology surrounded by low-surface brightness lobes or a ring-like structure, which may have formed through tidal interactions with other cluster members [arXiv:1407.2368].

sources.summary: The paper describes SA 0426-002 as an unusual Perseus dwarf elliptical with a bar, lobes, and ring-like structure, arguing it is transforming from a low-mass disc through harassment.

quotes.quote: We furthermore examine SA 0426-002, a unique dE in our sample with a bar-like morphology surrounded by low-surface brightness wings/lobes ( _ B = 27 mag arcsec ^ 2 ). Given its morphology, velocity dispersion ( _ 0 = 33.9 6.1 km s ^ 1 ), velocity relative to the brightest cluster galaxy NGC 1275 (2711 km s ^ 1 ), size ( R_ e =2.1 0.10 kpc), and S ersic index ( n= 1.2 0.02 ), we hypothesise the dwarf has morphologically transformed from a low mass disc to dE via harassment. The low-surface brightness lobes can be explained as a ring feature, with the bar formation triggered by tidal interactions via speed encounters with Perseus Cluster members. Alongside spiral structure found in dEs in Fornax and Virgo, SA 0426-002 provides crucial evidence that a fraction of bright dEs have a disc infall origin, and are not part of the primordial cluster population.

SA 0426-002 exhibits a bar-like morphology with R_ e = 2.1 0.1 kpc and S ersic n = 1.24 0.02 . Based on its bar-like morphology, ring structure, and its location in the Perseus Cluster, we hypothesise that SA 0426-002 is near its orbit pericentre, and is currently undergoing morphological transformation from a disc galaxy into a dwarf elliptical. The wing-like structure is the result of stellar mass loss due to tidal heating by repeated interactions with other cluster galaxies, else tidal interactions with the BCG NGC 1275.

gmw_00011989.name: CGW38
gmw_00011989.overview: CGW38 is a dwarf elliptical galaxy located in the Perseus cluster [arXiv:1407.2368]. It hosts an old, metal-poor stellar population and has a measured central velocity dispersion of approximately 40 km/s [arXiv:1407.2368].

sources.summary: The paper measures CGW38 as a Perseus dwarf elliptical with a central velocity dispersion near 40 km/s, an old metal-poor stellar population, and a derived stellar mass.

quotes.quote: We therefore use our esi spectroscopy to measure central velocity dispersions and dynamical masses for three dwarfs in our sample with a minimum signal-to-noise of 7 at 8500 : SA 0426-002, CGW38, and CGW39. To measure global internal velocity dispersions for the dEs, we use the Penalised Pixel Fitting ( Ppxf ) routine 2004PASP..116..138C . We utilise five standard stars taken under an identical instrumental setup to the dwarf observations. The Fe, Mg, CaT, and H lines are used in determining the velocity dispersion for CGW39, and the H line used for CGW38 and SA 0426-002 due to the CaT feature blending with skylines. For SA 0426-002, we measure = 32.4 5.5 km s ^ 1 . We measure = 38.8 4.4 km s ^ 1 for CGW38, and = 26.3 4.3 km s ^ 1 for CGW39. The errors are the formal errors provided by ppxf . To compare our results to the literature, we convert these global values of to central values _ 0 at R_ e /8 using equation 1 of 2006MNRAS.366.1126C . These conversions are <10 of the aperture velocity dispersion, and within the error bars of the uncorrected values of . For SA 0426-002, we find _ 0 = 33.9 6.1 km s ^ 1 . CGW38 has a central velocity dispersion _ 0 = 40.0 5.5 km s ^ 1 , and for CGW39, we determine a central velocity dispersion _ 0 = 29.7 7.3 km s ^ 1 .

Penny Conselice (2008) estimate CGW38 and CGW39 to have old ( >10 Gyr) stellar populations, with metallicities [ Z/H ] <-0.33 dex. To calculate stellar mass-to-light ratios, we utilise the SSP models of Bruzual Charlot (2003), assuming a Salpeter IMF, a stellar age of 10 Gyr, and a metallicity [ Fe/H ] = -0.33 dex. Based on these assumptions, CGW38 and CGW39 have B band stellar mass-to-light ratios 6 . We note this ratio is an upper limit, as lower metallicity stellar populations will have lower stellar mass-to-light ratios. By converting their luminosities to stellar masses, we find M_ = (1.9 0.3) 10^ 9 M _ for CGW38, and M_ = (1.7 0.5) 10^ 9 M _ for CGW39.

gmw_00011990.name: CGW39
gmw_00011990.overview: CGW39 is a dwarf elliptical galaxy located in the Perseus cluster [arXiv:1407.2368]. It hosts an old stellar population estimated to be over 10 billion years old, with a low metallicity and a central velocity dispersion of approximately 29.7 km/s [arXiv:1407.2368].

sources.summary: The paper measures CGW39 as a Perseus dwarf elliptical with a central velocity dispersion near 30 km/s and an old metal-poor stellar population, deriving its stellar mass with CGW38.

quotes.quote: We therefore use our esi spectroscopy to measure central velocity dispersions and dynamical masses for three dwarfs in our sample with a minimum signal-to-noise of 7 at 8500 : SA 0426-002, CGW38, and CGW39. To measure global internal velocity dispersions for the dEs, we use the Penalised Pixel Fitting ( Ppxf ) routine 2004PASP..116..138C . We utilise five standard stars taken under an identical instrumental setup to the dwarf observations. The Fe, Mg, CaT, and H lines are used in determining the velocity dispersion for CGW39, and the H line used for CGW38 and SA 0426-002 due to the CaT feature blending with skylines. For SA 0426-002, we measure = 32.4 5.5 km s ^ 1 . We measure = 38.8 4.4 km s ^ 1 for CGW38, and = 26.3 4.3 km s ^ 1 for CGW39. The errors are the formal errors provided by ppxf . To compare our results to the literature, we convert these global values of to central values _ 0 at R_ e /8 using equation 1 of 2006MNRAS.366.1126C . These conversions are <10 of the aperture velocity dispersion, and within the error bars of the uncorrected values of . For SA 0426-002, we find _ 0 = 33.9 6.1 km s ^ 1 . CGW38 has a central velocity dispersion _ 0 = 40.0 5.5 km s ^ 1 , and for CGW39, we determine a central velocity dispersion _ 0 = 29.7 7.3 km s ^ 1 .

Penny Conselice (2008) estimate CGW38 and CGW39 to have old ( >10 Gyr) stellar populations, with metallicities [ Z/H ] <-0.33 dex. To calculate stellar mass-to-light ratios, we utilise the SSP models of Bruzual Charlot (2003), assuming a Salpeter IMF, a stellar age of 10 Gyr, and a metallicity [ Fe/H ] = -0.33 dex. Based on these assumptions, CGW38 and CGW39 have B band stellar mass-to-light ratios 6 . We note this ratio is an upper limit, as lower metallicity stellar populations will have lower stellar mass-to-light ratios. By converting their luminosities to stellar masses, we find M_ = (1.9 0.3) 10^ 9 M _ for CGW38, and M_ = (1.7 0.5) 10^ 9 M _ for CGW39.

gmw_00011991.name: SDSS J081330.15 + 541844.4
gmw_00011991.overview: SDSS J081330.15 + 541844.4 is a galaxy with optical emission line flux ratios that indicate the presence of an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1811.01973]. It also hosts an overlapping X-ray source that is spatially offset from the galaxy's centroid [arXiv:1811.01973].

sources.summary: The paper lists SDSS J081330.15 + 541844.4 as a target galaxy selected for AGN-like optical line ratios, an overlapping AGN-like X-ray source, and a spatial offset from the SDSS galaxy centroid.

quotes.quote: SDSS J081330.15 + 541844.4 & 0.041 & 147 & 918 & 1092 SDSS J094032.25 + 311328.5 & 0.170 & 147 & 618 & 1329 SDSS J102141.89 + 130550.4 & 0.077 & 147 & 618 & 1302 SDSS J111458.01 + 403611.4 & 0.076 & 147 & 618 & 1329 SDSS J123420.12 + 475155.7 & 0.183 & 147 & 918 & 1041 SDSS J212512.48 071329.9 & 0.064 & 147 & 918 & 999 Column 1: Target galaxy name; Column 2: SDSS spectroscopic redshift; Column 3: band image exposure time; Column 4: band image exposure time; and Column 5: band image exposure time. The main sample analyzed in this paper consists of galaxies identified using the method from that were observed by (Program: GO 14068, PI: Barrows). Full details of the sample selection are provided in , but here we provide the basic properties: each galaxy was originally observed by the Data Release 7 spectroscopic fiber survey and has optical emission line flux ratios of / and / that place them in the AGN region of a typical Baldwin-Phillips-Terlevich (BPT) diagram Baldwin1981 as defined in Kewley:2006 . This diagnostic suggests that an AGN is present within the 1 5 fiber radius centered on the galaxy. Furthermore, an X-ray source with a rest-frame, unabsorbed 2-10 keV luminosity ( ) or hardness ratio consistent with the presence of an AGN is also detected and has a 1 error ellipse that spatially overlaps with the SDSS fiber. We then selected galaxies where the X-ray source is spatially offset from the galaxy centroid based on SDSS imaging.

gmw_00011996.name: SDSS J212512.48 071329.9
gmw_00011996.overview: SDSS J212512.48 071329.9 is a galaxy with optical emission line ratios indicative of an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1811.01973]. It hosts an overlapping X-ray source that is spatially offset from the galaxy's centroid [arXiv:1811.01973].

sources.summary: The paper lists SDSS J212512.48 071329.9 as a target galaxy selected for AGN-like optical line ratios, an overlapping AGN-like X-ray source, and a spatial offset from the SDSS galaxy centroid.

quotes.quote: SDSS J081330.15 + 541844.4 & 0.041 & 147 & 918 & 1092 SDSS J094032.25 + 311328.5 & 0.170 & 147 & 618 & 1329 SDSS J102141.89 + 130550.4 & 0.077 & 147 & 618 & 1302 SDSS J111458.01 + 403611.4 & 0.076 & 147 & 618 & 1329 SDSS J123420.12 + 475155.7 & 0.183 & 147 & 918 & 1041 SDSS J212512.48 071329.9 & 0.064 & 147 & 918 & 999 Column 1: Target galaxy name; Column 2: SDSS spectroscopic redshift; Column 3: band image exposure time; Column 4: band image exposure time; and Column 5: band image exposure time. The main sample analyzed in this paper consists of galaxies identified using the method from that were observed by (Program: GO 14068, PI: Barrows). Full details of the sample selection are provided in , but here we provide the basic properties: each galaxy was originally observed by the Data Release 7 spectroscopic fiber survey and has optical emission line flux ratios of / and / that place them in the AGN region of a typical Baldwin-Phillips-Terlevich (BPT) diagram Baldwin1981 as defined in Kewley:2006 . This diagnostic suggests that an AGN is present within the 1 5 fiber radius centered on the galaxy. Furthermore, an X-ray source with a rest-frame, unabsorbed 2-10 keV luminosity ( ) or hardness ratio consistent with the presence of an AGN is also detected and has a 1 error ellipse that spatially overlaps with the SDSS fiber. We then selected galaxies where the X-ray source is spatially offset from the galaxy centroid based on SDSS imaging.

gmw_00011997.name: NGC 2592
gmw_00011997.overview: NGC 2592 is classified as an early-type galaxy [arXiv:2004.08182]. It features a large bulge-to-total light ratio of 0.54 and exhibits a strongly skewed stellar angular-momentum distribution [arXiv:2004.08182].

sources.summary: The paper uses NGC 2592 as an early-type example with large bulge-to-total light ratio, a strongly skewed stellar angular-momentum distribution, and a circular profile that remains positively skewed.

quotes.quote: NGC 2906 is a late-type spiral galaxy with low bulge-to-total light ratio = 0.06 . Its is broad and symmetric, and peaks near = 1. NGC 2592 is an early-type galaxy with large = 0.54 ; its is strongly-skewed and peaks near = 0. In between these two extremes, the for NGC 7311 ( = 0.27 ) has characteristics of both disk and bulge. In Figure fig:pdfj_c we show derived using circular-only velocity, that is, calculated without the dispersion term. The circular for late-type NGC 2906 is symmetric, centred near = 1. For early-type NGC 2592 the circular peaks nearer = 0.5 and is positively skewed, while NGC 7311 has skewness intermediate between them.

It is clear that the halo curves do not at all describe late-type NGC 2906, intermediate NGC 7311, or even early type NGC 2592. In all cases there is less baryonic material compared with the DM halo at low ( 0 ), more at average ( 1 ), and less at high ( 3 ). The analytic solution for an exponential disk with constant rotation velocity is p(s)=4s e ^ 2s , plotted in Fig. fig:pdfj_o as orange dot-dashed lines. As expected, this model fails to describe the galaxies in our sample, even the almost-pure disks such as NGC 2906 which has =0.06 , since the rotation velocities are not constant but decrease towards the galaxy centres.

gmw_00011998.name: NGC 7311
gmw_00011998.overview: NGC 7311 is an intermediate-type galaxy with a bulge-to-total light ratio of 0.27 [arXiv:2004.08182]. Its stellar angular-momentum distribution exhibits characteristics of both a disk and a bulge, showing an intermediate circular skewness that is not well described by dark matter halo curves [arXiv:2004.08182].

sources.summary: The paper presents NGC 7311 as an intermediate case whose stellar angular-momentum distribution has both disk and bulge characteristics, intermediate circular skewness, and mismatch with halo curves.

quotes.quote: NGC 2906 is a late-type spiral galaxy with low bulge-to-total light ratio = 0.06 . Its is broad and symmetric, and peaks near = 1. NGC 2592 is an early-type galaxy with large = 0.54 ; its is strongly-skewed and peaks near = 0. In between these two extremes, the for NGC 7311 ( = 0.27 ) has characteristics of both disk and bulge. In Figure fig:pdfj_c we show derived using circular-only velocity, that is, calculated without the dispersion term. The circular for late-type NGC 2906 is symmetric, centred near = 1. For early-type NGC 2592 the circular peaks nearer = 0.5 and is positively skewed, while NGC 7311 has skewness intermediate between them.

It is clear that the halo curves do not at all describe late-type NGC 2906, intermediate NGC 7311, or even early type NGC 2592. In all cases there is less baryonic material compared with the DM halo at low ( 0 ), more at average ( 1 ), and less at high ( 3 ). The analytic solution for an exponential disk with constant rotation velocity is p(s)=4s e ^ 2s , plotted in Fig. fig:pdfj_o as orange dot-dashed lines. As expected, this model fails to describe the galaxies in our sample, even the almost-pure disks such as NGC 2906 which has =0.06 , since the rotation velocities are not constant but decrease towards the galaxy centres.

gmw_00011999.name: ESO 474-G040
gmw_00011999.overview: ESO 474-G040 is an incomplete, knotted ring galaxy that appears empty on sky survey images [arXiv:1506.00409]. It likely formed from a past merger of disk galaxies, and its individual knots contain old stellar populations alongside active star formation [arXiv:1506.00409].

sources.summary: SALT imaging, spectroscopy, and Fabry-Perot data show ESO 474-G040 as an incomplete knotted empty ring with old and young populations, likely formed by an old disk-galaxy merger and bead instability.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: The kinematic centre, determined as the centre of symmetry in the velocity distribution, is located at [ (2000.0)=00:53:59.1: (2000.0)=-27:08:36.5 ] with our astrometry solution.

quotes.quote: We describe observations of the apparently empty ring galaxy ESO 474-G040 obtained with the Southern African Large Telescope (SALT). The observations, consisting of imaging, long-slit spectroscopy and Fabry-P e rot mapping of the H line, allow determining the ring kinematics as well as estimating the metallicity of the ring and the stellar population composition in its various parts. We propose that the object could best be understood as being the result of a past merger of disk galaxies, which formed a gas ring that subsequently disrupted via the bead instability and is presently forming stars.

ESO474 has the appearance of an empty ring galaxy on sky survey images. We started this investigation in an attempt to understand its nature, origin and evolution. The results from the SALT observations show that the ring is not complete and uniform, but at least some of its elements (knots) contain considerable fractions, by mass, of old stellar populations while also forming young stars; e.g., knot F is essentially very young. Each of the knots appears as a dwarf galaxy containing older stellar populations, while also undergoing present star formation.

gmw_00012000.name: G3
gmw_00012000.overview: The galaxy G3 is located at a redshift of 0.149556 [arXiv:2206.13550]. It hosts its own barely resolved radio counterpart at 1.5 GHz, which exhibits the spectrum of an active point-like radio source [arXiv:2206.13550].

sources.summary: The paper rules out G3 as associated with USS source B, noting it lies nearby but has a discrepant redshift and a barely resolved, normal-spectrum point-like radio counterpart.

quotes.quote: Galaxies G3 and G4 are seen close to B, however their redshifts ( z = 0.149556 and 0.152417) are significantly discrepant from that of A980. Furthermore, each of them has its own barely resolved radio counterpart seen in the VLA L-band low-resolution map at 1.5 GHz (see Fig. fig:radio_high_opt a), with the spectrum ( 1 ; see Appendix apdx:radio-spectrum ) of an active point-like radio source. Therefore both these normal-spectrum radio sources are highly unlikely to be physically associated with the diffuse USS source B.

gmw_00012001.name: G4
gmw_00012001.overview: G4 is a galaxy located at a redshift of approximately 0.1524 [arXiv:2206.13550]. It features its own barely resolved, point-like radio counterpart that exhibits a normal spectrum characteristic of an active radio source [arXiv:2206.13550].

sources.summary: The paper rules out G4 as associated with USS source B, noting it lies nearby but has a discrepant redshift and a barely resolved, normal-spectrum point-like radio counterpart.

quotes.quote: Galaxies G3 and G4 are seen close to B, however their redshifts ( z = 0.149556 and 0.152417) are significantly discrepant from that of A980. Furthermore, each of them has its own barely resolved radio counterpart seen in the VLA L-band low-resolution map at 1.5 GHz (see Fig. fig:radio_high_opt a), with the spectrum ( 1 ; see Appendix apdx:radio-spectrum ) of an active point-like radio source. Therefore both these normal-spectrum radio sources are highly unlikely to be physically associated with the diffuse USS source B.

gmw_00012002.name: G2
gmw_00012002.overview: The galaxy G2 is located at a redshift of z=0.161662 [arXiv:2206.13550]. It has an apparent magnitude of 20.06, which corresponds to an absolute magnitude of -19.39 and makes it under-luminous compared to typical elliptical galaxies that host low-redshift radio sources [arXiv:2206.13550].

sources.summary: The paper argues G2 is unlikely to host USS source A: it is undetected by VLA-FIRST and is much fainter than typical ellipticals hosting low-redshift radio galaxies.

quotes.quote: The galaxy G2, which is centrally located within the USS source A is undetected in the VLA-FIRST survey. Moreover, its apparent magnitude m_ =20.06 0.05 (PANSTRASS-DR1, chambers_2016arXiv ) corresponds to an absolute magnitude M_r=-19.39 0.05 , at its redshift z=0.161662. This is 3.4 magnitudes under-luminous compared to the average absolute magnitude ( 22.8 0.5 ) of the ellipticals hosting radio galaxies at z < 0.5 Treves_2005ApJ , which makes it a very unlikely host of the source `A'.

gmw_00012003.name: F563-1
gmw_00012003.overview: F563-1 is a low surface brightness galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0604576]. Its H-alpha rotation curve continues to rise beyond 15 arcseconds, whereas long-slit rotation curves turn over and flatten out [arXiv:astro-ph/0604576].

sources.summary: F563-1 is discussed as having one offset pointing, weak approaching-side emission, agreement with long-slit rotation curves inside 15 arcsec, and a rising H-alpha curve needing more coverage.

quotes.quote: F563-1: There was one pointing for this galaxy. The fiber positions are shown on an \ image (W.J.G. de Blok 2005, private communication). The fiber velocities were the average of the , \ and \ lines.

The fibers were offset toward the receding side of the galaxy, and there was very little emission in the fibers on the approaching side. The position angle was fixed to the value in MRdB and dBB . When compared to the long-slit \ rotation curves of dBMR and dBB , there is good agreement up to 15 . Beyond that, however, the long-slit curves and the \ curve DMV turn over and flatten out, while the \ curve continues to rise.

gmw_00012004.name: F583-4
gmw_00012004.overview: F583-4 is a low surface brightness galaxy with central and interstitial pointings [arXiv:astro-ph/0604576]. Its optical rotation curves are offset to higher velocities than its neutral hydrogen rotation curve due to beam smearing in the radio data [arXiv:astro-ph/0604576].

sources.summary: F583-4 has central and interstitial pointings, better approaching-side coverage, optical rotation curves above the HI curve, and a case where beam smearing in HI was important.

quotes.quote: F583-4: There were two pointings for this galaxy: a central pointing and an interstitial pointing; the fiber positions are shown on the R band image from DMV . The fiber velocities are the averages of the , \ and \ lines, and the approaching side of the galaxy has slightly better coverage than the receding side. The position angle was fixed to the value in MRdB .

The \ rotation curve is generally consistent with the long-slit \ rotation curve of dBMR . Both optical rotation curves are offset to noticeably higher velocities than the \ rotation curve of DMV . This is one of the few galaxies where beam smearing in the \ data turned out to be important dBMR .

gmw_00012005.name: F583-1
gmw_00012005.overview: F583-1 is a low surface brightness galaxy with a dark matter halo [arXiv:astro-ph/0604576]. Its rotation curve, derived from averaged emission-line velocities, is consistent with previous long-slit and neutral hydrogen rotation curves [arXiv:astro-ph/0604576].

sources.summary: F583-1 is described from one pointing with more approaching-side coverage, averaged emission-line velocities, a fixed MRdB position angle, and consistency with previous long-slit and HI rotation curves.

quotes.quote: F583-1: There is one pointing for this galaxy. The \ fibers are shown on the R band image of the galaxy from DMV . The fiber velocities are the averages of the , \ and \ lines, and more of the approaching side of the galaxy is seen than the receding side. The MRdB position angle was taken as the \ position angle. The \ rotation curve is consistent with both the long-slit \ rotation curve of dBMR and the \ rotation curve of DMV .

gmw_00012006.name: UGC 2216
gmw_00012006.overview: UGC 2216 is an irregular galaxy that contains a galaxy disk [arXiv:1505.00337]. The galaxy's radial oxygen abundance gradient is strongly biased by an H II region located at a galactocentric distance of 4.43 kpc [arXiv:1505.00337].

sources.summary: UGC 2216 has unusually divergent statistical and bootstrap oxygen-gradient errors because one H II region strongly biases its radial abundance gradient, making the gradient uncertain without that point.

quotes.quote: The statistical and bootstrapped errors of the oxygen abundance gradients are close to each other except in the case of the galaxy UGC 2216 where the bootstrapped error exceeds dramatically the statistical error. This is caused by the following. The radial abundance gradient in the UGC 2216 is strongly biased by an H ii region at a galactocentric distance of 4.43 kpc. When the bootstrapped subsample does not contain this point then the value of the radial abundance gradient is very uncertain since in this case the gradient is determined from measurements at close galactocentric distances. As a result, the bootstrapped error of the radial abundance gradient for this galaxy is quite large.

gmw_00012007.name: host galaxy of \
gmw_00012007.overview: The host galaxy of the transient is a low-mass system characterized by a low velocity width of its neutral gas [arXiv:1409.8287]. It is a faint galaxy with no detected emission lines and an unobscured star-formation rate upper limit of less than 0.07 solar masses per year [arXiv:1409.8287].

sources.summary: The paper describes the host as having cold interstellar absorption, narrow low-velocity neutral gas indicating a low-mass system, no detected emission lines, a low SFR limit, and faint late-time imaging.

quotes.quote: We detect narrow absorption lines of Mg 1 , Mg 2 , and Fe 2 , associated with the cold interstellar medium in the host galaxy, at two different epochs with X-shooter at the VLT, at a resolving power R 6000 . From Voigt-profile fitting, we derive the column densities log N ( Mg 1 ) = 11.94 0.06 , log N ( Mg 2 ) =14.7 0.3 , and log N ( Fe 2 ) =14.25 0.10 .

From the nondetection of Fe 2 fine-structure absorption lines, we derive a strict lower limit on the distance between the supernova and the narrow-line absorbing gas of 50 pc. The neutral gas responsible for the absorption in \ exhibits a single narrow component with a low velocity width, V=76   , indicating a low-mass host galaxy. No host-galaxy emission lines are detected, leading to an upper limit on the unobscured star-formation rate of SFR _ O II <0.07  . Late-time imaging shows the host galaxy of \ to be faint, with g_ AB 27.0 and R_ AB 26.0  mag, which roughly corresponds to M_ B, Vega 17.7  mag.

gmw_00012008.name: KIC 11614932
gmw_00012008.overview: KIC 11614932 is a spectroscopically confirmed active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1803.06436]. Its light curve exhibits stochastic variability, but it is contaminated by a nearby star with a 1.6-day periodicity [arXiv:1803.06436].

sources.summary: The paper treats KIC 11614932 as a spectroscopically confirmed AGN whose light curve is contaminated by a nearby periodic star, forcing exclusion from the sample.

quotes.quote: As a cautionary tale, we show the case of our spectroscopically-confirmed AGN KIC 11614932. Figure  fig:cautionlc shows the full light curve, which exhibits the stochastic variability expected for an AGN. However, a closer inspection reveals a periodic stellar variability signature (easiest to see by eye starting at approximately 160 days). The 1.6  day periodicity of this star can also be seen in the PSD (inset in the figure).

The star in question was close enough to the extended AGN host galaxy that it was impossible to remove from the mask while still encompassing the drift effects discussed above. Additionally, only partially encompassing the star will also result in drift effects. The only possible aperture is shown in Figure  fig:starmask , and so the object is excluded from our sample.

gmw_00012009.name: KIC 9650712
gmw_00012009.overview: KIC 9650712 is an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1803.06436]. Its light curve is poorly fit by standard models, instead showing a better fit with a broken power law or a single power law with an added quasi-periodic component [arXiv:1803.06436].

sources.summary: The paper uses KIC 9650712 to show thermal-recovery corrections affect power spectra, then notes its light curve is better fit by a broken power law or quasi-periodic component.

quotes.quote: As an example, in Figure  fig:thermlc and Figure  fig:thermps we show the result of flagging and removing the thermal recovery periods from the light curve of KIC 9650712. There is no other difference between the reduction methods of the light curves displayed in the graphs. The power spectrum is clearly affected, displaying a spurious characteristic timescale when generated by the uncorrected light curve.

One of these, KIC 9650712, is poorly fit by all of our values of , and is better fit by a broken power law with t_ char 53  days. However, it can also be well-modeled as a single power law with the addition of a quasi-periodic component. For this reason we use a different symbol for this break timescale in plots, so that readers may ignore it in this context.

gmw_00012010.name: KIC 7610713
gmw_00012010.overview: KIC 7610713 is an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1803.06436]. It exhibits an insignificant bimodal histogram that is likely caused by red-noise fluctuations rather than steady switching behavior [arXiv:1803.06436].

sources.summary: The paper reports that KIC 7610713 has the least significant Hartigan dip statistic among tested bimodal cases, with an insignificant bimodal histogram attributable to red-noise fluctuations.

quotes.quote: Figure fig:hartigan demonstrates the results of this test for KIC 9215110, which has the most significant ``dip" in the CDF, to KIC 7610713, the least significant These plots were made using a Python implementation of the Hartigan Dip Test which can be found at https://github.com/tatome/dip test. .

One of our objects, KIC 9145961, is only marginally significant, coming in at 97 with 1-day bins and 55 at 4-day bins, while KIC 7610713 is fully insignificant at both bin sizes (6 and 16 for 1- and 4-days). Indeed, KIC 7610713 is the only object with a bimodal histogram that does not exhibit the steady switching behavior just discussed; an examination of its light curve suggests that the bimodal histogram is simply due to red noise fluctuations.

gmw_00012011.name: source B
aliases_external: source B / component B companion of IRAS 20181-2244
gmw_00012011.overview: Source B is a nearby late-type companion galaxy located at the same redshift as a neighboring active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1807.08953]. It features a disk morphology with a red, dust-rich nucleus and displays tidal-tail residuals indicative of an ongoing interaction [arXiv:1807.08953].

sources.summary: The paper identifies source B as a nearby late-type companion galaxy at the same redshift as the AGN, with disk morphology, red nucleus, and tidal-tail residuals from interaction.

quotes.quote: In addition to the AGN and its host galaxy (component A in Fig.  fig:finding_chart ), we observe a nearby galaxy at position angle (P.A.) 26 ^ (component B in Fig.  fig:finding_chart ). We also identify an extended emission at P.A. 119 ^ which might be a point-like source distorted by seeing, and therefore another possible galaxy (component C in Fig.  fig:finding_chart ). All the other objects surrounding the system are instead confirmed stars.

Conversely, source B is a late-type galaxy, well reproduced by a S ersic profile for the bulge with the addition of an exponential profile for the disk. Also this object shows a luminosity excess in the residuals, which is suggestive of a tidal tail produced by its interaction with the AGN host.
The nucleus of the companion galaxy is also fairly red ( 1.6), showing that dust might be very abundant in both of these interacting galaxies.

gmw_00012012.name: Mkn 888
gmw_00012012.overview: Mkn 888 is a blue early-type galaxy that exhibits clear signatures of a galaxy merger [arXiv:2308.12153]. It features a star-forming blue central region surrounded by a red stripe, with faint blue structures in its outer disk that may represent a post-merger spiral structure [arXiv:2308.12153].

sources.summary: The paper includes Mkn 888 among star-forming blue early-type galaxies with merger signatures, a blue central region, red stripe, blue outer-disk faint features, and possible post-merger spiral structure.

quotes.quote: These galaxies are designated Mkn 888 and CGCG-315-014 in Fig. 5 of Keel_2022 . The galaxies show a merger signature throughout our optical color-composite and deep -band imaging data. The color map also shows a blue central region and a surrounding stripe with red color. The outer disk shows faint features with blue color that extends to the faint features seen in the -band imaging. The blue patch seen in the center could be the spiral structure that is resolved in HST imaging (as in the case of NGC 7252).

gmw_00012013.name: CGCG-315-014
gmw_00012013.overview: CGCG-315-014 is a blue early-type galaxy that is undergoing an ongoing tidal interaction with a neighboring galaxy [arXiv:2308.12153]. It exhibits merger signatures, a central spiral structure, and blue outer features associated with star formation from a recent gas-rich merger [arXiv:2308.12153].

sources.summary: The paper describes CGCG-315-014 as a star-forming blue early-type galaxy with merger signatures, ongoing tidal interaction, central spiral structure, and blue outer features linked to recent gas-rich merging.

quotes.quote: These galaxies are designated Mkn 888 and CGCG-315-014 in Fig. 5 of Keel_2022 . The galaxies show a merger signature throughout our optical color-composite and deep -band imaging data. The color map also shows a blue central region and a surrounding stripe with red color. The outer disk shows faint features with blue color that extends to the faint features seen in the -band imaging. The blue patch seen in the center could be the spiral structure that is resolved in HST imaging (as in the case of NGC 7252).

We note that galaxy CGCG-315-014 (ID 46) is undergoing an ongoing tidal interaction with a neighboring galaxy (see also George_2015 ). The spiral structure at the center is very clear in the HST imaging for this galaxy. The blue color along the faint features outside the galaxy could be the star formation associated with a very recent gas-rich wet merger.

gmw_00012014.name: NGC 1308
gmw_00012014.overview: NGC 1308 is an SB0 galaxy that hosts a strong central bar [arXiv:astro-ph/0501163]. The bulge of the galaxy is highly rotation-dominated, rotating faster than classical bulges and oblate spheroid models [arXiv:astro-ph/0501163].

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 1308's SB0 bulge as unusually rotation dominated and suggests its strong bar may have influenced bulge formation.

quotes.quote: The bulges of NGC 1308 and NGC 4340 rotate faster than bulges of unbarred galaxies and models of isotropic oblate spheroids with equal ellipticity.

This is not the case of the bulges of NGC 1308 and NGC 4340 which are rotating significantly faster than oblate spheroid models and are more similar to the triaxial SB0 bulges studied by Kormendy (1982).

The bulges of NGC 1308 and NGC 4340 are more rotation-dominated than classical bulges and oblate spheroid models in the V_ max / _ 0 - diagram (e.g., Kormendy Kennicutt 2004). The bulge of IC 874 is a low- outlier in the FJ relation and it is outside the 3 band in the FP of elliptical galaxies and S0 bulges. Since IC 874, NGC 1308 and NGC 4340 host the strongest bars of the sample (Table tab:bar ) we could speculate that the formation of their bulges has been influenced by the bar.

gmw_00012015.name: NGC 4340
gmw_00012015.overview: NGC 4340 is a barred S0 galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0501163] that features a strong central bar [arXiv:astro-ph/0501163] with pronounced major-axis profile shoulders [arXiv:2303.04828]. The galaxy hosts a fast-rotating pseudobulge [arXiv:astro-ph/0501163] whose formation may have been influenced by the bar [arXiv:astro-ph/0501163].

sources.summary: The paper uses NGC 4340 as an observed barred-galaxy example with pronounced major-axis profile shoulders, comparing its Spitzer bar profile and shoulder length to simulated bar-shoulder models.

quotes.quote: Fig. fig:Sigma_1D_examples shows examples of bar major-axis profiles for the galaxies NGC 3681 and NGC 4340 from S^4G Sheth_2010 and for the simulation SD1 (described in Sec. sec:sims ) at 7 Gyr (dotted black/red) and at 10Gyr (dash-dotted blue/orange). The bars are aligned with the x axis and for the simulation we select all star-particles within |y|/ <0.145 , where is the bar length -- see Sec. sec:sims for details. For the observed galaxies, we show one-pixel-wide profiles along the bar major axis from Spitzer 3.6 images. NGC 3681 has a pure-exponential profile, while NGC 4340 and the model SD1 have profiles with shoulders (red), as detected by Anderson2022 . The galaxy NGC 4340 was chosen as an example of a bar profile with particularly pronounced shoulders, and these look much more pronounced than the ones in the model SD1 at 7Gyr.

In fact, we see that the shoulders of SD1 get stronger at 10Gyr (blue/orange dash-dotted lines), although its length (in units of ) is approximately constant and smaller than in the observed galaxy NGC 4340.

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 4340 as a strong-barred SB0 whose bulge is a reliable pseudobulge and rotates faster than classical bulges and oblate models.

quotes.quote: The bulges of NGC 1308 and NGC 4340 rotate faster than bulges of unbarred galaxies and models of isotropic oblate spheroids with equal ellipticity.

This is not the case of the bulges of NGC 1308 and NGC 4340 which are rotating significantly faster than oblate spheroid models and are more similar to the triaxial SB0 bulges studied by Kormendy (1982).

We find that the bulges of IC 874, NGC 1380 and NGC 4340 are the most reliable pseudobulges in our sample. The bulges of NGC 1308 and NGC 4340 are more rotation-dominated than classical bulges and oblate spheroid models in the V_ max / _ 0 - diagram (e.g., Kormendy Kennicutt 2004). The bulge of IC 874 is a low- outlier in the FJ relation and it is outside the 3 band in the FP of elliptical galaxies and S0 bulges. Since IC 874, NGC 1308 and NGC 4340 host the strongest bars of the sample (Table tab:bar ) we could speculate that the formation of their bulges has been influenced by the bar.

gmw_00012016.name: IC 874
gmw_00012016.overview: IC 874 is an S0 galaxy that hosts a strong stellar bar [arXiv:astro-ph/0501163]. The galaxy contains a pseudobulge whose formation may have been influenced by this bar structure [arXiv:astro-ph/0501163].
