This large apparent size is not explained by a projected companion galaxy or visible tidal features, suggesting its associated redshift may be incorrect [arXiv:2109.03345].

sources.summary: The paper flags SMDG0014598+023448 as a UDG candidate with unrealistically large effective radius, finds no projected companion or tidal-feature explanation, and suggests its weak H I redshift may be wrong.

quotes.quote: First, the first system in the Table (SMDG0014598+023448) appears to have an unrealistically large value for r_e . This could indicate that the associated redshift is for a second galaxy seen in projection. Upon inspection, there is no such system within 5 \ of the source. The large apparent size could also indicate a tidal nature for the source. Tidal features are often identified as low surface brightness galaxy candidates and can have large extents kadowaki21 .
This does not appear to be the case here either. We revisited the GALFIT modeling and that appears to be fine as well. It is possible that the redshift is incorrect.

gmw_00012073.name: J074610.50+230710.8
gmw_00012073.overview: J074610.50+230710.8 is a red, compact, radio-loud broad absorption line quasar [arXiv:0907.2251]. It exhibits strong C IV absorption, enhanced high-velocity absorption structures, and a radio spectrum that peaks near 5 GHz [arXiv:0907.2251].

sources.summary: The paper describes J074610.50+230710.8 as a red, compact BAL radio-loud quasar with strong C IV absorption, enhanced high-velocity BAL absorption, and a radio spectrum peaking near 5 GHz.

quotes.quote: J074610.50+230710.8 has a relatively large C IV absorption index of AI=2955 km s ^ 1 (Trump et al. 2006), and has wide and deep BAL-like absorption structure (Figures 3 and 6) despite being the only snapshot BAL RLQ not included in the Gibson et al. (2009) BAL quasar catalog. The HET spectrum shows enhanced absorption in the higher-velocity BAL (Figure 6), perhaps qualitatively consistent with the tendency of BAL RQQs to vary within narrow discrete regions (Gibson et al. 2008b). It is the reddest snapshot BAL RLQ with (g-i) 1.1 (the next reddest snapshot BAL RLQ has (g-i) 0.6 ). An archival VLA X-band image (program AB862, observation date 1998-05-04) suggests the radio spectrum of this compact-morphology source peaks near 5 GHz.

gmw_00012074.name: J083749.59+364145.4
gmw_00012074.overview: J083749.59+364145.4 is a radio-loud quasar characterized by particularly strong ultraviolet broad absorption lines [arXiv:0907.2251]. It exhibits a compact, possibly variable GHz-peaked radio spectrum and possesses the largest carbon IV equivalent width in its sample [arXiv:0907.2251].

sources.summary: The paper highlights J083749.59+364145.4 for especially strong UV broad absorption lines, the sample's largest C IV equivalent width, and a compact GHz-peaked radio spectrum.

quotes.quote: J083749.59+364145.4 has particularly strong C IV, Si IV, Ly , and O VI BALs. The C IV EW of 34.6 is the largest in the snapshot or archival BAL RLQ sample. It appears to have a GHz-peaked (possibly variable) radio spectral shape and is unresolved at milliarcsecond scales (Montenegro-Montes et al. 2008, 2009).

gmw_00012075.name: J112506.95 001647.6
gmw_00012075.overview: J112506.95 001647.6 is a quasar resolved into a double-lobed morphology by FIRST [arXiv:0907.2251]. It features a low-velocity primary C IV absorption trough that splits its emission line, but it does not show extended X-ray emission [arXiv:0907.2251].

sources.summary: The paper describes J112506.95 001647.6 as a double-lobed FIRST source with no extended Chandra X-ray emission and a low-velocity C IV absorption trough splitting the emission line.

quotes.quote: J112506.95 001647.6 is resolved into a double-lobed morphology by FIRST (de Vries et al. 2006; see Figure 7). The Chandra image does not show any extended X-ray emission; there are 80 X-ray source counts. The primary C IV absorption trough is at low velocity and splits the emission line.

gmw_00012076.name: J123411.73+615832.6
gmw_00012076.overview: J123411.73+615832.6 is a broad-absorption line quasar [arXiv:0907.2251]. The object exhibits an atypical broad-absorption line structure with a deep, wide trough, narrow redshifted C IV absorption, and a radio spectral index near 0.5 [arXiv:0907.2251].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J123411.73+615832.6 as having an atypical deep BAL trough, narrow redshifted C IV absorption, and a VLA radio spectral index near 0.5.

quotes.quote: J123411.73+615832.6 has an atypical BAL structure, with a deep and wide trough that decreases gradually in depth until smoothly meeting the base of the Si IV emission line. Narrow redshifted C IV absorption is also present. A C-band VLA image (program AP450, observation date 2003-02-27) indicates the radio spectral index is _ 0.5 .

gmw_00012077.name: J020022.01 084512.0
gmw_00012077.overview: J020022.01 084512.0 is a broad-absorption line quasar [arXiv:0907.2251]. It is classified as a radio-loud source, with a radio-loudness value that places it on the border for inclusion in archival samples [arXiv:0907.2251].

sources.summary: The paper treats J020022.01 084512.0 as an archival BAL radio-loud quasar, observed serendipitously with Chandra ACIS-I and lying near the survey radio-loudness threshold.

quotes.quote: J020022.01 084512.0 (also FBQS J0200 0845) was identified as a BAL RLQ by Becker et al. (2001). It was observed serendipitously in an 18 ks ACIS-I image (ObsID 3265; PI Ebeling) and is discussed by Gallagher et al. (2005). The source has a radio-loudness value of R^ * =48 , on the border for inclusion in the archival sample.

gmw_00012078.name: FBQS J0256 0119
gmw_00012078.overview: FBQS J0256 0119 is a broad-absorption line, radio-loud quasar [arXiv:0907.2251]. It exhibits a steep radio spectrum that may be variable, alongside a soft but X-ray-weak spectrum [arXiv:0907.2251].

sources.summary: The paper describes FBQS J0256 0119 as a BAL radio-loud quasar with a steep, possibly variable radio spectrum and a soft but X-ray-weak Chandra detection.

quotes.quote: FBQS J0256 0119 was identified as a BAL RLQ by Becker et al. (2001). Flux measurements by Montenegro-Montes et al. (2008) indicate a steep radio spectrum; those authors also note the increased flux measured by FIRST relative to NVSS may be due to variability. The 5 ks ACIS-S observation shows FBQS J0256 0119 to be X-ray weak but with a soft spectrum (Brotherton et al. 2005). We do not have access to photometric magnitudes for this object, so we take the lack of intrinsic reddening noted by Brotherton et al. (2005) as justification to set the relative color (g-i)=0 .

gmw_00012079.name: J081426.45+364713.5
gmw_00012079.overview: J081426.45+364713.5 is a low-radio-luminosity, very red broad-absorption line quasar [arXiv:0907.2251]. Its optical spectrum displays weak emission lines alongside deep and wide absorption lines, and the object is characterized as X-ray weak [arXiv:0907.2251].

sources.summary: The paper describes J081426.45+364713.5 as a low-radio-luminosity, very red BAL quasar with FeLoBAL-like absorption, weak emission lines, and X-ray weakness in Chandra exposures.

quotes.quote: J081426.45+364713.5 has a low radio luminosity ( l_ =32.7 ) and the reddest relative color [ (g-i)=1.1 ] in the snapshot or archival BAL RLQ sample. The optical spectrum shows deep and wide BALs in both high and low-ionization lines (Trump et al. 2006 categorize it as an FeLoBAL) and only weak emission lines. It was observed serendipitously in two 10 ks ACIS-I exposures (ObsID 3436, 3437; PI Fox) and is X-ray weak.

gmw_00012080.name: J091951.29+005854.9
gmw_00012080.overview: J091951.29+005854.9 is a borderline radio-loud broad-absorption line quasar [arXiv:0907.2251]. The galaxy features a relatively narrow C IV broad-absorption line and was not detected during a 5 ks Chandra X-ray observation [arXiv:0907.2251].

sources.summary: The paper describes J091951.29+005854.9 as a borderline radio-loud BAL quasar with a relatively narrow C IV BAL and no detection in a 5 ks Chandra observation.

quotes.quote: J091951.29+005854.9 has a non-zero BI=673.1 km s ^ 1 and a C IV EW of 6.9 , but the BAL is relatively narrow and the absorption index is low ( AI=1268 km s ^ 1 ). The radio loudness is also borderline for our sample ( R^ * =51 ). It was observed serendipitously in a 5 ks ACIS-S image (ObsID 7056; PI Murray) but is not detected.

gmw_00012081.name: J131213.57+231958.6
gmw_00012081.overview: J131213.57+231958.6 is a radio-loud broad-absorption line quasar [arXiv:0907.2251] that exhibits a deep C IV absorption trough [arXiv:0907.2251]. The source is core-dominated with two-sided extended radio emission [arXiv:0907.2251], shows likely variability [arXiv:0907.2251], and is characterized as X-ray weak [arXiv:0907.2251].

sources.summary: The paper describes J131213.57+231958.6 as a BAL radio-loud quasar with a deep C IV trough, two-sided extended radio emission, CSS-like traits, and an X-ray-weak Chandra spectrum.

quotes.quote: J131213.57+231958.6 (also FBQS J131213.5+231958) was identified as a BAL RLQ by Becker et al. (2000) and shows a wide and deep C IV absorption trough that extends to 25000 km s ^ 1 (from FBQS data; the DR7 SDSS spectrum does not have sufficient short wavelength coverage to see the BAL). It shows two-sided extended radio emission on milliarcsecond scales but is core dominated (Jiang et al. 2003) and likely variable (Montenegro-Montes et al. 2008); these radio characteristics do not provide a self-consistent orientation measure. Liu et al. (2008) suggest this object is similar in some respects to CSS sources. The 5 ks ACIS-S observation (Brotherton et al. 2005) shows it to be X-ray weak but with an X-ray spectrum actually somewhat softer than is typical of non-BAL RLQs.

gmw_00012082.name: LBQS 2211 1915
gmw_00012082.overview: LBQS 2211 1915 is a radio-loud quasar [arXiv:0907.2251] that has been identified as a marginal broad-absorption line quasar [arXiv:0907.2251]. Observations show that the object is X-ray weak [arXiv:0907.2251] and exhibits a moderately red relative color [arXiv:0907.2251].

sources.summary: The paper describes LBQS 2211 1915 as a marginal BAL quasar that is radio-loud from NVSS, X-ray weak in a Chandra observation, and moderately red in relative color.

quotes.quote: LBQS 2211 1915 was identified as a ``marginal'' BAL quasar by Weymann et al. (1991), with a non-zero but low BI=27 km s ^ 1 , and is radio-loud based on an NVSS flux measurement. The 6 ks ACIS-S observation (Gallagher et al. 2006) shows it to be X-ray weak relative to non-BAL RLQs. We estimate the relative color to be (g-i)=0.32 based on data from Gallagher et al. (2007).

gmw_00012083.name: Source 1
gmw_00012083.overview: Source 1 is a highly bright submillimeter galaxy that is gravitationally lensed [arXiv:1003.0827]. Its spectral energy distribution is comparable to template starburst galaxies, and its multiple images are subject to large magnification due to its proximity to a caustic line [arXiv:1003.0827].

sources.summary: The paper identifies Source 1 as an exceptionally bright lensed SMG whose multiple images receive large magnification and whose SED is compared with starburst galaxy templates.

quotes.quote: Source 1: With a deboosted flux density of 48.0 1.3 mJy, this source is one of the brightest SMGs ever detected around 870 m . This is very likely because of its proximity to a caustic line, which provides a large magnification. From their lensing model, estimated that the two brighter images of the galaxy, A and B, which are separated by 8.6'' , have a magnification of 25 and 50, whereas the third image, C, located 47'' away from the first one, would have a magnification four times lower than the first one. Our final map was smoothed to 22'' and our fit to the Source 1 gives a position between the images A and B; the flux that we measure comes most likely from those two images. The total magnification is therefore 75.

Figure fig:sed shows the spectral energy distribution of Source 1. The measured data points are from from Spitzer, BLAST, LABOCA and AzTEC. We compared the models of of template starburst galaxies to those data points.

gmw_00012084.name: Source 2
gmw_00012084.overview: Source 2 is a high-redshift infrared galaxy detected as a submillimeter source [arXiv:1003.0827]. It has likely counterparts in all available Spitzer bands, with a potential northern source that may cause an apparent elongation in its emission [arXiv:1003.0827].

sources.summary: The paper reports Source 2 as the second-most significant LABOCA detection, likely also seen in earlier AzTEC data, with candidate Spitzer counterparts in all available bands.

quotes.quote: Source 2: This is our second most significant detection at Gaussian filter value 29.9 or signal-to-noise ratio S/N 15. No previous mm or submm detection of this source has been reported, although in the map showing the detection of Source 1 in W08 (their Fig. 1) there is an indication that they also detected this source. Fig. fig:post shows that this galaxy has likely counterparts in all available Spitzer bands. There is a source to the north of the center of the LABOCA detection in the 3.6 and 4.5 m maps that might be the reason for the apparent elongation of the red contour towards the north. However, this source is outside the 10 search radius and does not show up in the 24 m map.

gmw_00012085.name: ESDLA towards QSO SDSS J1143 + 1420
gmw_00012085.overview: The ESDLA towards QSO SDSS J1143 + 1420 is an extremely strong damped Lyman-alpha absorber located at a redshift of 2.323 [arXiv:2205.03088]. It lies in close spatial proximity to its associated galaxy and contains signatures of dust, diffuse molecular hydrogen, and polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons [arXiv:2205.03088].

sources.summary: The paper models the z=2.323 ESDLA near its associated galaxy, inferring PAHs indirectly from CLOUDY fits to warm H2 and C I and finding a weak 2175 bump.

quotes.quote: We look at the optical VLT/X-shooter observations of an intervening, extremely strong damped Lyman- absorber (or ESDLA, with log ( N (H I) 21.7)) towards QSO SDSS J1143 + 1420 at redshift, z_ ESDLA = 2.323. Literature studies have shown that this ESDLA contains signatures of dust and diffuse molecular hydrogen and it was specifically chosen for our study due to its close spatial proximity (impact parameter, = 0.6 0.3 kpc) with its associated galaxy. There is no direct detection of PAHs emission in the limited observations of infrared(IR)-spectra along this sight-line. Hence, we use CLOUDY numerical simulation modelling to indirectly probe the presence of PAH in the ESDLA. We note that PAHs need to be included in the models to reproduce the observed column densities of warm H _2 and C I.

Thus, we infer the presence of PAHs indirectly in our ESDLA, with an abundance of PAH/H = 10 ^ 7.046 . We also measure a low 2175 bump strength (E _ bump 0.03 - 0.19 mag) relative to star-forming galaxies by modelling extinction of QSO spectra by dust at the absorber rest-frame. This is consistent with the low PAH abundance obtained indirectly using CLOUDY modelling.

gmw_00012086.name: W0410-0913
gmw_00012086.overview: W0410-0913 is a highly luminous, gas-rich galaxy located at a redshift of z = 3.631 [arXiv:2208.03248]. It features highly attenuated ultraviolet emission and fast, active galactic nuclei-driven outflows [arXiv:2208.03248].

sources.summary: The paper studies W0410-0913 as an extremely FIR-luminous Hot DOG at z=3.631, finding attenuated UV emission, fast AGN-driven outflows, and mass build-up in a dense circumgalactic environment without robust merger signs.

quotes.quote: In this work, we report rest-frame UV and rest-frame fir-infrared (FIR) spectroscopic imaging observations of the Hot DOG WISE J041010.60-0913056.2 (hereafter W0410-0913), obtained with the integral field spectrograph Multi Unit Spectroscopic Explorer (MUSE) at the Very Large Telescope (VLT), and with the Atacama Large Millimeter/Sub-millimeter Array (ALMA), respectively. W0410-0913 was originally selected from a WISE all-sky survey and, with a total FIR luminosity of L_ FIR 2 10^ 14   L_ , and with large stellar ( M_ star ) and molecular gas ( M_ gas ) masses of   10^ 11   M_ , is one of the brightest, most massive and gas-rich galaxies in the distant Universe . Extensive rest-frame FIR observations, tracing several molecular lines, have shown W0410-0913 to be located at a systemic redshift of z = 3.631 (see details on the redshift determination in the subsection ALMA data in Methods), about only 1.7  Gyr after the Big Bang.

The spectrum of W0410-0913 observed with MUSE shows highly attenuated rest-frame UV emission. We detect very broad (full widths at half maxima of about FWHM 2800   km   s^ 1 ) blueshifted components of 1240 and 1548,1550, both offset by more than 2000   km   s^ 1 with respect to the systemic redshift, likely tracing fast AGN-driven outflows . Also, we find a narrow ( FWHM 400   km   s^ 1 ) Lyman- line, which, similarly to the narrow component of the optical 5007 line , is blueshifted by about 1400   km   s^ 1 .

Data support that the mass build-up of W0410-0913 takes place in a dense circumgalactic environment, but we cannot identify robust signs of merger that could sustain the growth of the massive galaxy. Instead, we find that gravitational encounters, if any, can have only a modest impact on shaping the morpho-kinematic properties of the ISM.

gmw_00012087.name: SDSS J1241+2602
gmw_00012087.overview: SDSS J1241+2602 is classified as a Type-1.9 active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2311.05138]. Evidence suggests that this galaxy hosts unobscured broad-line regions, as its virial black hole mass is consistent with the mass derived from its host-galaxy stellar velocity dispersion [arXiv:2311.05138].

sources.summary: The paper argues that SDSS J1241+2602, a Type-1.9 AGN, has unobscured broad-line regions because its host-galaxy velocity dispersion gives a black-hole mass consistent with the virial estimate.

quotes.quote: In this manuscript, strong evidence is reported to support unobscured broad line regions (BLRs) in Type-1.9 AGN SDSS J1241+2602 with reliable broad H but no broad H . Commonly, disappearance of broad H can be explained by the AGN unified model expected heavily obscured BLRs in Type-1.9 AGN. Here, based on properties of two kinds of BH masses, the virial BH mass and the BH mass through the relation, an independent method is proposed to test whether are there unobscured central BLRs in a Type-1.9 AGN. By the reliable measurement of stellar velocity dispersion about 110 12km/s through the host galaxy absorption features in , the BH mass through the relation is consistent with the virial BH mass (3.43 1.25) 10^7 M_ determined through properties of the observed broad H without considering effects of obscurations in SDSS J1241+2602.

Meanwhile, if considering heavily obscured BLRs in SDSS J1241+2602, the reddening corrected virial BH mass is tens of times larger than the expected value, leading SDSS J1241+2602 to be an outlier in the space with confidence level higher than 5 . Therefore, the unobscured BLRs are preferred in the Type-1.9 AGN SDSS J1241+2602.

gmw_00012088.name: NGC 3949
gmw_00012088.overview: The galaxy NGC 3949 features a clear central source at its nucleus [arXiv:astro-ph/0503693]. This central region exhibits a surface-brightness excess that cannot be accounted for by a Nuker profile alone, leading to its classification as a point-like source [arXiv:astro-ph/0503693].

sources.summary: The paper identifies a clear central source and a surface-brightness excess in NGC 3949, and says S04 would classify it as point-like.

quotes.quote: The presence of central sources seems clear for NGC 2748, NGC 3949, NGC 4030 and NGC 4303, where a well sampled The surface brightness profile for NGC 157 also shows an excess above the Nuker fit, but in this analysis the excess is defined by a single point only.
Consequently, if we applied the S04 definition NGC3949 and NGC4303 would also be classified as point sources in Table quantify_source .
In 4 cases; NGC 2748, NGC 3949, NGC 4030 and NGC 4303 there appears to be an excess which can not be accounted for by the Nuker profile alone.

gmw_00012089.name: HUCD1
gmw_00012089.overview: HUCD1 is an ultracompact dwarf galaxy with a luminosity and mass comparable to the most massive ultracompact dwarfs in the Virgo and Fornax clusters [arXiv:1103.5463]. It is highly compact with a half-light radius of 25.4 pc and shows weak indications of a faint halo [arXiv:1103.5463].

sources.summary: HUCD1 is described as the brightest object in the sample, comparable to the most massive Virgo/Fornax UCDs, unusually compact, and showing a weak halo indication.

quotes.quote: With M_V=-13.37 mag, HUCD1 is the brightest object in our sample.

Hence, HUCD1 has a luminosity/mass comparable to the most massive UCDs in Virgo and Fornax (VUCD7 and UCD3), but with its half-light radius of r_ h =25.4 pc, it is the most compact object among the highest luminosity UCDs.
A weak indication for a faint halo is observed for HUCD1, but not for the other two luminous UCDs.

gmw_00012090.name: J0331
gmw_00012090.overview: J0331 is a far-infrared-bright quasar host galaxy [arXiv:1612.00010]. It lacks an interacting companion, suggesting it may be observed in the final coalescence stage of a major merger [arXiv:1612.00010].

sources.summary: J0331 is a FIR-bright quasar host whose ALMA SED and SFR are below Herschel estimates, making it the outlier among FIR-bright systems without a companion.

quotes.quote: For the third FIR-bright object, J0331, the luminosities expected from the ALMA-based SEDs are significantly lower, by factors of about 2 and 3, than the fluxes observed with \ for the gray-body and CE01 SEDs, respectively.
The only outlier is the FIR-bright system J0331, where the ALMA-based FIR SED and SFR estimates are found to be considerably lower than the based ones.

The only way in which all the quasars in our sample can be explained as triggered by major merger is if the FIR-bright systems that lack an interacting companion (namely J0331 and J1341) are observed in the final coalescence stage, while the two interacting FIR-faint quasar hosts have already experienced a close first passage that triggered the SMBH growth and are observed close to their apocenter.

gmw_00012091.name: J1341
gmw_00012091.overview: J1341 is a far-infrared-bright quasar host galaxy that lacks a detected interacting companion [arXiv:1612.00010]. It is characterized by a high gas mass of 6.6 billion solar masses, which comprises 60 percent of its total mass, and an extremely high black-hole-to-host mass ratio [arXiv:1612.00010].

sources.summary: J1341 is presented as an exceptional FIR-bright quasar host with high black-hole mass, high BH-to-host ratio, notable gas fraction, and no detected companion.

quotes.quote: For most of the systems, and particularly the quasar hosts, the gas masses comprise <20 of \ and reach 60 in only one quasar host (J1341).
We finally highlight the exceptional properties of the J1341 system, which has a high of =6.6 10^ 9 , an extremely high BH-to-host mass ratio of 1/10 and a low mass-growth ratio of 1/190 .

The only way in which all the quasars in our sample can be explained as triggered by major merger is if the FIR-bright systems that lack an interacting companion (namely J0331 and J1341) are observed in the final coalescence stage, while the two interacting FIR-faint quasar hosts have already experienced a close first passage that triggered the SMBH growth and are observed close to their apocenter.

gmw_00012092.name: J1511
gmw_00012092.overview: J1511 is a far-infrared-bright quasar host galaxy [arXiv:1612.00010]. Observations reveal two submillimeter-emitting companion galaxies within four arcseconds, one of which appears to be physically interacting while the more distant companion may be a projected chance association [arXiv:1612.00010, arXiv:2305.05173].

sources.summary: J1511 is a FIR-bright quasar host with two submillimeter companions; the paper argues one is interacting while the more distant source may be projected.

quotes.quote: For one of the FIR-bright sources, J1511, the ALMA data reveal two faint sub-mm-emitting apparent companions, which are detected at the 7-8 level in continuum emission.
This may suggest that the most distant source seen in the ALMA maps of J1511 is a projected companion source, not physically related with the J1511 system.
This latter interpretation is further justified by our findings of clearly interacting companions, both for J1511 and two of the FIR-faint quasar systems, as described below.

sources.summary: SDSS J151155.98+040802.9 has two band-7 submillimeter companions within 4 arcseconds; one appears interacting, while the other is likely a chance association or minor merger.

quotes.quote: SDSS J151155.98+040802.9 was observed by in band 7 and was confirmed to have two sub-mm companions within 4 of the main quasar. Both are clearly visible in the continuum image in Fig. fig:companions . It was concluded that the closer companion was an interacting companion, while the further companion was likely to be a chance association or a minor merger.

gmw_00012093.name: J0923
gmw_00012093.overview: J0923 is a far-infrared-faint quasar host galaxy [arXiv:1612.00010]. It has an interacting companion submillimeter galaxy detected in the continuum, and its emission lines exhibit a blue shift [arXiv:1612.00010].

sources.summary: J0923 is a FIR-faint quasar host with a continuum-detected companion, blue-shifted line emission, and a lower line-to-FIR ratio than its companion SMG.

quotes.quote: We identify two additional emission sources in the maps of two of the FIR-faint sources, J0923 and J1328 -- one around each of these quasars -- which are clearly detected in the ALMA continuum maps.
Interestingly, the only sources presenting \ blue shifts, of 220 and 630 , are the two FIR-faint sources, J0923 and J1328 respectively, which have interacting SMGs.
Specifically, we find that the \ ratio in the quasar hosts of J1511, J0923, and J1328 is lower by factors of about 1.7, 3 and 2.9, respectively, compared with the companion SMGs.

gmw_00012094.name: J1328
gmw_00012094.overview: J1328 is a far-infrared-faint quasar host galaxy [arXiv:1612.00010]. It has an interacting companion submillimeter galaxy detected in the continuum, and its emission lines exhibit a blue shift of 630 kilometers per second [arXiv:1612.00010].

sources.summary: J1328 is a FIR-faint quasar host with a continuum-detected companion, blue-shifted line emission, and a lower line-to-FIR ratio than its companion SMG.

quotes.quote: We identify two additional emission sources in the maps of two of the FIR-faint sources, J0923 and J1328 -- one around each of these quasars -- which are clearly detected in the ALMA continuum maps.
Interestingly, the only sources presenting \ blue shifts, of 220 and 630 , are the two FIR-faint sources, J0923 and J1328 respectively, which have interacting SMGs.
Specifically, we find that the \ ratio in the quasar hosts of J1511, J0923, and J1328 is lower by factors of about 1.7, 3 and 2.9, respectively, compared with the companion SMGs.

gmw_00012095.name: J0935
gmw_00012095.overview: J0935 is a far-infrared-faint quasar host galaxy [arXiv:1612.00010]. It exhibits the weakest line emission among its class and has no companion galaxies out to a distance of 50 kiloparsecs [arXiv:1612.00010].

sources.summary: J0935 is a FIR-faint quasar host with the weakest line emission among the hosts, redshifted ISM emission, and no companions out to 50 kpc.

quotes.quote: All \ quasar hosts are clearly detected in our new ALMA data, both in continuum and in \ line emission, with the least significant \ detection having S/N 4.5 (for J0935; see tab:lum_cii ).
The only obvious outlier is the host of J0935, where the \ emission is weaker.
Even this scenario fails to account for one of the FIR-faint systems (J0935), which has no companions out to 50 .

gmw_00012096.name: MRC 0225--065
gmw_00012096.overview: MRC 0225--065 is a young compact symmetric radio galaxy located at a redshift of 0.445 [arXiv:2301.00977]. High-resolution imaging resolves the active galactic nucleus into a bright central core and two symmetric lobes spanning a projected linear size of 430 parsecs [arXiv:2301.00977].

sources.summary: The paper images MRC 0225--065 with VLBI, resolving it as a young compact symmetric object with a bright core, two lobes, 430 pc extent, and an SSA peaked-spectrum classification.

quotes.quote: MRC 0225--065 (GLEAM J022744-062106) was initially identified as variable in R21 but further monitoring over a year found no evidence of variability (R22). As such, it was predicted MRC 0225--065 would have resolved structures on milliarcsecond scales with a compact feature 25 mas, resulting in variability from refractive interstellar scintillation (RISS) on a longer timescale with a dampened modulation index due to the extended structure.

We resolve MRC 0225--065 into three components at both 2.4 GHz and 8.3 GHz: a bright central region containing 50 of the total flux density, and two fainter regions roughly equal distance from the central region. In R21 and R22, MRC 0225--065 was found to show low levels of variability with a constant spectral shape, and presented as showing variability due to ISS from a compact morphology with resolved structure on mas scales. We find the projected linear size to be 430 pc, using a spectroscopic redshift of 0.445. Using spectral modelling, we calculate the magnetic field assuming a purely SSA model, and find it is in agreement with the magnetic field calculated assuming equipartition. We therefore conclude MRC 0225--065 is a young CSO, with a PS classification due to SSA.

gmw_00012097.name: PMN J0322--4820
gmw_00012097.overview: PMN J0322--4820 is an active galactic nucleus that is likely a blazar [arXiv:2301.00977]. It exhibits a variable spectral shape [arXiv:2301.00977, arXiv:2012.01842] and displays a compact radio morphology on milliarcsecond scales [arXiv:2301.00977].

sources.summary: The paper identifies PMN J0322--4820 as a likely blazar: its spectral shape varied, VLBI left it compact on milliarcsecond scales, and its size-turnover position disfavors a genuine peaked-spectrum source.

quotes.quote: Conversely, PMN J0322--4820 (GLEAM J032237--482010) was selected due to the variable spectral shape identified in R21. To explain the variable spectral shape, R21 concluded PMN J0322--4820 was likely a blazar caught flaring in 2014. As such, it was predicted to show a compact morphology even on milliarcsecond scales.

Radio morphology: The high resolution image of PMN J0322--4820 using the LBA found it was still compact on mas scales. This is also entirely consistent with a blazar morphology, which appears compact due to orientation effects. Linear size and turnover relation: PMN J0322--4820 sits well below the linear size and turnover relation typically associated with PS sources. This could either be because it is a frustrated source and is thus more compact than expected for it's predicted age. However, more likely, is that the temporary peak detected with the MWA in 2014 was a result of the variability of a blazar with effects like Doppler boosting influencing measurements and thus the spectral peak is unrelated to the source age or absorption mechanisms.

sources.summary: The paper highlights GLEAM J032237--482010 as a radio source with strong spectral-shape variability, a SUMSS flux density of 0.5 Jy, and an AT20G non-detection that complicates a blazar interpretation.

quotes.quote: We note one source in particular, GLEAM J032237--482010, has a significant change in spectral shape (MOSS 90) yet has no AT20G counterpart. GLEAM J032237--482010 does have a flux density of 0.5 Jy at 840 MHz according to SUMSS. If GLEAM J032237--482010 is a quasar with a flat spectrum around 0.5 Jy or a blazar with a temporary peak in the SED, the core should be bright enough for an AT20G detection. The non-detection of AT20G suggests this source is not core dominated, contradictory to what the variability suggests. The SED for GLEAM J032237--482010 is presented in Figure fig:blazars .

gmw_00012098.name: MRC 2236-454
gmw_00012098.overview: MRC 2236-454 is a peaked-spectrum radio galaxy and active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2301.00977]. It exhibits variability below 231 MHz and is dominated by a compact feature, which is suggested to be surrounded by an inhomogeneous environment [arXiv:2301.00977].

sources.summary: The paper treats MRC 2236-454 as a variable peaked-spectrum target predicted to show compact asymmetric structure, but VLBI images could not be recovered because pointing offsets put it outside the usable correlated field.

quotes.quote: Finally, MRC 2236-454 (GLEAM J223933--451414) was identified by R21 as the only PS source in their sample that showed significant variability but maintained a constant peak frequency below 231 MHz. A low peak frequency is typically associated with PS sources that are of the order of tens of kilo-parsecs across, but the RISS detected by R22 suggested MRC 2236-454 is dominated by a compact feature, and showed variability due to a surrounding inhomogeneous environment. As such, it was predicted MRC 2236-454 may be resolved on milliarcsecond scales and show an asymmetrical morphology, often associated with frustrated sources in an inhomogeneous surrounding environment 2006A&A...450..959O .

Unfortunately, due to large phase errors from a pointing offset, we were unable to recover images for MRC 2236--454 at either frequency, or for PMN J0322--4820 at 8.3 GHz, this was because the source positions were beyond the observed correlated field of view for recovery in each case. For MRC 2236--454, the pointing offset was over 11 arcseconds for both the 2.4 GHz and 8.3 GHz observations, thus the phase errors from this pointing offset was beyond recovery.

gmw_00012099.name: S331
gmw_00012099.overview: S331 is an active galactic nucleus classified as a type 1 AGN based on its X-ray properties and a strong radio excess [arXiv:1011.2400]. The source is also identified as a compact double-lobed radio galaxy with a radio flux density of 40 mJy [arXiv:1209.4176].

sources.summary: S331 is classified as an AGN from a strong radio excess, a q_24 value in the AGN regime, and X-ray properties suggesting a type 1 AGN.

quotes.quote: S331 This source has been classified as AGN because of its more than tenfold radio excess over the radio-infrared relation. Its value of q_ 24 is -1.96 which clearly puts it into the AGN regime. Its X-ray properties ( Lehmer2005 , Szokoly2004 ) suggest that it is a type 1 AGN.

sources.summary: The paper interprets RID 73's two possible radio-lobe counterparts as parts of one bright central galaxy, arguing its 40 mJy flux fits a compact double-lobed radio galaxy rather than two nearby radio AGN.

quotes.quote: RID 73 (KID 14): there are two possible counterparts for the radio lobes but we believe that they are both associated with the bright central galaxy. One strong indication for this is the strength of the radio flux density, which at 40 mJy is reasonable for a compact double lobed radio galaxy. If separate sources, they would have to both be strong radio AGN very close in projection on the plane of the sky.

gmw_00012100.name: S393
gmw_00012100.overview: S393 is an active galactic nucleus at a redshift of 1.07 [arXiv:1011.2400]. It is classified as a type 2 active galactic nucleus with a steep radio spectrum and a compact, extended structure comparable in size to a gigahertz-peaked spectrum source [arXiv:1011.2400].

sources.summary: S393 is an extended VLBI source at redshift 1.07, classified as a type 2 AGN with a steep radio spectrum and compact GPS-scale structure.

quotes.quote: S393 This is the only VLBI-detected source with a pronounced extension in the VLBI observations. It is an X-ray source at a redshift of 1.07 ( Zheng2004 ), and it has been identified as AGN by because of its X-ray hardness ratio. The criteria result in a type 2 AGN classification, and its radio spectral index is -1.2 ( Kellermann2008 ). Only a very small fraction (5 ) of its arcsec-scale flux density is recovered in the VLBI observations. At its redshift, the angular separation of 32 mas of the two components seen in our VLBI images translate to a projected distance of 263 pc, and is therefore in the regime of GPS sources (and is of a size comparable to the narrow-line region).

gmw_00012101.name: S411
gmw_00012101.overview: S411 is a compact radio source at a redshift of 1.61 [arXiv:1011.2400] that is classified as a type 1 quasar [arXiv:1011.2400] and a luminous, extremely radio-loud active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1207.4478]. It appears as a featureless, round object in Hubble Space Telescope images [arXiv:1011.2400] with a peculiar optical-infrared spectral energy distribution resembling a variable blazar [arXiv:1207.4478].

sources.summary: The paper presents J033210.91-274414.9 as an extremely radio-loud, X-ray-detected luminous AGN whose peculiar power-law optical-NIR SED resembles a highly beamed, variable blazar.

quotes.quote: J033210.91-274414.9 has a spectroscopic redshift of 1.6 and a photometric redshift of 1.86. It is extremely radio-loud, with a value log(R) 3.5 , it is also detected in the X-rays with =1.6 and has an absorption corrected absolute luminosity of 2.38 10^ 44 erg/s, making it one of the most luminous objects in this sample. The object has an absolute magnitude in z of -23.04. The entire SED is shown in Fig. F:Ind_Blazar .
The SED is rather peculiar. The optical-NIR spectrum is a power-law. Such shapes are usually common in blazars: highly beamed, extremely variable AGN urry_unified_1995 .

sources.summary: S411 is a compact VLBI source whose hardness ratio and X-ray luminosity classify it as a type 1 QSO at redshift 1.61.

quotes.quote: S411 This is a compact source -- all arcsec-scale emission is recovered in the VLBI image. Its hardness ratio of -0.44 and X-ray luminosity qualify it as a type 1 QSO ( Luo2008 , Szokoly2004 ) and it is located at a redshift of 1.61 ( Afonso2006 ). It has a spectral index of -0.5 ( Kellermann2008 ), and it is a featureless, round object in the HST ACS images ( Giavalisco2004 ).

gmw_00012102.name: S414
gmw_00012102.overview: S414 is an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1303.6282, arXiv:1011.2400] with a redshift of 1.57 [arXiv:1011.2400] or 0.605 [arXiv:1303.6282]. It is a compact, stochastically varying radio source [arXiv:1303.6282, arXiv:1011.2400] classified as a type 1 quasar based on its high X-ray luminosity [arXiv:1011.2400].

sources.summary: ID 297 has a steep positive radio spectral index, is suggested to be a young GPS-source AGN, and has radio, infrared, and HST properties consistent with an AGN.

quotes.quote: ID 297: This source has a mean 1.4 GHz flux density of 3.60 0.04 mJy. hhl+12 measure a 5.5 GHz flux density of 12.25 mJy, implying a spectral index of +0.89 .

One the basis of this steep positive spectral index, hhl+12 suggest that this is part of a class of Gigahertz Peaked Spectrum (GPS) sources, thought to be a young AGN. The redshift z=0.605 tvg+09 implies a mean radio luminosity of log L_R = 31.5 (cgs). ID 297 appears to be a stochastically varying source with no specific trend in its light-curve (Figure Fig:lightCurves ). The radio position is consistent with a bright, unresolved HST source.

On the basis of departure from radio-FIR correlation naa+06 classify this source as an AGN. The mid-infrared colors (Figure Fig:IRACColors ) and far-infrared spectral index (Table Tab:Variables ) are consistent with this identification.

sources.summary: S414 is a compact source with most ATCA flux recovered by VLBI, an AGN-like q_24 value, and type 1 QSO X-ray luminosity.

quotes.quote: S414 This is a compact source -- the VLBI observations recover 95 of the flux density measured with the ATCA ( Norris2006a ). It has q_ 24 =-0.29 which makes this an AGN. S414 has a redshift of 1.57 ( Mainieri2008 ) and is the brightest X-ray source in our sample, with L_X=1.5 10^ 38 W (0.5 keV -- 8 keV), qualifying it as a type 1 QSO ( Lehmer2005 , Szokoly2004 ).

gmw_00012103.name: S423
gmw_00012103.overview: S423 is an elliptical galaxy [arXiv:1011.2400] at a redshift of 0.73 [arXiv:1011.2400] that hosts a low-luminosity active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1303.6282]. The host galaxy exhibits star-forming activity [arXiv:1303.6282, arXiv:1011.2400] and is potentially merging with a closely separated companion [arXiv:1303.6282, arXiv:1011.2400].

sources.summary: ID 337 is a repeatedly outbursting radio source whose optical counterpart is associated with a fainter galaxy, with evidence for a low-luminosity AGN in a star-forming host.

quotes.quote: ID 337: This source has a mean 1.4 GHz flux density of 494 11 Jy. From its radio light-curve, ID 337 appears to have repeated outbursts roughly every 25d.

The photometric redshift of z=0.734 vcd+08 implies a mean radio luminosity of log L_R = 30.8 (cgs). The HST image (Figure Fig:cutouts ) shows that the optical counterpart to the radio source is actually associated with the fainter (or more extincted) galaxy among a group of two closely separated galaxies.

The spectral index and radio luminosity argue that ID 337 is a low-luminosity AGN. amk+06 state that the bright radio source is a luminous star-forming galaxy, possibly part of a merging system. Comparison of the K-corrected spectral luminosities, L_ (5.8 m ) = 4.0 10^9 L_ and L_ (24 m ) < 1.1 10^ 10 L_ , with the SED templates and , together with the mid-infrared colors (Figure Fig:IRACColors ) advocate the star-forming nature of the host galaxy.

sources.summary: S423 is classified as a normal or elliptical galaxy with star-forming/merger evidence; only the VLBI detection suggests an AGN, with Seyfert-like radio luminosity.

quotes.quote: S423 This compact radio source is classified as a normal galaxy using the scheme by . It has a spectral index of -0.2 ( Kellermann2008 ) and a redshift of 0.73 ( Afonso2006 ). Afonso et al. report spectroscopic evidence for star-forming activity in this galaxy, potentially merging with a nearby ( 0.5'' ) object. They identify five objects closer than 30'' with similar redshifts. classify this object as an elliptical galaxy, using photometry in 17 bands.

gmw_00012104.name: S443
gmw_00012104.overview: S443 is an Sbc spiral galaxy located at a redshift of 0.076 [arXiv:1207.4478, arXiv:1011.2400]. It features a central point source [arXiv:1207.4478] and exhibits weak X-ray emission that may originate from star-forming activity north of the nucleus [arXiv:1207.4478, arXiv:1011.2400].

sources.summary: The paper describes J033229.99-274404.8 as a low-redshift, extended radio-detected spiral with a central point source, low AGN contribution, and weak X-ray detection.

quotes.quote: Two of our objects (J033229.88-274424.4 and J033229.99-274404.8 ) are clearly extended spiral galaxies at very low redshift (both objects have a spectroscopic redshift of 0.076). They show clear extended spiral galaxy patterns as well as clear central point sources. Both objects have radii of about 2 arcseconds. Both objects are detected in radio but are radio-quiet ( R 2-3 ). Given the fact that the AGN contribution to the overall flux in these objects is lower than 10 , this is a clear lower limit and the AGN itself is radio-loud in both objects.

Due to the small contribution from the central point source, the X-ray emission is so weak that of those two object, only one (J033229.99-274404.8) is detected in the X-rays, with a low X-ray luminosity of only 1.3 10^ 40 erg/s and =1.6 .

sources.summary: S443 is the faintest VLBI-detected source, associated with an Sbc spiral galaxy without AGN indicators; the paper treats the detection as marginal and possibly a radio supernova.

quotes.quote: S443 This compact object is the faintest VLBI-detected radio source in our survey, with an arcsec-scale flux density of only 0.4 mJy ( Norris2006a ). It is associated with a spiral galaxy, with a spectral index of -0.5 ( Kellermann2008 ) and a redshift of 0.076 ( Afonso2006 ). Its q_ 24 value of 1.05 is broadly consistent with the value of 0.84 found by for the radio-infrared relation, and also its X-ray properties suggest that it is a galaxy without AGN. report that the X-ray emission comes from north of the galaxy nucleus, with indications of star-forming activity. classify it as an Sbc object based on 18-band photometry.

gmw_00012105.name: S447
gmw_00012105.overview: S447 is an active galactic nucleus and type 1 quasar located at a spectroscopic redshift of 1.96 [arXiv:1011.2400]. It is a complex radio source featuring a clear core, two radio lobes, and other potentially associated components [arXiv:1209.4176].

sources.summary: S447 is an AGN/QSO at redshift 1.96, unresolved by VLBI, with most flux from under 100 pc and the second-highest X-ray luminosity in the sample.

quotes.quote: S447 This source has been classified as AGN based on q_ 24 and also is an X-ray source. It has a spectroscopic redshift of 1.96 ( Norris2006a ), where 1 mas corresponds to 8.5 pc. It is unresolved in our images, hence most of the VLBI flux density (20 of the arcsec-scale flux density) comes from a region smaller than 100 pc. It is the second most luminous X-ray source in our sample of 96 sources, with L_X=1.3 10^ 38 W (0.5 keV -- 8 keV, Lehmer2005 ). Nevertheless it has a soft spectrum with HR=-0.37 which qualifies it as a type 1 QSO ( Szokoly2004 ).

sources.summary: The paper describes RID 500 as a complex radio source with a clear core and two lobes, possible additional components, and an unreliable faint-galaxy counterpart candidate for component 148E.

quotes.quote: RID 500 (KID 148): this is a complex radio source. We identify a clear core and two radio lobes (KID 148A and 148B). There are two other components, KID 148D and 148E, possibly associated with this source. We found a secure counterpart for 148D and so we listed it as a separated source (RID 504). For the 148E component the only counterpart candidate is a faint galaxy at = 03:32:32.59, = 28:03:15.4 with R-mag=23.7, but it is under our reliability threshold and therefore it probably remains unidentified.

gmw_00012106.name: S474
gmw_00012106.overview: S474 is a compact radio source located at a redshift of 0.98 [arXiv:1011.2400]. It displays faint infrared emission and remains undetected in deep X-ray observations, though all of its radio flux is detected in low-resolution VLBI imaging [arXiv:1011.2400].

sources.summary: S474 is a compact redshift 0.98 radio source with all ATCA flux seen by VLBI, faint infrared emission, and no X-ray detection even in deep Chandra data.

quotes.quote: S474 This is a compact source which displays all of its ATCA flux density in a low-resolution VLBI image. Its redshift is 0.98 ( LeFevre2004 ) and its spectral index is -1.4 ( Kellermann2008 ). It is a faint infrared source and is undetected in the X-ray. This is the only VLBI-detected radio source which remained undetected by the 2 Ms Chandra exposure ( Luo2008 ).

gmw_00012107.name: S482
gmw_00012107.overview: S482 is a compact radio and X-ray source located at a redshift of 0.15 [arXiv:1011.2400]. Although its low X-ray luminosity qualifies the object as a galaxy, its infrared-to-radio flux ratio classifies it as an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1011.2400].

sources.summary: S482 is a compact radio and X-ray source whose luminosity qualifies it as a galaxy, while q_24 classifies it as an AGN.

quotes.quote: S482 This is a compact radio source. It is also an X-ray source ( Lehmer2005 ) with hardness ratio -0.33, but its luminosity is only L_X=5.2 10^ 33 W (0.5 keV -- 8 keV), which then qualifies it as a galaxy ( Szokoly2004 ). It was classified by as an AGN based on its q_ 24 of -0.61. It has a spectral index of -0.5 ( Kellermann2008 ) and redshift of 0.15 ( Norris2006a ).

gmw_00012108.name: S500
gmw_00012108.overview: S500 is a compact radio galaxy located at a redshift of 0.73 [arXiv:1011.2400]. It is classified as a type 2 active galactic nucleus based on its absorbed X-ray spectrum and its q_24 index [arXiv:1011.2400].

sources.summary: S500 is a compact radio object at redshift 0.73 with an absorbed X-ray spectrum and q_24 value supporting a type 2 AGN classification.

quotes.quote: S500 This compact radio object has an absorbed X-ray spectrum with hardness ratio 0.6 ( Lehmer2005 ) , and it is classified as a type 2 AGN by the criteria. It has a spectral index of -0.9 ( Kellermann2008 ) and a redshift of 0.73 ( Mainieri2008 ). Based on q_ 24 =-0.21 , this is an AGN.

gmw_00012109.name: S503
gmw_00012109.overview: S503 is a very compact radio source with a redshift of z=0.81 [arXiv:1011.2400]. It is identified as a type 1 active galactic nucleus in X-rays, and its core is resolved into two components separated by approximately 10 milliarcseconds [arXiv:1011.2400].

sources.summary: S503 is the brightest VLBI source in the survey, very compact but resolved into two core components, and identified as a type 1 AGN in X-rays.

quotes.quote: S503 This is the brightest source in our VLBI observations, with a flux density of (21.2 3.0) mJy (compared to an ATCA flux density of 19.3 mJy). Hence this source is very compact, but our uniformly-weighted image indicates that its core is resolved into two components with a separation of around 10 mas (76 pc at the distance of S503; z=0.81 , Mainieri2008 ). It is an X-ray source indicating a type 1 AGN ( Lehmer2005 , Szokoly2004 ).

gmw_00012110.name: S520
gmw_00012110.overview: S520 is an active galactic nucleus at a redshift of 1.4 [arXiv:1011.2400]. The source is mildly resolved in radio observations, showing a possible 25 milliarcsecond extension and a very faint infrared counterpart [arXiv:1011.2400].

sources.summary: S520 is mildly resolved in VLBI, with most arcsecond-scale flux recovered, a possible 25 mas extension, redshift 1.4, faint infrared counterpart, and no ECDFS detection.

quotes.quote: S520 This source is mildly resolved. From its arcsec-scale flux density of 3.7 mJy (2.5 0.4) mJy could be recovered in our lowest-resolution image. There is a hint of a 25 mas extension towards the south-west in the naturally and uniformly-weighted image which at the redshift of the source of 1.4 ( Wolf2004 ) corresponds to around 200 pc. It has only a very faint IR counterpart in the co-located Spitzer image, and is not detected in the ECDFS survey by (it is outside the GOODS region).

gmw_00012111.name: J1057 4039
gmw_00012111.overview: J1057 4039 is a radio-loud active galactic nucleus classified as a narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy [arXiv:2202.11065]. It features a flat radio spectrum and a central core with an extended linear structure likely associated with a relativistic jet [arXiv:2202.11065].

sources.summary: J1057 4039 is a radio-loud NLS1 with extended structure and a core/linear feature likely tied to a relativistic jet, plus flat radio spectra.

quotes.quote: Two RL objects (see Section 3), J1057 4039 and J1500 7248, exhibit strong extended structures at both 5.5 GHz and 9.0 GHz.

The morphology of J1057 4039 clearly shows a central core, plus an extended linear structure to the south-east of the core at both frequencies, which is likely associated with a relativistic jet (see Section 4.1.1).

Flat spectrum Three of the 17 objects (J0436 3010, J1057 4039, and J1500 7248) have flat or inverted spectra at 1--9 GHz, of which two are RL with R_ L = 621 for J1057 4039 and R_ L = 112 for J1500 7248. Their spectra are flat with 0.51 and 0.20 in J1057 4039, and inverted with 0.24 in J1500 7248. Hence a core-dominated relativistic jet is likely to be present in these two sources, which is consistent with their radio morphology showing a central core plus extended structures (see Fig. maps ).

gmw_00012112.name: J1500 7248
gmw_00012112.overview: J1500 7248 is a radio-loud narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy [arXiv:2202.11065]. It exhibits a flat or inverted radio spectrum and a central core with extended, two-sided structures that likely represent a relativistic jet [arXiv:2202.11065].

sources.summary: J1500 7248 is a radio-loud NLS1 with extended two-sided structure and a flat or inverted spectrum, interpreted as a compact source with jet emission.

quotes.quote: Two RL objects (see Section 3), J1057 4039 and J1500 7248, exhibit strong extended structures at both 5.5 GHz and 9.0 GHz.

In J1500 7248, since the smoothed beam size of the 5.5 GHz image is larger than that of the 9.0 GHz image, the 9.0 GHz image generally shows the structure of the unresolved central core in the 5.5 GHz image. We see that this object also exhibits extended structures at both sides, which is possibly related to a two-sided jet (see Section 4.1.1).

Flat spectrum Three of the 17 objects (J0436 3010, J1057 4039, and J1500 7248) have flat or inverted spectra at 1--9 GHz, of which two are RL with R_ L = 621 for J1057 4039 and R_ L = 112 for J1500 7248. Their spectra are flat with 0.51 and 0.20 in J1057 4039, and inverted with 0.24 in J1500 7248. Hence a core-dominated relativistic jet is likely to be present in these two sources, which is consistent with their radio morphology showing a central core plus extended structures (see Fig. maps ).

gmw_00012113.name: J0436 3010
gmw_00012113.overview: J0436 3010 is a radio-quiet narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy [arXiv:2202.11065]. It exhibits a flat to inverted radio spectrum, which suggests that its radio emission is dominated by an optically thick compact core [arXiv:2202.11065].

sources.summary: J0436 3010 is a radio-quiet NLS1 whose flat and inverted radio spectrum is interpreted as optically thick compact-core emission becoming more dominant at higher frequency.

quotes.quote: Flat spectrum Three of the 17 objects (J0436 3010, J1057 4039, and J1500 7248) have flat or inverted spectra at 1--9 GHz, of which two are RL with R_ L = 621 for J1057 4039 and R_ L = 112 for J1500 7248.

In contrast, J0436 3010 is RQ with R_ L = 4 and its flat spectrum ( _ 3.0-5.5 0.18 ) suggests that the radio emission originates from an optically-thick compact core. The more compact emission at higher frequencies becomes more dominant with an inverted slope ( _ 5.5-9.0 0.13 ).

gmw_00012114.name: J1042 4000
gmw_00012114.overview: J1042 4000 is classified as a steep-spectrum narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy [arXiv:2202.11065]. It is a radio-loud source that lies close to the boundary between radio-loud and radio-quiet objects [arXiv:2202.11065].

sources.summary: J1042 4000 is placed among steep-spectrum NLS1s, is near the radio-loud boundary, and has a larger inferred radio-sphere than the radio-quiet sources.

quotes.quote: Steep spectrum Nine sources (J0040 3713, J0217 3004, J1042 4000, J1325 3824, J1329 2925, J1410 3644, J1515 7820, J2039 3018, and J2216 3947) show a trend of flatter spectral slopes with decreasing frequencies. One object, J1042 4000, is RL with R_ L = 19, although it is close to the RL and RQ border line ( R_ L = 10).
In the only one RL source (J1042 4000), R 3.7 pc, an order of magnitude larger.

gmw_00012115.name: J1515 7820
gmw_00012115.overview: J1515 7820 is classified as a steep-spectrum narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy [arXiv:2202.11065]. The active galactic nucleus is undetected at 1.4 GHz and 3.0 GHz but is detected at 843 MHz, suggesting a spectral turnover below 100 MHz [arXiv:2202.11065].

sources.summary: J1515 7820 is classed with steep-spectrum NLS1s, lacks 1.4 and 3.0 GHz detections but appears at 843 MHz, implying a turnover below 100 MHz.

quotes.quote: Steep spectrum Nine sources (J0040 3713, J0217 3004, J1042 4000, J1325 3824, J1329 2925, J1410 3644, J1515 7820, J2039 3018, and J2216 3947) show a trend of flatter spectral slopes with decreasing frequencies. One object, J1042 4000, is RL with R_ L = 19, although it is close to the RL and RQ border line ( R_ L = 10). J1515 7820 is not detected at 1.4 GHz and 3.0 GHz but detected at 843 MHz, and we assume its _ 3.0-5.5 = _ 1.4-3.0 = _ 0.8-5.5 .
The spectral flattening toward low frequencies (1.4--3.0 GHz or 3.0--5.5 GHz) suggests _ p 0.3--2.7 GHz at rest frame, excluding J1515 7820 where _ p < 100 MHz.

gmw_00012116.name: J1048 3902
gmw_00012116.overview: J1048 3902 is a galaxy characterized by uniform, steep radio spectra [arXiv:2202.11065]. This spectral profile suggests that its radio emission is dominated by star formation activity, with an implied star formation rate of approximately 4 solar masses per year [arXiv:2202.11065].

sources.summary: J1048 3902 has uniform steep radio spectra consistent with star-formation-dominated emission, an implied SFR near 4 solar masses per year, and no 1.4 GHz detection.

quotes.quote: Star formation (SF) The remaining two sources (J1048 3902 and J1512 3333) are characterized by uniform steep spectra with 0.8 0.4 , without significant curvature, which may indicate that the radio emission is dominated by SF activity. The small spectral curvature in J1512 3333 suggests _ p = 0.2 GHz at rest frame, but _ p can not be estimated in J1048 3902 due to the lack of an appropriate spectral curvature. If the radio emission originates from SF only, which is a combination of thermal and non-thermal components Murphy2011 , the star formation rate (SFR) can be estimated based on 5 GHz luminosity via Tabatabaei2017 SFR M_ yr^ 1 = 4.1 10^ 38 L_ erg s^ 1 assuming a thermal electron temperature T_ e = 10^4 K and a spectral index of the non-thermal emission = -1 . The implied SFR for J1048 3902 and J1512 3333 are about 4 M _ yr ^ 1 and 8 M _ yr ^ 1 respectively.

In the plot of _ 3.0-5.5 versus _ 5.5-9.0 (Fig. slope left panel), three objects (J1048 3902, J1325 3824, and J1512 3333) lie close to the equal slope relation within the range of = -0.8 0.4 populated by SF galaxies, and two more objects reside within 1 from this area. However in the plot of _ 3.0-5.5 versus _ 1.4-3.0 (Fig. slope right panel), only one object (J1512 3333) stays in the area populated by SF galaxies (J1048 3902 is not detected at 1.4 GHz, and J1325 3824 is detected but is outside the SF area.), and three more objects reside within 1 from this area.

gmw_00012117.name: J1512 3333
gmw_00012117.overview: J1512 3333 is an active galaxy characterized by uniform steep radio spectra [arXiv:2202.11065]. Its radio emission is likely dominated by star formation activity [arXiv:2202.11065], with an estimated star formation rate of approximately 8 solar masses per year [arXiv:2202.11065].

sources.summary: J1512 3333 has star-formation-like steep spectra, an SFR near 8 solar masses per year, and the lowest turnover frequency, magnetic field, and longest variability scale in the subset.

quotes.quote: Star formation (SF) The remaining two sources (J1048 3902 and J1512 3333) are characterized by uniform steep spectra with 0.8 0.4 , without significant curvature, which may indicate that the radio emission is dominated by SF activity. The small spectral curvature in J1512 3333 suggests _ p = 0.2 GHz at rest frame, but _ p can not be estimated in J1048 3902 due to the lack of an appropriate spectral curvature. If the radio emission originates from SF only, which is a combination of thermal and non-thermal components Murphy2011 , the star formation rate (SFR) can be estimated based on 5 GHz luminosity via Tabatabaei2017 SFR M_ yr^ 1 = 4.1 10^ 38 L_ erg s^ 1 assuming a thermal electron temperature T_ e = 10^4 K and a spectral index of the non-thermal emission = -1 . The implied SFR for J1048 3902 and J1512 3333 are about 4 M _ yr ^ 1 and 8 M _ yr ^ 1 respectively.

In the plot of _ 3.0-5.5 versus _ 5.5-9.0 (Fig. slope left panel), three objects (J1048 3902, J1325 3824, and J1512 3333) lie close to the equal slope relation within the range of = -0.8 0.4 populated by SF galaxies, and two more objects reside within 1 from this area. However in the plot of _ 3.0-5.5 versus _ 1.4-3.0 (Fig. slope right panel), only one object (J1512 3333) stays in the area populated by SF galaxies (J1048 3902 is not detected at 1.4 GHz, and J1325 3824 is detected but is outside the SF area.), and three more objects reside within 1 from this area.

In contrast, J1512 3333 which may be dominated by SF activity based on its spectral shape, has the lowest turnover frequency (0.2 GHz), the lowest magnetic field (0.02 Gauss), and the longest variability time scale (several decades).

gmw_00012118.name: J0135 3539
gmw_00012118.overview: J0135 3539 is an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2202.11065]. The galaxy exhibits spectral steepening at high frequencies, which is interpreted as relic emission from an acceleration event that occurred approximately 1.6 years ago [arXiv:2202.11065].

sources.summary: J0135 3539 shows spectral steepening to an ultra-steep high-frequency drop-off interpreted as relic emission, with 5 GHz electrons from an event about 1.6 years earlier.

quotes.quote: Relic emission In two other objects we see an opposite effect, the same steepening at low frequencies, but exceptional steepening to < -2 at high frequencies, rather than flattening. Specifically, in J0135 3539, _ 1.4-3.0 0.42 steepens to _ 3.0-5.5 0.95 ( _ p = 1.3 GHz), and becomes very steep with _ 5.5-9.0 2.23 . In J2059 3147, _ 0.8-1.4 0.49 steepens to _ 1.4-3.0 0.85 ( _ p = 0.5 GHz), and becomes very steep already at _ 3.0-5.5 2.52 and remains steep at _ 5.5-9.0 2.14 . These ultra steep drop-offs may indicate the presence of relic emission, i.e. \ significant cooling since the time of a previous AGN activity which accelerated the electrons Kharb2006,Kharb2016,Congiu2017,Congiu2020,Silpa2020 .

This may explain the preferential steepening to < -2 , or the so-called relic emission, observed at higher frequencies in J0135 3539 and J2059 3147. If this is the case, the radio emitting electrons at 5 GHz in J0135 3539 were produced in an acceleration event 1.6 years ago.

gmw_00012119.name: J2059 3147
gmw_00012119.overview: J2059 3147 is an active galactic nucleus that exhibits very steep high-frequency radio slopes [arXiv:2202.11065]. This steepening is interpreted as relic emission from an acceleration event that occurred approximately 22.2 years ago [arXiv:2202.11065].

sources.summary: J2059 3147 shows very steep high-frequency radio slopes interpreted as relic emission, with synchrotron timing suggesting an acceleration event roughly two decades earlier.

quotes.quote: Relic emission In two other objects we see an opposite effect, the same steepening at low frequencies, but exceptional steepening to < -2 at high frequencies, rather than flattening. Specifically, in J0135 3539, _ 1.4-3.0 0.42 steepens to _ 3.0-5.5 0.95 ( _ p = 1.3 GHz), and becomes very steep with _ 5.5-9.0 2.23 . In J2059 3147, _ 0.8-1.4 0.49 steepens to _ 1.4-3.0 0.85 ( _ p = 0.5 GHz), and becomes very steep already at _ 3.0-5.5 2.52 and remains steep at _ 5.5-9.0 2.14 . These ultra steep drop-offs may indicate the presence of relic emission, i.e. \ significant cooling since the time of a previous AGN activity which accelerated the electrons Kharb2006,Kharb2016,Congiu2017,Congiu2020,Silpa2020 .

This may explain the preferential steepening to < -2 , or the so-called relic emission, observed at higher frequencies in J0135 3539 and J2059 3147. If this is the case, the radio emitting electrons at 5 GHz in J0135 3539 were produced in an acceleration event 1.6 years ago. In J2059 3147, t_ syn 15.8 years at 5 GHz, but since the steepening in this object already starts at 3 GHz, where is somewhat smaller, the acceleration event might occur 22.2 years ago.

gmw_00012120.name: J0316+4328
gmw_00012120.overview: J0316+4328 is a high-confidence asymmetric gravitational lens system [arXiv:0707.2679]. The system features two bright lensed images separated by 0.40 arcseconds, which exhibit a flux density ratio of 19 [arXiv:0707.2679].

sources.summary: The paper reports J0316+4328 as a high-confidence asymmetric gravitational lens whose two bright quasar images have an unusually large flux-density ratio and can constrain the lens-galaxy mass profile.

quotes.quote: We report a probable gravitational lens J0316+4328, one of 19 candidate asymmetric double lenses (2 images at a high flux density ratio) from CLASS. Observations with the Very Large Array (VLA), MERLIN and the Very Long Baseline Array (VLBA) imply that J0316+4328 is a lens with high confidence. It has 2 images separated by 0 40, with 6 GHz flux densities of 62 mJy and 3.2 mJy. The flux density ratio of 19 (constant over the frequency range 6-22 GHz) is the largest for any 2 image gravitational lens. High resolution optical imaging and deeper VLBI maps should confirm the lensing interpretation and provide inputs to detailed lens models.

Compared to quad or symmetric double lenses, J0316+4328 is more likely to show a central image in sensitive VLBI maps, and even an upper limit on the central image flux density will be a more stringent model constraint. We have detected a probable gravitational lens with 2 bright images at a considerably higher flux density ratio than any previously known double lens.

gmw_00012121.name: UGC 7131
gmw_00012121.overview: UGC 7131 is a blue compact dwarf galaxy with a slight comet-like structure [arXiv:astro-ph/9811096]. Deep CCD imaging shows the galaxy to be unresolved and amorphous, supporting a revised distance estimate that places it in the Coma I group [arXiv:astro-ph/9811096].

sources.summary: The survey highlights UGC 7131 as a distance outlier: revised stellar-population distances place it beyond its observed velocity, bringing its H I mass into line and favoring Coma I membership.

quotes.quote: This includes UGC 7131, which was found to lie at a distance d>14  Mpc, considerably further than indicated from the observed velocity listed in Table 5 ( V^ hel = 251  ) or for flow motions corrected velocity ( V^ POT = 487  ). However, with this new independent distance estimate UGC 7131 does fall within the mass range expected for its linewidth. Its morphology as evident on the sky survey plates does not indicate a morphology earlier than the galaxy type listed in Table 2a of Sdm for which one could expect a higher mass in agreement with its new determination ( shift in Fig.  vwid_MHI.fig ). It has a slight comet-like structure not atypical for BCD galaxies.

On the other hand, the deep CDD-image in Markarova (1998, their Fig. 3) finds UGC 7131 to be unresolved and amorph, which does confirm the larger distance and is not consistent with a nearby (low--velocity) galaxy.

With the exception of the observed velocity, all further indications about UGC 7131 support the considerably larger distance --- even its distribution in redshift space. The locations of the \ selected galaxies are shown in a cone diagram in Fig.  cone.fig with the radial coordinate of Heliocentric velocity and where the POTENT distances are drawn as contours. An arrow indicates the revision with regard to the location of UGC 7131. It is clear from this display that UGC 7131 is not a member of the nearby CVn I group, nor of the more distant CVn II group, but most likely is a member of the Coma I group.

gmw_00012122.name: NGC 6574
gmw_00012122.overview: NGC 6574 is a Seyfert 2 active galaxy that hosts a stellar bar [arXiv:0712.3133]. Its molecular gas is distributed across its nucleus, bar, spiral arms, and an extended disk, with bar-driven inflows transporting gas toward the center [arXiv:0712.3133].

sources.summary: The paper maps CO emission in the Seyfert 2 galaxy NGC 6574, finding molecular gas in its nucleus, bar, spiral arms, disk, and bar-driven inflow toward the center.

quotes.quote: We present interferometric observations of ^ 12 CO(1-0) and ^ 12 CO(2-1) line emission from the Seyfert 2 galaxy NGC 6574, obtained with the IRAM Plateau de Bure Interferometer (PdBI). These data have been combined with 30m mapping data in these lines to correct for the flux resolved by the interferometer. At an angular resolution of 0.7 '' ( 110pc), the ^ 12 CO(2-1) emission is resolved into an inner disk with a radius of 300 pc. The molecular gas in NGC 6574 is primarily distributed in four components: nucleus, bar, spiral arms - winding up into a pseudo-ring - and an extended underlying disk component.

We computed the gravity torques exerted from the stellar bar on the gas, deriving the gravitational potential from near-infrared images, and weighting the torques by the CO distribution. We find negative torques for the gas inside the ring, since the gas aligned with the bar has a slight advance phase shift, leading the bar. This means that gas is flowing in towards the center, at least down to 400pc in radius, which can explain the observed high nuclear gas concentration.

gmw_00012123.name: CGCG 453-062
gmw_00012123.overview: CGCG 453-062 is a moderately inclined galaxy with an inclination angle of approximately 54 degrees [arXiv:2402.17830]. It hosts galactic outflows, with its median H-beta outflow velocity increasing from 125 km/s to a deprojected velocity of 213 km/s after correcting for inclination [arXiv:2402.17830].

sources.summary: CGCG 453-062 is the one moderately inclined DUVET target; the paper highlights it separately and deprojects its outflow velocities, increasing the median H velocity from 125 to 213 km s^-1.

quotes.quote: Nine of these targets have inclinations of i<15^ , and one target is moderately inclined (CGCG 453-062: i=54^ ). The inclined target was observed due to a range in LST at the time of observation that did not have sufficient targets available that meet the DUVET sample selection criterion. We will highlight CGCG 453-062 in our discussion of results and in the main results plots.

CGCG 453-062's high inclination angle ( i 54^ ) required that the velocities be deprojected by dividing by i . The originally observed velocities are shown in the CGCG 453-062 panels of Fig. fig:sigsfr_vout_individual_hbeta and Fig. fig:sigsfr_vout_individual_OIII as transparent orange circles. The deprojected velocities are shown as blue points. The median H outflow velocity before correction is 125 km s ^ 1 , and the inclination correction increases this to 213 km s ^ 1 .

gmw_00012124.name: UGC 12150
gmw_00012124.overview: UGC 12150 is a star-forming galaxy that exhibits a very weak signal for outflowing ionized gas [arXiv:2402.17830]. This outflow component is detected in only a single spaxel of the hydrogen-alpha emission line [arXiv:2402.17830].

sources.summary: UGC 12150 is identified as an outlier with weak ionized-gas outflow evidence; the KCWI field misses part of it, so its SFR is a lower limit, and only one H spaxel shows outflow.

quotes.quote: The outlier is UGC 12150, which we find has a very weak signal for outflowing ionised gas, as can be seen in the individual outflow scaling relations in Appendix appendix:ind_gals .

We note that for six of the galaxies in our sample (CGCG 453-062, UGC 12150, NGC 0695, NGC 7316, IRAS 20351+2521 and KISSR 1084) the galaxy extends beyond the FOV of our KCWI pointing. The SFR that we measure is therefore a lower limit for the total SFR of these galaxies.

Three of our galaxies (UGC 12150, NGC 7316 and IRAS 15229+0511 ) do not have the signal-to-noise necessary to fit an outflow component in [ OIII ] 5007 . In fact, we find only one spaxel in UGC 12150 where H is fit with an outflow component.

gmw_00012125.name: J033239.72-275154.7
gmw_00012125.overview: J033239.72-275154.7 is a galaxy at redshift z=0.41 that features a large, young 6 kpc bar [arXiv:0812.1593]. It is modeled as a 1:3 retrograde merger remnant that will likely evolve into an S0 galaxy [arXiv:0812.1593].

sources.summary: The paper models J033239.72-275154.7 as a z=0.41 barred merger remnant whose morphology and kinematics are reproduced by a 1:3 retrograde encounter and may evolve into an S0 galaxy.

quotes.quote: We present a physical model of the formation of J033239.72-275154.7, a galaxy observed at z=0.41 and characterized by a big young bar of size 6 kpc. The study of this system is particularly interesting for understanding the connection between mergers and bars as well as the properties and fate of this system as it relates to disk galaxy formation. We compare the morphological and kinematic properties of J033239.72-275154.7, the latter obtained by the GIRAFFE spectrograph, to those derived from the merger of two spiral galaxies described by idealized N-body simulations including a star formation prescription. We found that the general morphological shape and most of the dynamical properties of the object can be well reproduced by a model in which the satellite is initially put in a retrograde orbit and the mass ratio of the system is 1:3.
In its later evolution, however, we find that J033239.72-275154.7, whose major progenitor was an Sab galaxy, will probably become a S0 galaxy.

gmw_00012126.name: Z11598 0112
gmw_00012126.overview: Z11598 0112 is an ultraluminous infrared galaxy hosting a radio-loud narrow-line Seyfert 1 nucleus [arXiv:2105.13460]. The galaxy hosts a warm molecular outflow detected via hydroxyl absorption, and its carbon monoxide emission lines exhibit high-velocity wings extending to approximately 300 km/s [arXiv:2105.13460].

sources.summary: Z11598 0112 is a ULIRG with a radio-loud NLS1 nucleus and warm molecular outflow; the CO wings extend to about 300 km/s but fall short of outflow criteria.

quotes.quote: Z11598 0112 is a ULIRG kim98 with a radio-loud narrow-line Seyfert 1 (NLS1) nucleus condon98b,moran96 . Optical spectra show no Na  i D absorption that would be indicative of a neutral outflow rupke05 , but weak absorption is detected in more sensitive data along with a miniBAL in the UV martin15 . A warm molecular outflow is detected based on OH  119  m absorption with a blueshift of 153 50   \ veilleux13b . Single-dish CO observations with a _b=24 beam show potential for high-velocity wings to the \ line profile xia12 .

The single-dish \ showed the potential for high-velocity line wings. Here, these seem to extend out to approximately 300  (or slightly farther on the blue side, but no spatial extension is associated with this emission). Binning in velocity does highlight the wings, but does not boost any signal above the noise at velocities higher than |300|  . The position-velocity diagram in Figure  fig:pv suggests that this emission is not rotation, as it is not reflected on the blue side of the line.

gmw_00012127.name: RXCJ 0232.2-4420 A
gmw_00012127.overview: RXCJ 0232.2-4420 A is a brightest cluster galaxy that forms a pair with a neighboring galaxy separated by 100 kpc [arXiv:0804.0299]. The diffuse stellar emission surrounding the galaxy extends up to 100 kpc and exhibits a southwest extension that resembles the distribution of the cluster's X-ray emitting gas [arXiv:0804.0299].

sources.summary: The paper describes RXCJ 0232.2-4420 A as one BCG in a 100 kpc pair whose diffuse light follows the cluster X-ray gas and extends westward from A.

quotes.quote: Diffuse stellar emission is robustly detected around all the seven BCGs in the three X-ray selected clusters, and its distribution and B-R colour down to 26 R- (observed frame) are investigated. The diffuse light around BCGs extends up to 100 kpc from their centres. At this distance, we expect that on average 30--40 of the detected emission (including galaxies) is due to the intracluster light (ICL), as from the statistical results obtained from 683 optically selected clusters of galaxies at 0.2 z 0.3 (Zibetti et al. 2005).

The distribution of the diffuse light around the pair of BCGs in RXCJ 0232.2 4420 resembles that of the envelopping X-ray emitting gas. The centres of these two BCGs are aligned in the EW direction and separated by 100 kpc ; the major axes of RXCJ 0232.2 4420 A and B have position angles (measured counter-clockwise from north to east) of about 45 and 100 , respectively (see Fig. FigBR2 ). The cocoon of diffuse stellar emission within the 26 R- isophote exhibits a SW extention which reaches out to 80 kpc towards the west at a galactocentric distance of 90 kpc along the direction of the major axis of RXCJ 0232.2 4420 A. This behaviour is analogous to that of the X-ray emission of the cluster, whose projected distribution is centred on RXCJ 0232.2 4420 A but deviates from centrally symmetry owing to a broad SW extention (see Fig. FigXrayOpt2 ).

gmw_00012128.name: RXCJ 0232.2-4420 B
gmw_00012128.overview: RXCJ 0232.2-4420 B is a brightest cluster galaxy that forms a pair with another brightest cluster galaxy at a separation of 100 kpc [arXiv:0804.0299]. The pair is surrounded by a cocoon of diffuse stellar emission that resembles the distribution of the enveloping X-ray emitting gas [arXiv:0804.0299].

sources.summary: The paper describes RXCJ 0232.2-4420 B as one BCG in a 100 kpc pair embedded in diffuse light resembling the enveloping X-ray emitting gas.

quotes.quote: Diffuse stellar emission is robustly detected around all the seven BCGs in the three X-ray selected clusters, and its distribution and B-R colour down to 26 R- (observed frame) are investigated. The diffuse light around BCGs extends up to 100 kpc from their centres. At this distance, we expect that on average 30--40 of the detected emission (including galaxies) is due to the intracluster light (ICL), as from the statistical results obtained from 683 optically selected clusters of galaxies at 0.2 z 0.3 (Zibetti et al. 2005).

The distribution of the diffuse light around the pair of BCGs in RXCJ 0232.2 4420 resembles that of the envelopping X-ray emitting gas. The centres of these two BCGs are aligned in the EW direction and separated by 100 kpc ; the major axes of RXCJ 0232.2 4420 A and B have position angles (measured counter-clockwise from north to east) of about 45 and 100 , respectively (see Fig. FigBR2 ). The cocoon of diffuse stellar emission within the 26 R- isophote exhibits a SW extention which reaches out to 80 kpc towards the west at a galactocentric distance of 90 kpc along the direction of the major axis of RXCJ 0232.2 4420 A.
